### nonchalant — 2015-05-03T22:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163214>

Thomas, I would say the first step is to realize that humans, after all, are a part of nature. It is when we try to control our environment that we go wrong.

When we insulate ourselves from the natural environment, we begin to develop disease. Also when we change our environment, by adding energy such as sound, radio frequencies, artificial light, heat, etc, or substances like fluoride to our water or food, we also begin to develop disease.

If I had to do it all over again, I would first work on re-establishing my circadian rhythm. The biggest environmental clue our bodies receive is the basic light/dark signal. And I would get Jack's book. No where does he write things more succinctly.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-04T05:16:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163227>

I assume that you're talking about how to learn the knowledge displayed on the site. This is distinct from living an optimal lifestyle, and members like @[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) and @[prAna303](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1363/) have displayed an intuitive methodology for living the optimal life without needing to know all the details.

So first off, you cannot assimilate new knowledge without creating it yourself. When you read something, you're building a mental model of the knowledge in your head. However, reading is just one way of creating these mental models, and you should experiment with how to go about learning best.

\----
**Personal Perspective**

People on this site know that I've long been complaining that there's not enough information being given yet . As I wrote in my journal, I am a Programmer used to having source code thrust in my face. All the workings of a program are in the source code. It may be garbled up and hard to read, but I can trust that everything that a program does is described in those lines of text.

How the Universe works, literally at all scales, is by becoming aware of as many possibilities as possible. In quantum mechanics, this is described as an object being in many possible superposition states. Then, using that information, the object makes the most probably decision

Dr Kruse has not yet publicly written everything that he knows; this stuff isn't easy to write . eg: He's been hinting at the Nitrogen piece for awhile now, but hasn't yet talked in detail about the mechanics. ie: I do not have full access to all superposition states, and thus will have to attempt to create my own models of understanding as I go along.

I have described how I like to think in this post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/#post-157499](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/long-term-sulfasalazine-effects.13155/#post-157499)

So what I am saying is that my ideal starting place would have been with all the information that Dr Kruse knows laid out in front of me (which is yet to happen), and then I will dive head-first into the full material and figure things out one logical step at a time.

\----
**Rules of Thumb
**
There are some people who can process many separate threads of thought in their head at once. I have met programmers who can "think in the shape of their code", which leads me to believe that they have a certain intuition for that mode of thinking. This makes it very easy for them to manipulate their code, and to see patterns and connections between different parts of their code. Communicating with such people has been a real pain in my experience

I can't do that, and I have to step through my thoughts one logical step after another, one train-of-thought at a time. To ensure that I am constantly building on the correct train-of-thought, I need easy-to-use rules of thumb to ensure that if I'm going in the wrong direction, I notice it as early as possible.

Personally, I always use 2 rules-of-thumb:

(1) **Is this problem caused by a lack of energy, or a lack of information?**
(2) **Nature is obsessed with efficiency, and Efficiency requires tight coupling. (between EM and matter like water or Nitrogen)**

Given that we do not have full information about how everything functions, we need to instead focus on how things break.

In a complex system, components are coupled and thus depend heavily on each other. When things break in such a system, they will break in completely non-deterministic ways (random). See this post by Frederic Herbert for how software engineers deal with failure -- [http://ferd.ca/lessons-learned-while-working-on-large-scale-server-software.html](http://ferd.ca/lessons-learned-while-working-on-large-scale-server-software.html)

In man-made systems, which can be fully understood (since they are created by humans), you can eventually figure out why things happened, and fix the symptoms of a problem.

In a natural system to which we cannot fully understand, the right approach is not to focus on fixing the system, but in figuring out why things break, and then trusting nature to be able to self-repair once the offending stressor is removed.

Back to my two rules-of-thumb, "breakage" always takes the form of either a component of the body not being able to provide enough energy (inefficiency), or because parts of the body cannot communicate with each other.

NOTE: information doesn't require significant energy to be transferred. The best example are quantum-entangled objects -- when one object has a change in state, the other object can respond seemingly without any energy transfer. Of course, there has to be some sort of signal, but that signal is probably carried by neutrinos, and is so weak that it is insignificant for doing any sort of useful work.

Also, a lack of energy in a place where energy is expected, also constitutes a form of information.​

When you are Leptin Resistant and obese, the problem is with Information Signalling, not that you don't have stored energy. The Leptin receptors are not able to respond to stimulus (there can be many reasons), and cannot regulate energy use. This uncoupling of signalling leads to energetic inefficiency.

Same story with Insulin signalling to the brain -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ubiquination-2-is-now-live.13031/page-2#post-156679](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ubiquination-2-is-now-live.13031/page-2#post-156679)

Lack of signalling is usually the cause for breakage. Though there are some cases where a lack of energy is the main concern. When you feel like you "need carbs to exercise", it is a function of "not being enough of a fat burner" to provide for energy fast enough. (Note: there is a signalling issue here too, whereby carb intake slows down circadian clocks)

\----

Back to my original point about building mental models ... I try to navigate the information presented to me using those simple rules-of-thumb. When a particular rule-of-thumb fails to accurate model reality, then I have new information, which hopefully can be used to create a new rule-of-thumb that is more accurate.

....

### Penny — 2015-05-04T07:04:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163228>

**Blue Light-Dependent Interaction of CRY2 with SPA1 Regulates COP1 activity and Floral Initiation in Arabidopsis**
[Zecheng Zuo](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Zuo%20Z%5Bauth%5D),1,3,4 [Hongtao Liu](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Liu%20H%5Bauth%5D),3,4 [Bin Liu](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Liu%20B%5Bauth%5D),2,3,4 [Xuanming Liu](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Liu%20X%5Bauth%5D),1,\* and [Chentao Lin](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20C%5Bauth%5D)3,\*
[Author information ►](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#) [Copyright and License information ►](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#)

The publisher's final edited version of this article is available at [Curr Biol](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/elink.fcgi?dbfrom=pubmed&retmode=ref&cmd=prlinks&id=21514160)
See other articles in PMC that [cite](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/citedby/) the published article.

[Go to:](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#)
**Summary**
Cryptochromes are blue light receptors that mediate light regulation of gene expression in all major evolution lineages, but the molecular mechanism underlying cryptochrome signal transduction remains not fully understood \[[1](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R1), [2](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R2)\]. It has been reported that cryptochromes suppress activity of the multifunctional E3 ubiquitin ligase _CONSTITUTIVE PHOTOMORPHOGENIC 1_ (COP1) to regulate gene expression in response to blue light \[[3](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R3), [4](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R4)\]. But how plant cryptochromes mediate light suppression of COP1 activity remains unclear. We report here that _Arabidopsis_ CRY2 (cryptochrome 2) undergoes blue light-dependent interaction with the COP1-interacting protein _SUPPRESSOR OF PHYTOCHROME A 1_ (SPA1) \[[5](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R5), [6](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R6)\]. We demonstrate that SPA1 acts genetically downstream from CRY2 to mediate blue light suppression of the COP1-dependent proteolysis of the flowering-time regulator _CONSTANS_ (CO) \[[7](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R7), [8](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/#R8)\]. We further show that blue light-dependent CRY2-SPA1 interaction stimulates CRY2-COP1 interaction. These results reveal for the first time a wavelength-specific mechanism by which a cryptochrome photoreceptor mediates light regulation of protein degradation to modulate developmental timing in _Arabidopsis_

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3150455/)

### Penny — 2015-05-04T07:04:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163229>

**Effcts of Blue Light and Ammonia on Nitrogen Metabolism in a Colorless Mutant of _Chlorella_**

1.  [Akio Kamiya](http://pcp.oxfordjournals.org/search?author1=Akio+Kamiya&sortspec=date&submit=Submit)

1.  Laboratory of Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Teikyo UniversitySagamiko, Kanagawa, 199-01 Japan

-   Received November 25, 1988.
-   Accepted March 6, 1989.

**Abstract**
Illumination of a colorless mutant of _Chlorella vulgaris_ 1lh (M125) with blue light enhanced both the uptake of nitrate and the release of ammonia. These effects were not observed under illumination with red light. The release of ammonia was also enhanced by the addition of methionine sulphoximine (MSX), an inhibitor of glutamine synthetase (GS). Addition of MSX to cultures in the dark increased the rate of breakdown of starch.

Algal cells grown in nitrate-containing medium did not show the aminating activity of glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH). Addition of large (millimolar) amounts of ammonia in the dark resulted in the induction of NADPH-GDH activity and, in addition, a decrease in GS activity. From these results it appears that GS catalyzes the primary step in the assimilation of ammonia in algal cells grown in nitrate-containing medium. Two isoforms (GS1 and GS2) of GS have been separated by ion exchange chromatography.. The activities of both isoforms were decreased upon the addition of ammonia.

Illumination of the alga with blue light at intensities up to 10,000 mW m−2enhanced the measurable activity of GS in vitro, while higher intensities were ineffective. In red light no such effect was observed.

The effects of blue light and ammonia on nitrogen metabolism in algal cells are discussed.

### Penny — 2015-05-04T07:36:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163231>

**Boswellia sacra essential oil induces tumor cell-specific apoptosis and suppresses tumor aggressiveness in cultured human breast cancer cells.**
[Suhail MM](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Suhail%20MM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782)1, [Wu W](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Wu%20W%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Cao A](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Cao%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Mondalek FG](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Mondalek%20FG%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Fung KM](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Fung%20KM%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Shih PT](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Shih%20PT%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Fang YT](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Fang%20YT%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Woolley C](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Woolley%20C%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Young G](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Young%20G%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782), [Lin HK](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Lin%20HK%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=22171782).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22171782#)**

**Abstract**
**BACKGROUND:**
Gum resins obtained from trees of the Burseraceae family (Boswellia sp.) are important ingredients in incense and perfumes. Extracts prepared from Boswellia sp. gum resins have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and anti-neoplastic effects. Essential oil prepared by distillation of the gum resin traditionally used for aromatic therapy has also been shown to have tumor cell-specific anti-proliferative and pro-apoptotic activities. The objective of this study was to optimize conditions for preparing Boswellea sacra essential oil with the highest biological activity in inducing tumor cell-specific cytotoxicity and suppressing aggressive tumor phenotypes in human breast cancer cells.

**METHODS:**
Boswellia sacra essential oil was prepared from Omani Hougari grade resins through hydrodistillation at 78 or 100 °C for 12 hours. Chemical compositions were identified by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry; and total boswellic acids contents were quantified by high-performance liquid chromatography. Boswellia sacra essential oil-mediated cell viability and death were studied in established human breast cancer cell lines (T47D, MCF7, MDA-MB-231) and an immortalized normal human breast cell line (MCF10-2A). Apoptosis was assayed by genomic DNA fragmentation. Anti-invasive and anti-multicellular tumor properties were evaluated by cellular network and spheroid formation models, respectively. Western blot analysis was performed to study Boswellia sacra essential oil-regulated proteins involved in apoptosis, signaling pathways, and cell cycle regulation.

**RESULTS:**
More abundant high molecular weight compounds, including boswellic acids, were present in Boswellia sacra essential oil prepared at 100 °C hydrodistillation. All three human breast cancer cell lines were sensitive to essential oil treatment with reduced cell viability and elevated cell death, whereas the immortalized normal human breast cell line was more resistant to essential oil treatment. Boswellia sacra essential oil hydrodistilled at 100 °C was more potent than the essential oil prepared at 78 °C in inducing cancer cell death, preventing the cellular network formation (MDA-MB-231) cells on Matrigel, causing the breakdown of multicellular tumor spheroids (T47D cells), and regulating molecules involved in apoptosis, signal transduction, and cell cycle progression.

**CONCLUSIONS:**
Similar to our previous observations in human bladder cancer cells, Boswellia sacra essential oil induces breast cancer cell-specific cytotoxicity. Suppression of cellular network formation and disruption of spheroid development of breast cancer cells by Boswellia sacra essential oil suggest that the essential oil may be effective for advanced breast cancer. Consistently, the essential oil represses signaling pathways and cell cycle regulators that have been proposed as therapeutic targets for breast cancer. Future pre-clinical and clinical studies are urgently needed to evaluate the safety and efficacy of Boswellia sacra essential oil as a therapeutic agent for treating breast cancer.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22171782](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22171782)

and it works because of this?

**Phosphorylation meets ubiquitination: the control of NF-\[kappa\]B activity.**
[Karin M](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Karin%20M%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=10837071)1, [Ben-Neriah Y](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Ben-Neriah%20Y%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=10837071).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10837071#)**

**Abstract**
NF-kappaB (nuclear factor-kappaB) is a collective name for inducible dimeric transcription factors composed of members of the Rel family of DNA-binding proteins that recognize a common sequence motif. NF-kappaB is found in essentially all cell types and is involved in activation of an exceptionally large number of genes in response to infections, inflammation, and other stressful situations requiring rapid reprogramming of gene expression. NF-kappaB is normally sequestered in the cytoplasm of nonstimulated cells and consequently must be translocated into the nucleus to function. The subcellular location of NF-kappaB is controlled by a family of inhibitory proteins, IkappaBs, which bind NF-kappaB and mask its nuclear localization signal, thereby preventing nuclear uptake. Exposure of cells to a variety of extracellular stimuli leads to the rapid phosphorylation, ubiquitination, and ultimately proteolytic degradation of IkappaB, which frees NF-kappaB to translocate to the nucleus where it regulates gene transcription. NF-kappaB activation represents a paradigm for controlling the function of a regulatory protein via ubiquitination-dependent proteolysis, as an integral part of a phosphorylationbased signaling cascade. Recently, considerable progress has been made in understanding the details of the signaling pathways that regulate NF-kappaB activity, particularly those responding to the proinflammatory cytokines tumor necrosis factor-alpha and interleukin-1. The multisubunit IkappaB kinase (IKK) responsible for inducible IkappaB phosphorylation is the point of convergence for most NF-kappaB-activating stimuli. IKK contains two catalytic subunits, IKKalpha and IKKbeta, both of which are able to correctly phosphorylate IkappaB. Gene knockout studies have shed light on the very different physiological functions of IKKalpha and IKKbeta. After phosphorylation, the IKK phosphoacceptor sites on IkappaB serve as an essential part of a specific recognition site for E3RS(IkappaB/beta-TrCP), an SCF-type E3 ubiquitin ligase, thereby explaining how IKK controls IkappaB ubiquitination and degradation. A variety of other signaling events, including phosphorylation of NF-kappaB, hyperphosphorylation of IKK, induction of IkappaB synthesis, and the processing of NF-kappaB precursors, provide additional mechanisms that modulate the level and duration of NF-kappaB activity.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10837071](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10837071)

### Penny — 2015-05-04T07:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advanced-members-if-you-had-to-do-it-again.14287/post-163232>

Durk Pearson & Sandy Shaw’s®
Life Extension NewsTM
Volume 15 No. 8 • December 2012

Improved Cognition in Aged Animals

Inhibition of NFkappaB, Master Regulator of Pro-inflammatory Signaling Pathway, Delays DNA Damage-induced Cell Senescence in Mouse Model of Accelerated Aging

The evidence continues to accumulate that chronic inflammation is a major underlying factor in many age-related diseases, such as atherosclerosis, arthritis, cancer, diabetes, osteoporosis, dementia, vascular diseases, obesity, and metabolic syndrome disorders (eg., insulin resistance) and possibly in aging itself.1–4 As a result, the investigation of anti-inflammatory effects of foods, nutrients, and food constituents (phytochemicals) has intensified. This is great news for those of us who would like to live as long as possible in as good condition as possible and also provides an important alternative to the FDA-controlled pathway to medical therapies and to the part of the American medical system that has become seriously dysfunctional as a result of extensive political micromanagement fostered by vested interests.

A new study1 reports on the effects of NFkappaB inhibition in a mouse model of accelerated aging, the XFE progeroid syndrome, caused by a defect in DNA repair. As DNA repair is a major factor in aging and is known to decline with age, this is a good model to study for anti-aging treatments. NFkappaB is a transcription factor, controlling the expression of a large number of genes, that is activated by cellular damage, stress, and inflammation. The authors’ basic premise for the study was that the cellular response to damage may be the key driver to aging.1

“NFkappaB was identified as the transcription factor most associated with mammalian aging, based on patterns of gene expression.” “Furthermore, chronic activation of NFkappaB is observed in numerous age-related diseases, including muscle atrophy, multiple sclerosis, atherosclerosis, heart disease, both type 1 and 2 diabetes, osteoarthritis, dementia, osteoporosis, and cancer. However these studies do not demonstrate a causal relationship between NFkappaB activation and aging.” (several citations were provided here)

As an example of some evidence in support of NFkappaB being causal in aging processes, the authors mention a study in which the depletion of NFkappaB in the skin of transgenic mice reversed age-related gene expression and histologic changes.

The researchers studied the XFE progeroid mice because the pattern of aging in these mice shows progressive degenerative changes that correlate strongly with natural aging. The overall results of the study showed that genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of NFkappaB activation delayed the onset of numerous aging-related symptoms and pathologies. “Inhibition of IKK/NF-kappaB activity reduced cellular senescence and oxidative damage, including DNA and protein damage, revealing that cellular stress responses promote further cellular damage. Our findings strongly suggest that inhibitors of the IKK/NF-kappaB pathway may delay damage and extend healthspan in patients with accelerated aging and chronic degenerative diseases of old age.”

Part of the study1 involved examination of the activity of NFkappaB in aging wild type mice with normal DNA repair. The mice were genetically treated so that a “reporter” gene under the control of NFkappaB would indicate when NFkappaB was activated. The older wild type mice had more cells expressing the reporter than young wild type mice, indicating increased NFkappaB activity with age. Similar to natural aging, NFkappaB activity increased with age in the XPE progeroid syndrome mice. The increase in the progeroid mice was greater than the increase in the wild type mice, though, with 2.5 fold increase in kidney, 2.5 fold increase in pancreas, 1.7 fold increase in muscle, and 4-fold increase in liver as compared to the wild type mice. The researchers identified the p65 subunit of NFkappaB as the predominant subunit contributing to this increased activity.

[http://www.life-enhancement.com/mag...mmatory-signaling-pathway-delays-dna-damage-i](http://www.life-enhancement.com/magazine/article/2781-inhibition-of-nfkappab-master-regulator-of-pro-inflammatory-signaling-pathway-delays-dna-damage-i)

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-06T15:20:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-happens-when-the-sun-goes-down.14351/post-163447>

It seems like regardless of the season my problems with my body burning up and temperature issues always get much, much worse as the sun starts to set. Why would this happen? We're planning our big move south and I'm anxious to see how getting more sun will help me. I know I do better even where I am now over the summer; however, the longer daylight hours seem to help a lot too and even in FL we'll still have only 10 hours of daylight over the winter, so that's not going to be much of a help.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-07T01:22:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-happens-when-the-sun-goes-down.14351/post-163466>

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-10T16:27:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-happens-when-the-sun-goes-down.14351/post-163765>

Why do you do better in light? Light orders atoms. It also uncondenses them to a small degree because like is an oxidant. When light is really powerful it makes UVB light which creates a reducing chemical that has no nitrogen in it called Vitamin D. This chemical allows you to eat foods that cause more inflammation because vitamin D keeps you from effluxing calcium in cells and calcium is the glue that holds your cell membrane lipid bilayers together. So, if you do better in light it tells me at night when it is absent your environment is very toxic. nnEMF liberates calcium.......and destroys your cell membrane. You need to bio hack your night time existence. I have a sense it is quite bad given what you wrote here.

# Thread: The original human diet was loaded with seafood.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-original-human-diet-was-loaded-with-seafood.14354/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-07T00:27:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-original-human-diet-was-loaded-with-seafood.14354/post-163464>

-   [http://www.kmbz.com/pages/21302532.php?contentType=4&contentId=17173047](http://www.kmbz.com/pages/21302532.php?contentType=4&contentId=17173047)
-
    Kenniwick man...Washington state...subsisted along the Columbia River 350 miles inland in an area abundant with terrestorial animals but chose to eat seafood...probably salmon spawning in the fresh water
    The original DHA MAN. Epi-paleo Rx for the win.
    "Schwarcz, who studies the diets of ancient people by analyzing isotopes in bones, said he was amazed after analyzing the collagen in the bones of Kennewick man in an effort to identify his "paleo" diet.
    "This guy was apparently living on a diet almost exclusively of marine foods; foods that come from the ocean," Schwarcz said.
    The results are surprising because the man was found 350 miles inland along the Columbia River near plains that were teeming with terrestrial
    wildlife. While he could have been subsisting on salmon swimming upstream, he was likely not venturing to hunt in the fields.
    "He was choosing not to eat that wildlife"

    How do wellness and time link?

    Health is merely the slowest form of death we create. This implies biology had to build its clock well.

    Most people know DHA (fish oil) is important to wellness, but few have any idea why. Biology needs a constant recurrent source of DHA from seafood to run your suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) at a faster rate than all other peripheral circadian clocks in tissues. If this timing relationship is altered relatively, the tissue with the faster running clock than the SCN develops HIGHER ubiquitination rates and ages much faster. When this happens initially illness manifests first. When it continues on chronically death comes faster than it should.
    Light has a universal speed limit at 186,000 miles an hour. Light travels 30 centimeters in one nanosecond. The only way to increase its energy is to increase its frequency of the wavelength. If timing is off, distant signals will also be awry. This very same feature, mentioned above about the Rolex in your head (SCN) is why GPS clocks orbiting the Earth must run faster than clocks on the ground to navigate properly. Atomic clocks are 38 microseconds faster than clocks on the ground. In 38 microseconds, at the speed of light, if this clock difference did not exist, GPS devices on Earth would be off by a factor over ten kilometers a day on the surface of Earth. How good would your iphone app be if it was off ten kilometers in Austin? When this mis-timing occurs in our brains’ mitochondria, the inaccurate timing results in things like neuro-degeneration, T2D, and autoimmunity. This is why low melatonin, low vitamin D levels, and high ubiquination rates are all associated with disease generation. The “mis-timing” leads to massive effects at the quantum scale of protons and electrons in CSF, because the scale of action of the SCN is on subatomic particles, not on GPS devices. The differences are smaller, because unlike orbiting clocks, our SCN’s are only 6 feet above our feet, while not rotating 14,000 kilometer’s an hour like atomic clocks orbiting Earth are. But you better believe, Einstein’s relativity is still functioning in that six foot difference even though we cannot measure the precise effects well yet. I know it exists because the laws of physics says I am correct. Just because biology couldn't measure these small time differences in the 20th century yet means little that I am right. Early in the 21 st century they became able to measure it and showed I was correct. Absence of evidence is not absence of effect. This was a prediction, I was confident that would be proven. Scientists realized that 6 foot difference has a massive effect on how the electrons and protons in your body move in our mitochondria. This was the beginning of where medicine got back on the rails in the 21 st century.
    Why was I so sure I was correct? Gravity bends light. Did you know that? This bending of blue and purple light gives a precise deflection to make a perfect reference point in building a clock mechanism. Every animal alive on this planet has a light timing mechanism in its body. This explains why the further we get from the Earth’s surface, the faster a clock must run to make timing accurate for biochemical reactants, because blue light bends in its wake. Because of gravitational clock speeding-up, this accounts for half of the effect of the bending of light in a gravitational field.

    When you consider that modern life uses blue light for technology, the problem becomes worse because of this light frequency relationship for our SCN. Blue light destroys DHA in cells and it lowers melatonin levels. This destruction of DHA slows the SCN clock in your head causing diseases to manifest. Are you beginning to see why modern life is destroying your cells?
    Blue light has a higher frequency, longer wavelength and higher photon energies in comparison to red light. In the early 21st century we need to advance technology the use red light and not blue to slow down ubiquitin rates to slow down the speed at which death comes to us. It is an idea who's time has come.

# Thread: Cause and effect or is it all correlation?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cause-and-effect-or-is-it-all-correlation.14356/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-07T06:56:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cause-and-effect-or-is-it-all-correlation.14356/post-163492>

-   When you really understand physics, we might say, shallow men believe in luck or in circumstance. Strong men believe in cause and effect. Those with natural wisdom, however, asks is cause and effect a cornerstone of science or a myth? Are there only probabilities and N =1? Bertrand Russell (1872 –1970) had an interesting take when he said, "All philosophers, of every school, imagine that causation is one of the fundamental axioms or postulates of science, yet, oddly enough, in advanced sciences such as gravitational astronomy, the word “cause” never occurs. … The law of causality, I believe, like much that passes muster among philosophers, is a relic of a bygone age, surviving, like the monarchy, only because it is erroneously supposed to do no harm." Karl Pearson (1856 – 1936), who has been credited with inventing mathematical statistics, would have agreed with Russell. He never talked about causation, though, only correlation. My idea about cause and effect is that its an idea created by relations of coupled cycles in biology.

-   Me, today with biophysics glasses..........I like the roll of the dice with Werner Heisenberg. I dont believe in cause and effect any longer.

-   Cause and effect went out of favor as a cornerstone of science about the time quantum mechanics was developed. Quantum mechanics is non-deterministic with events occurring randomly. Within the context of quantum mechanics, there is no reason or cause for an atom to decay at one time and not at another. The rise of quantum mechanics and the decline in the prominence of cause and effect are probably indeed cause and effect. However, even outside quantum mechanics there are problems with cause and effect. Much of physics, as Russell observed, does not explicitly use cause and effect. The equations work equally well forwards or backwards, deriving the past from present as much as the future from the past. Indeed, the equations of physics can even propagate spatially sideways rather than temporally forwards or backwards.

-   Relational order helps organisms carry out biochemical reactions to cause a coordinated movement of their parts. These movements are characterized by the laws of kinetics and thermodynamics.
    In biologic systems at all levels we observe events associated with the emergence of novelty and uniqueness of function. This allows life, to specifically associate with the creative power of nature in how it recycles atoms in the abiotic ecosystem on Earth. This system is based upon nitrogen, hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen cycles. All of these cycles are coupled directly by light and ordered under the power of chronobiology. Time is formed in the relations and collisions between light and particles in atoms. This is what "creates time" in biology, circadian or quantum time.

# Thread: SIBO, GERD, or IBS?  Ubiquitination 12 is live.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 74
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-07T15:05:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163521>

To know a person's library is, in some measure, to know their mind. There is a great difference between knowing and understanding: you can know a lot about something and not really understand it. Let Nature guide you. An unnatural point of view can be a dangerous luxury when substituted for real insight and understanding. Don't let modern reality, get in way of your insight. It is a barrier to scale. The strength of your obstacle determines the weight of your potentials. Your potential to use nature will provide the power to destroy today's reality.

[http://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-12-nitrogen-cycle-ecodynamics/](http://www.jackkruse.com/ubiquitination-12-nitrogen-cycle-ecodynamics/)

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-08T05:58:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163568>

Now this is the kind of blog that I like . Major details laid out here. As usual, I make notes to understand the blogs -- [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12)

Lots of hanging thoughts, which I think I'll eventually get to, but I'll post the ones at the top of my mind first. Hopefully the thoughts are somewhat coherent.

\----

This blog comes at a time when I have just literally yesterday experienced a bit of gut issues. Ate some fermented cabbage and pickled onions, and immediately felt bloated with some gut pain over the course of the day. No change to bristol score today, but the pain is clearly explained in the blog:

**Pain in these syndromes will vary, but pain will be associated with mismatched blood flow in the mesenteric plexus with the uncoupled production of nitrogen gases are made in the lumen of the gut and plasma of the mesenteric plexus.**​

Essentially, it's a problem of too much nitrogen (high Ubiquitination), likely from a combination of nitrates in said vegetables, and too much ammonia production. Lately, I've been eating a bit more protein too, and this may not be a good idea.

The blog mentioned the Ammonia -> Ubiquitination link, and would also explain another mechanism of how dietary protein can increase Ubiquitination rates. Earlier in the series, we see Protein intake stimulating mTOR, and now we see how excess ammonia can result from protein intake if de-nitrifiers cannot perform their job in an oxygen-rich gut environment.

\----

Now, fixing a bad gut basically comes down to decreasing Oxygen concentrations in the gut, while increasing oxygen concentrations in the rest of the tissues.

Somehow I feel nicotine can do this by increasing blood flow to the tissues surrounding the gut lumen, while potentially increasing NAD+. This assumes of course, that the GALT has enough DHA already in place to allow for the light-mediated reactions to happen. Not 100% on the details, and my own research is in progress.

Hydrogen-infused water sounds like a straightforward solution, since both the H+ and H- are useful. From the blog:

**This should also point out why now reverse osmosis water or supercooled water build health. It provides a huge army of protons without any food added in this process. This helps you maintain a diverse flora best with small risks and low O2 levels in the gut.**
​

I don't have access to that sort of water now, but I suspect that Sodium Bicarbonate is a cheap fix as well. The Bicarbonate ion could potentially deliver more protons to the gut flora. Another stray thought I have is that the similar electric configurations of the bicarbonate ion and Nitrates can help with Nitrogen-Carbon balance.

I also wonder how drinking stuff like vinegar would help? This could again be another proton source, but I may have my facts confused. Anecdotally it's thought to be a remedy to SIBO. Hmmm, maybe I should experiment with some apple cider vinegar + higher dose sodium bicarbonate.

Higher fat intake and lower protein intake is probably wise.

\----

When there is oxygen in your gut, I don't know what adding fermented foods to "re-populate the gut with good bacteria" would do. It could provide more bacteria to get rid of the oxygen, or it could makes your condition worse by making more ammonia to further accelerate ubiquitination rates.

\----

Why am I experiencing these symptoms now, and not earlier this year (where my gut was basically bulletproof)? It obviously has to do with the change in seasons, with simultaneous reduced light and reduced magnetism

Sidenote: one of the downsides of being in the Southern Hemisphere is that there are larger variations in solar output even when you're closer to the Equator like I am (16.5deg south). There is 7% more in Solar Radiation in December compared to June, meaning that you get diminished magnetism in Southern Hemisphere winters in addition to diminished sunshine. Summers here are also more light-dominant as a result.​

The "tropical Australian" climate will still present major variations in Nitrogen cycling as a result. Anecdotally, I'm not seeing that many green vegetables out in the farmer's markets, though fruit like pineapples and papayas are present.

\----

Like I said, more to come over the next few days, this was a hectic blog post (just the way I like it )

....

### DavidL — 2015-05-08T19:13:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163626>

Lots to digest here...

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-05-08T19:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163627>

Quote from the blog **"Today, about 60% of the total fixed nitrogen is produced industrially using the Haber-Bosch process. The Haber-Bosch process (manmade) now matches the entire nitrogen-fixation output of the oceans and exceeds the microbial fixation on land"
**
I find that pretty shocking. I really under-estimated how much we have interfered with Nature. I grew up on a farm and we used Nitrogen - sparingly. We were aware it was 'artificial' and at all times preferred to use cow manure for example. It was well known that the use of artificial Nitrogen 'weakened' the soil over time and was seen as something to be used as little as possible. But that was an older tradition for example we had no tractors of any kind, all 'work' was done by horses and people. Over time people get so disconnected from the land and tradition they jettison all their old beliefs and wisdom in favor of the 'new'. That's a reason I don't resonate so well with all this business about jettisoning 'old ideas' some of them are very good................a lot better than a lot of the so called 'new' ideas................

### patgrif@hotmail.com — 2015-05-08T20:10:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163628>

Dr Kruse, I find the parallels of the bacteria in the soil and in our gut very interesting. But would it be correct to think that though there are many similarities the function is also quite different? The bacteria in the soil is fixing Nitrogen out of thin air so to speak...............in the gut it seems a part of their job is 'disposing' of Nitrogen. I suppose a bit like the difference/similarity of chloroplasts and mitochondria? Similar but different. Does this mean there are literally different 'species' of bacteria in both realms or is it a case of different 'behavior' . For example do bacteria in the soil ALSO 'release light'................sorry I am such a 'beginner' here any light released on this topic would be of interest to me.............

On top of that 4 days ago we had a >7.0 magnitude earthquake in New Guinea, which is pretty close to where I'm at. 2 days ago, we had another >7.0 magnitude earth in New Guinear  -- [http://www.abc.net.au/news/2015-05-07/earthquake-strikes-off-png-local-tsunami-alert-issued/6453462](http://www.abc.net.au/news/2015-05-07/earthquake-strikes-off-png-local-tsunami-alert-issued/6453462)

Ben Davidson talks about how Solar Activity (specifically solar holes) is primarily responsible for causing Earthquakes. I'm awaiting his talk in late June for details, but it sounds like quick shifts in the Sun's activity (like flipping of it's magnetic field) very literally tug on the tectonic plates, and make earthquakes likely. Obviously, that tugging affects us to, which makes this Autumn transition particularly stressful for the folks down in the South Hemisphere. Even @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) is seeing some stress on his lab results

> [patgrif@hotmail.com said:](/goto/post?id=163628)
>
> Dr Kruse, I find the parallels of the bacteria in the soil and in our gut very interesting. But would it be correct to think that though there are many similarities the function is also quite different? The bacteria in the soil is fixing Nitrogen out of thin air so to speak...............in the gut it seems a part of their job is 'disposing' of Nitrogen. I suppose a bit like the difference/similarity of chloroplasts and mitochondria? Similar but different. Does this mean there are literally different 'species' of bacteria in both realms or is it a case of different 'behavior' . For example do bacteria in the soil ALSO 'release light'................sorry I am such a 'beginner' here any light released on this topic would be of interest to me.............

They look pretty similar to me -- [http://aem.asm.org/content/79/1/2.full](http://aem.asm.org/content/79/1/2.full)

Picture below of plant rhizosphere from wikipedia -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhizosphere](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rhizosphere)

From the blog:

Symbiotic nitrogen-fixing bacteria such as Rhizobium usually live in the root nodules of legumes (such as peas, alfalfa, and locust trees). Here they form a mutualistic relationship with the plant, producing ammonia in exchange for carbohydrates.

This same relationship exists between your gut microbiome and you in your intestines. Because of this relationship, legumes will often increase the nitrogen content of nitrogen-poor soils. **Our microbiome is capable of doing this when oxygen is present to excess.**

**In your gut, your microbiome increases the ammonia content to liberate nitrogen for you to use for your ubiquitination pathways. The more simplified your gut flora is the more nitrogen you liberate to drive cell growth.**

An anaerobic microbiome restricts decomposition of nitrogen in plant soils and your microbiome to limit growth. Aerobic decomposition unleashes nitrogen and facilitates decomposition. This drives growth and the amount of CO2 consumed by roots of plants and expired in the guts of mammals.​

### Josh — 2015-05-09T13:38:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163690>

Saturated soil=maximum amount of structured water that can be held by that soil ecosystem. Exceeding this limits nutrient and energy flows by sursurrounding parts of the system with bulk water. Dehydration and mishydration in living organisms where there is not enough charged surface to structure water and create necessary flows is analogous. Altering isotope ratios that change atomiic mass, density and buoyancy has profound implications related to this As it alters the convection gradients.

_**"When the gut flora is more diverse, the less ammonia you liberate, and cell growth is controlled and constrained because ubiquination pathways are tightly coupled to this mechanism in soils and our guts.** This is ubiquitous across all living things, hence where is name came from. This is why ammonia is liberated in the guts of people with liver and kidney disease and they develop brain symptoms like [asterixis.](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sEnp2ss8VoA) Most ammonia in the body forms when protein is broken down by bacteria in the intestines. The liver normally converts ammonia into urea, which is then eliminated in urine."_

Ubiquination is a 'light story' but "N" is also connected how exactly. I already asked about the similarity/difference of gut bacteria and soil bacteria. I can or could think of more but the one I already asked is ignored anyway (except for Yew to give him his props at least tried to answer it)

It is not only this.................last week I asked a question about the big temperature drop from being inside to womb to being born................I thought it was interesting especially as possibly it might have some possible tie in to CT and all the rest of it..............again just 'ignored'. Now I understand Dr Kruse is very busy and I have often wondered where he gets the time, there are many others with questions too, I understand all that but it seems to me something has changed or is it only directed at me. But if it is well maybe let me know somehow...................if I don't know it just feels 'embarrassing' to keep on asking questions and being ignored. You know the next step is I STOP asking questions and the next is I leave the blog and the community all together. This is not some kind of lame 'threat' just being honest and open about it. I would miss it greatly but sometimes painful decisions have to be made.............

Anyway and I am just being 'honest' here...................I find it discouraging to be just ignored, gives me the message to just stop asking questions which is how I learn, how else can I learn? They are maybe not 'great' questions they are basic questions of someone who is curious and wants to know and I can only imagine others might have very similar questions. Any progress I make come through the process of questioning not giving 'likes' all over the place or constantly 'agreeing' and giving 'booms' to each other to me that does not involve learning more self assurance and self congratulations.

I really don't think I have a 'bad attitude' but I believe in being 'honest'as best as I can. I for example don't share Neil's high regard for Ayn Rand I have said this before and no need to re-iterate but I hope people here maybe even Dr K might think this I have some 'oppositional disorder' to use the kind of latest psychological jargon. I am a big boy and I think of Neil as the same no need to that to cause any ripples of unease it just a difference of opinion and I think the blog might be 'healthier' for that. Am I wrong?

I don't want to make too much of this and maybe I am (making too much of it) but right now I am considering cancelling my passage on the Cruise Sometimes I no longer find the 'atmosphere' that inspiring..............I am not happy to feel this way I have had a lot of hope and belief in what I see here and of course of Dr K but something feels 'off' now.............

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-11T23:59:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163880>

I'll post a conversation I had regarding Wine and Cheese here, since it's probably relevant:

\----

I can't find the comments from Jack right now, but I do know the mechanisms as to why wine and cheese is a good pairing, so here are some notes ....

**\# Cheese**

The $$$ comment is relevant with regard to cheese, because to get the benefits, you generally need to buy the good stuff.

Cheese has lots of casein, so milk from cows that only produce A2 beta-casein is preferred, whereas most cheeses contain lots of A1 beta-casein. I like aged goat's cheeses, which generally have casein that are not immuno-stimulatory.

The cheese needs to be aged, and is ideally made from raw milk, both factors increase probiotic count. Just search for something like "cheese probiotic blue mold" on pubmed for a ton of studies regarding probiotic and prebiotic content in such cheese.

Then, I was actually just reading in Mae-Wan Ho's 'Unravelling AIDS' regarding some of the immune benefits of cheese. Since AIDS is an immune disease, this is relevant, and there is lots of evidence to show that high acidic environments in the gut are good (I'll dig up some excerpts from the book later in my day).

The lactobacilus in the cheese produce acid in the \*\*absence of oxygen\*\*. "Acid" just means "lots of H+", and Ubiquitination #12 just explained again why H+ is good: [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12#toc13](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12#toc13)

This should also point out why now reverse osmosis water or supercooled water build health. It provides a huge army of protons without any food added in this
process. This helps you maintain a diverse flora best with small risks and low O2 levels in the gut.
​

Probably also explains why people gain a benefit from drinking Apple Cider Vinegar, since it does provide a source of protons as well.

Wine contains acid too, so that's a good segway ....

\----

**\# Wine**

In the case of wine, you need to get the varietals that are tolerant to high UV environments, and that are grown in such environments. (ie: not Californian wine. Australian is good, Chilean is good, etc ...)

Altitude is usually a good thing for growing wine grapes, since there's more UV the higher you go.

I also always hear that lower humidity is better for wine grapes, or at least that's what some of the Aussie growers say. My Grandfather's friend Keith Lokan, is a grower in McLaren Vale. Lookan just says that humidity leads to rot, but I'm betting there's a Physics based explanation behind this as well. (ask Jack over some wine )

Polyphenols are nature's way of "encoding UV light", so no surprise that high UV environmetns give you grapes with lots of polyphenols. Resveratrol is the most studied polyphenol in wine, but I'm betting I'll find a lot more if I look into it.

Look at the shape of polyphenols -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polyphenol](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polyphenol) . Round electron cloud with large dipole moments and high UV absorptive capacity. LOTS of Hydrogen and Oxygen (OH), ZERO nitrogen. In fact, they are characterised by a lack of nitrogen related function. I'm not sure of the features yet, but my hunch is that this is a non-nitrogen depedent (and hence non-Ubiquitination-stimulating) method of bringing UV light to where it needs to go.

The alcohol stress is going to be a factor of course, so caution be advised there.

\----

**\## Circadian Timing of Wine**

The effect of resveratrol is pretty quick, especially when absorbed through the mouth. Hence the recommendation to swish wine in your mouth while drinking.

Resveratrol is a great SIRT1 activator, which allows cells to better perform appropriate autophagy and apoptotic processes. Thus, wine consumed during the last 2 hours of sunset, with some protein that stimulates high levels of protein synthesis (cheese! ), during a time of the day when cortisol is low and systemic protein synthesis rates are high, allows for efficient protein synthesis.

Protein recycling activity is high at this time, but "efficient protein synthesis" means that only the broken proteins are turned-over (marked for Ubiquitination), while most of your proteins can be repaired and recycled without high energetic cost.

\----

The H2 gas production from wine is something that I haven't looked into yet. Don't know if it occurs, though I doubt so. (The hydrogen we're referring to above is H+, while H2 gas provides both H+ and H-)

But whatever, nuts provide H2 gas via bacterial fermentation in the colon.

So Nuts + Wine + Cheese is the classic pairing.

.....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-12T02:10:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163890>

Then, quote from @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) -- [https://www.facebook.com/groups/872...&total\_comments=1&comment\_tracking={"tn":"R"}](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/1000739286605632/?comment_id=1000742066605354&offset=0&total_comments=1&comment_tracking=%7B%22tn%22%3A%22R%22%7D)

The absence or presence of light is a key redox sensor for life and death. Using blue light night puts you closer to illness and death because of this relationship.

When light is absent, electrons are more dense in water, oxygen levels are higher in water, proteins are more hydrophilic, and water can rise in microtubules defying gravity. This defying action affect the optical lattice timing mechanism in the the SCN's MT's .​

So I'm thinking that with a disease where the GALT is compromised, you can't rely on DHA in the GALT to absorb the light given off by gut bacteria. Polyphenols would help absorb that light and "give it to the GALT" (due to the polar nature of polyphenol molecules). Reduction of free light in the gut gives the effects that Jack mentioned in the quote.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-12T03:21:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163894>

Vitamin D3 has no nitrogen in it and some find it weird.
Its not a vitamin first of all. Vitamins are supposed to be nitrogen containing compounds that act as co-enzymes which your get from your diet.

Vitamin D does not contain nitrogen, which might be acceptable since A, C, and E don't either, but unlike C and E, D is also not a cofactor vitamin. It activates 1000 genes. This is 5% of the human genome. Why would it be an activator of the genome and contain no N? Could that be tied to light and ubiquitin?

A acts like a co-factor or I should say the precursor of a co factor in the eye photoreceptor enzymes, and also gets processed in a controlled manner into hormones for other parts of the body.

D3 acts just like a hormone, raising blood calcium levels and helping cell differentiation across the body, but unlike A's hormonal form (the retinoic acids), and every other hormone, its production is not produced on demand by homeostatic mechanisms in the body.

Sunlight exposure to UVB light determines how much vitamin D you get. D3 from diet is something that happens at the poles naturally and not in the tropics. Why on earth would we want sunlight exposure to raise our calcium levels? Or why might it be necessary for absorbing calcium from our food. What happens when calcium is effluxed in cells because of something in the environment? In fact, D3 also triggers cellular changes that are necessary for the absorption of magnesium, iron, zinc and phosphate too.

I suppose in our past we may have always gotten enough UV light to create the proper pro-hormones, but what about nocturnal or burrowing mammals?

It's so weird to have a hormone, that is necessary for key nutrient absorption and dependent on exogenous factors that seem to have nothing to do with the hormones function. There is a deep story here tied to light and N.
**Get your circadian rhythm fixed first. Then CT, fix the mitochondria, and pour in the electrons.**

### DavidL — 2015-05-12T04:34:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-163902>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=163868)
>
> And Pat if you want to know why the Nitrogen is found around iron in heme and why it ties into the skin of humans: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25703782](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25703782)

Why we are less hairy the further we move away from the equator?...my father's mother and grandmother are Swedish...have little to no leg hair at all.

Melanin. Chaga mushroom contains a fair amount of melanin. So is the effect of ingesting melanin supplies electrons to the respiratory chain resulting in ATP extrasynthesis? The term melanin refers to the black or brownish pigment which appears to occur at all phyletic levels of biological organization and is ordinarily considered to be an insoluble, nitrogen-containing polymer.

"Commoner, Townsend, and Pake (1954) who had observed an electron spin resonance (e.s.r.) in natural melanin which they attributed to free radicals trapped in the pigment. The entrapment and protection of a free radical by a growing polymer had been demonstrated with methacrylate (Fraenkel, Hirshon, and Walling, 1954) and one might have expected that autoxidized melanins prepared from suitable biphenols such as dihydroxphenylanlanine (DOPA) would show e.s.r. signals. Subsequent investigation in our laboratory of both natural melanins (squid, human hair, and Harding-Passey mouse melanoma), and melanins produced in vitro by both autoxidation and enzymatic oxidation revealed similar e.s.r. signals in all cases and suggested that at least for the autoxidized melanin the unpaired electron was located to at most a few monomer units (Vivo-Acrivos and Blois, 1958). Mason, Ingram, and Allen (1960) have reported the results of an e.s.r. study of a number of natural melanin samples, including the finding that the spin density was increased upon irradiation by ultraviolet light. At the same time, to explain the paramagnetism of melanins, Longuet-Higgins (1960) suggested that melanin behaves as a one-dimensional semiconductor with the protons acting as electron traps."
source: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1367600/pdf/biophysj00640-0051.pdf](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1367600/pdf/biophysj00640-0051.pdf)

Chaga is also known for it's effects to improve the condition of the gut, namely IBD and colitis.

> Nitrogenase is composed of two iron associated proteins, so iron deficient regions cannot support diazotrophic growth. It is also oxygen labile, so nitrogen fixation must be carried out anaerobically; this can present difficulties, which diazotrophs overcome by spatial and/or temporal segregation from intracellular oxygen and oxygen production. So plowing and tilling, and otherwise energetically turning the soil would be detrimental to those nitrogen-fixing bacteria. Purple in leaves are high levels of anthrocyanins. For a normally green plant this would indicate a phosphate deficiency. Certain plants have been selected for their natural purple leaf color. Obviously they must also contain green chlorophyll to be able to grow. Remember the color you see is what the plant does not absorb.

### Danny — 2015-05-24T19:06:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-165216>

> [yewwei.tan said:](/goto/post?id=163568)
>
> Now this is the kind of blog that I like . Major details laid out here. As usual, I make notes to understand the blogs -- [http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12](http://qhwiki.com/excerpts:jk-ubiquitination-12)
>
> Lots of hanging thoughts, which I think I'll eventually get to, but I'll post the ones at the top of my mind first. Hopefully the thoughts are somewhat coherent.
>
> \----
>
> This blog comes at a time when I have just literally yesterday experienced a bit of gut issues. Ate some fermented cabbage and pickled onions, and immediately felt bloated with some gut pain over the course of the day. No change to bristol score today, but the pain is clearly explained in the blog:
>
>
> **Pain in these syndromes will vary, but pain will be associated with mismatched blood flow in the mesenteric plexus with the uncoupled production of nitrogen gases are made in the lumen of the gut and plasma of the mesenteric plexus.**​
>
>
> Essentially, it's a problem of too much nitrogen (high Ubiquitination), likely from a combination of nitrates in said vegetables, and too much ammonia production. Lately, I've been eating a bit more protein too, and this may not be a good idea.
>
> The blog mentioned the Ammonia -> Ubiquitination link, and would also explain another mechanism of how dietary protein can increase Ubiquitination rates. Earlier in the series, we see Protein intake stimulating mTOR, and now we see how excess ammonia can result from protein intake if de-nitrifiers cannot perform their job in an oxygen-rich gut environment.
>
> \----
>
> Now, fixing a bad gut basically comes down to decreasing Oxygen concentrations in the gut, while increasing oxygen concentrations in the rest of the tissues.
>
> Somehow I feel nicotine can do this by increasing blood flow to the tissues surrounding the gut lumen, while potentially increasing NAD+. This assumes of course, that the GALT has enough DHA already in place to allow for the light-mediated reactions to happen. Not 100% on the details, and my own research is in progress.
>
> Hydrogen-infused water sounds like a straightforward solution, since both the H+ and H- are useful. From the blog:
>
>
> **This should also point out why now reverse osmosis water or supercooled water build health. It provides a huge army of protons without any food added in this process. This helps you maintain a diverse flora best with small risks and low O2 levels in the gut.**
> ​
>
> I don't have access to that sort of water now, but I suspect that Sodium Bicarbonate is a cheap fix as well. The Bicarbonate ion could potentially deliver more protons to the gut flora. Another stray thought I have is that the similar electric configurations of the bicarbonate ion and Nitrates can help with Nitrogen-Carbon balance.
>
> I also wonder how drinking stuff like vinegar would help? This could again be another proton source, but I may have my facts confused. Anecdotally it's thought to be a remedy to SIBO. Hmmm, maybe I should experiment with some apple cider vinegar + higher dose sodium bicarbonate.
>
> Higher fat intake and lower protein intake is probably wise.
>
> \----
>
> When there is oxygen in your gut, I don't know what adding fermented foods to "re-populate the gut with good bacteria" would do. It could provide more bacteria to get rid of the oxygen, or it could makes your condition worse by making more ammonia to further accelerate ubiquitination rates.
>
> \----
>
> Why am I experiencing these symptoms now, and not earlier this year (where my gut was basically bulletproof)? It obviously has to do with the change in seasons, with simultaneous reduced light and reduced magnetism
>
>
> Sidenote: one of the downsides of being in the Southern Hemisphere is that there are larger variations in solar output even when you're closer to the Equator like I am (16.5deg south). There is 7% more in Solar Radiation in December compared to June, meaning that you get diminished magnetism in Southern Hemisphere winters in addition to diminished sunshine. Summers here are also more light-dominant as a result.​
>
>
> The "tropical Australian" climate will still present major variations in Nitrogen cycling as a result. Anecdotally, I'm not seeing that many green vegetables out in the farmer's markets, though fruit like pineapples and papayas are present.
>
> \----
>
> Like I said, more to come over the next few days, this was a hectic blog post (just the way I like it )
>
> ....

Have you tried ingesting diatomaceous earth? Jack talks about this helping conversion of ammonia to nitrates.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-25T06:13:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-165261>

@[Danny](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/12028/) : No, I have never tried diatomaceous earth. Thanks for reminding me. My gut is definitely my weak link, and is the first thing to fail in the face of any significant stressor. It also tends to be more vulnerable in lower light cycles (like now, where I'm getting about 11.25hrs of daylight, albeit it's pretty strong daylight).

Well, chance of harm is pretty low, so I picked up some from a source that is probably 100km from me in Mossman, so I guess it's decently local.

\----
**Footnote: Quote from Ubiquitiation #12**

When a plant or animal dies or an animal expels waste, the initial form of nitrogen is organic. Bacteria or fungi convert the organic nitrogen within the remains back into ammonium (NH4+), a process called ammonification or mineralization. The conversion of ammonia to nitrate is performed primarily by soil-living bacteria and other nitrifying bacteria. This also happens in our gut microbiome.

Why is all this important? In the primary stage of nitrification, the oxidation of ammonium (NH4+) is performed by bacteria such as the Nitrosomonas species, which converts ammonia to nitrites (NO2–). Other bacterial species such as Nitrobacter, are responsible for the oxidation of the nitrites into nitrates (NO3–).

It is important for the ammonia to be converted to nitrates because accumulated nitrites are toxic to plant life. It is also toxic to us.

This is why those with bad gut function often respond to diatomceous earth ingestion. You are effectively trying to recover coupling of your nitrogen cycle that is uncoupled in your gut microbiome because it is simplified.

The usual uncoupler is either excessive blue light exposure which leads to alterations in your lower esophageal sphincter (LES). When the LES does not work, GERD results and alters the amount of oxygen in the gut.

.....

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-25T19:37:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/post-165313>

Here are my thoughts about diet/ketosis/GERD/GI cancers/ and quantum biology linkages

Diet or metabolic therapies are clearly a step in the right direction but researchers need to go way deeper and ask way better questions. Will they? Hopefully, but my concern is that without significant clinical findings they will abandon it because the pipeline of $$$$ to molecular mechanisms is so greased in the NIH funding scheme. Until they realize the target of cancer is light signaling via ubiquitin marking of proteins the metabolic therapies will be a secondary player in cancer designed to by patients time while clinicians fix the environmental light issues driving the disease. You would think people get the light issue because photosynthesis is base of all food chains. Light and CO2 (thin air) = sugar. Carbon flows in all living things is controlled by ubiquitin. That is the level they need to get too. I truly hope you and others continue to stick their nose down this new rabbit hole because hopefully they will begin to trip over the truth to stop the nonsense we are doing now in research.

I am going even deeper. Medicine can no longer afford half truths. We need to get this right based upon nature's quantum rules of engagement. Your zip code or location, specifically environment and its associated positive charge (inflammation is + charged) in the atmosphere around you along with a chronic lack of DHA (collects electrons in cells) make you think the sun and melanoma are linked in the literature.........they are not. On the surface when I say this many scientist squirm because they believe dermatology dogma. Here is the key: An ignorant man's report of what a wise man says can never be fully accurate, because they unconsciously translates what they hear into something he can understand. If the sun was toxic to living systems, trees would be cancers main target yet plants control ubiquitin with auxin proteins from their roots where the microbiome meets the tree. You have a control mechanism in you too in your gut. The modern world just does not realize that being connected to Earth is that key mechanism within the gut. Sinclair's paper on pseudohypoxia and NAD+ levels is the key. Ketosis simulates the light signal, **_but can't recouple NADH to NAD+._** It helps for sure, but I am interested in reversals not suspended renovation. Healing the gut is entirely about re-establishing an anaerobic environment within it to stimulate the gut to use nitrogen. We do this by adding protons to the micro-biome and limiting O2 in the lumen, to force them to use other atoms (N and S) as the terminal electron acceptor compared to our enterocyctes which use O2 maximally. **_Protons without Oxygen = no cancer._** This is why GERD is associated with Barrett's change and esophogeal CANCER. The irony is that modern medicine advocate PPI's which block protons flow and think they are helping. The key part is closing the LES after food enters it. This is a job of clock genes in the LES and SCN working in unison. Today that is not happening and this is why the shit storm begins.

# Thread: Getting the Side-Eye about EMF
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### WereBear — 2015-05-07T17:09:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163527>

You know, when I talk about my Kruse Protocol, people are enthused about the morning smoothie/BAB concept. They get really interested about what I do for better sleep. Their eyes glaze over a bit when I start talking about eliminating wheat and sugar, and that's before I set foot on the conversational quicksand of low carb, seafood, and Omega 6 fats.

But when I bring up EMF, I see them edging away with their Crackpot Radar pinging like an old submarine movie.

I need to craft some approaches, because I've started a blog about my own health issues. (I'm a writer, it's what I do.) I have a slant towards feminism, how medical science ignores women hormone issues, and the amazing things good food, sleep, and water can do. But the thing I still grapple with is how to broach the subject of EMF pollution, grounding, and other concepts that have my listeners twirling their index fingers around their ears behind my back.

What have you done to explain this to your friends and relations?

### fitness@home — 2015-05-07T19:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163538>

^^ This. I am the square peg in a world of round holes.

At work just yesterday I was told that I was born in the wrong era...should have been a hippy. Really? I actually lived through that decade, although I was a child

I enjoy my uniqueness and wear it proudly.

### cantweight — 2015-05-08T01:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163553>

I think of it like this now....after being frustrated over and over when the glaze over occurred every time I even got into nnEMF/blue light....I am planting a seed. I will sow them every chance I get, some will take and some will not. When people are ready they will come back. It has happened multiple times now...."Hey remember those orange glasses you were telling me about?" Plant the seeds, some will bloom. Dont be frustrated by those that dont, give them time

### Lahelada — 2015-05-08T01:22:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163554>

Same as cantweigh. I start with morning sun,best sleep before midnight and eating fish. Here the dealbreaker could be any of the above but adding CT now that is an outright no no.....

### Penny — 2015-05-08T01:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163555>

I've taken to printing out pubmed studies like the latest batch I posted here which basically say that someone actually \*measured\* biophotons coming from a human body... I also have enough statistics available to get them thinking about how strange it is that autism is doubling every 5 years, one out of 2 Americans have a chronic disease (CDC data BTW), 1 out of 3 are at least gluten free - and did you ever know anyone with fibromyalgia growing up? And then I ask them what else in their environment is doubling every 5 years? Then I chat about cirdadean biology and what do they think happens to the signal to make melatonin when the equivalent of 10 morning suns hits them in the face while watching late night t.v....

In the end though, I just give away blue blockers - I think I've given out at least 10 pairs - 4 as Christmas presents - 2 also had 2 bottles of Bitter Melon for my nieces with PCOS... when I can get them to change a little and they feel better, then the lectures really intensify... I bore everyone I meet - last weekend I was at a party telling a group of people how it is impossible for 100,000 chemical reactions to take place within 1 second without being overrun by excrement... Then I chat about evolution and what the hell was there in the beginning - oh, light... duh - then there are all the light receptors built into our brain - and you can chat about how did life survive the ice age - show a few Magda Havas videos on blood clotting and EMF - ask them if they think it's strange that people are having strokes in their 50's.... ????

And then I'm just warming up... especially if it's a kid, all you want is to instill a sense of "don't believe all the BS you are being told" and "follow the $$$" - you can also chat about how the FCC is currently being headed up by the chief lobbyest for the cell phone industry and the 1996 telecommunications act where people don't get a say in where they put cell phone towers - then you ask "Does that sound like an agency that is trying to protect you?"

You can chat about Henry Lai and how EMF breaks the double bonds of DNA - and isn't it strange how infertility has skyrocketed - oh and I wouldn't put that cell phone in your pocket if you're a guy - you can even talk about how the cell phone manual says not to put it next to your body - or how Apple's new iphone/ipad now have a sensory chip where they lower the power if it's close to your skin - why is that do you suppose? Truly, the more Socratic you get, the more they have to think...

There is always the World Health Organization saying that wireless is a class 2b carcinogen -

### caroline — 2015-05-08T02:13:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163558>

I look at it this way now ....you can lead a horse to water - but you can't make them drink!

I have the most luck with young guys ....when I ask them where they keep their iPhone .....It is always in their pants pocket. You can really get their attention when you tell them they are frying their balls!

That is what one man asked me ...and now that is what I say!

I think planting a seed is the only way - no one wants to be preached at.

One of my dears friend often says to me now ..... What would Jack do/say about this?

JK always tells us .... we can only help ourself.....when someone is sick and tired of being sick and tired they will ask question.

Be the change you want to see in your loved ones ..... no one can argue with that!

### Martin — 2015-05-08T17:07:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163619>

It's especially tough to see the people you care for glaze over. I've always been a "know-it-all" and wanted to pass along cool stuff to help people in their lives because someone helped me with information (read: Doc Kruse).

My small circle of friends lean towards more "spiritual" reasons for everything, and know that I'm coming from a more science-based quantum perspective. The more I learn about what all those woo-woo claims and stories actually mean, the more I'll drop nuggets into the mix as we talk. Dismissed at first, after over five years on this site, I've keep up a steady stream of revelations and discoveries that cut through the bullshit some authors and lecturers put out there.

Little by little, I see how some well-meaning author/podcaster/guru claiming, "we are immortal beings of Light seeded here from the Pleiades cluster" without know what the hell that actually means. All sizzle, no steak, and it's buttered all over the place. When one can cut through the bull, one can see how utterly amazing the actual facts are!

I used a "light" reference recently at gathering and instead of getting the "Side-Eye" (love that!), they listened intently and nodded in recognition of something that resonated with them. I guess I'm getting through a little.

Maybe, just maybe, there's _something_ to this quantum science that's explaining all sorts of weird things that big-brained aquatic ape ancestors tend to put a lot of superstitious meaning on.

They can and do come around. Why, just the other day, my DW finally came around on wearing blue blockers. She's seen me wear them for nearly four years now and keeps seeing articles around what light does. Not sure what changed her thinking, but I welcomed it. Guess I look hot in wrap-around yellow glasses.

While we all ultimately have to come to those decisions ourselves, it took someone else to point out these quantum principles.

Thanks, Doc.

Martin

I bet you do Martin !
