I act for them, i play the role of being me. By doing that i can give proof to others that the things i do is for a reason.

In times like this, we tend to give words, both written and said to much meaning. Also they are quite bad at describing living things. This means that words like EMF will always have tons of different meanings depending on who you ask...

What i do because i done some deep work in the field of EMF for many years, i never say the words. I paint pictures for them that they can take bites out of, slowly building a web that when they start to see the big picture, woha... They see what modern technology indeed do to us.

This is why, real living must be physical not just digital media. I can pound words for days, still doing shitty things, i do not use myself as a living proof anymore. Therefor i think, to show others respect one must give up much to gain even more, give a little virus to others that can slowly catch on to them, wake them up to change... And of course, not everyone likes my ideas, those who do... I give away my whole life to.

### Valerie — 2015-05-09T00:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/getting-the-side-eye-about-emf.14383/post-163640>

Fortunately my DH is open to trying new things. After trying CT he noticed some incremental improvement...just not fast enough for his liking. He stopped and after getting pain in his foot he decided to re-start CT. Interestingly enough it was not very hard for him to get back in the ice baths after about 6-9 months. He does wear his blue blockers at night while watching his ipad most nights, which I suspect has helped immensely. I measured his fasting blood glucose and he has seen it come down from 110 to about 96. I consider that a win. He is a very observant person and I believe he has observed my improvements. He just recently allowed me to cut off WiFi after installing a MOCA system. I am hoping for further improvements. I didn't bother asking him to change his diet (I knew that would not happen). I asked him to focus on upping his seafood intake.

As far as my children....that is my next battle...bluelight, cell phones circadian cycle....

# Thread: Pool Design
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-08T15:21:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163603>

So we're definitely going to need a pool once we actually get to FL. What things should we consider? I know we'll want to go with a saltwater system, but if we are fortunate enough to install an inground pool, concrete would void any earthing, right? Is there a better material to use? Thanks for any info!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-08T16:52:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163614>

salt water or ozonated is best......avoid halogen chemicals to the greatest degree if possible

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-09T15:23:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163698>

Thanks Dr. Kruse. Appreciate the info. Another question - how do y'all keep your pools cold in the summer?!

### fitness@home — 2015-05-09T17:18:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163704>

> [lookingforanswers said:](/goto/post?id=163698)
>
> Thanks Dr. Kruse. Appreciate the info. Another question - how do y'all keep your pools cold in the summer?!

Can't do this here in Houston in summer. It feels like 85 degree bath water unless I dive the 12 feet in deep end.

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-10T15:04:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163761>

@[fitness@home](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5474/) - So how do you manage CT in the summer? I don't know that we'll be able to afford a commercial ice machine! What other options are there? Thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-10T16:28:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163766>

you can use an ice machine......but realize that pool water will always be lower then the ambient temperature in the atmosphere youre in so technically you will always be CTing.

### fitness@home — 2015-05-10T22:48:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163794>

> [lookingforanswers said:](/goto/post?id=163761)
>
> @[fitness@home](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5474/) - So how do you manage CT in the summer? I don't know that we'll be able to afford a commercial ice machine! What other options are there? Thanks

My husband is building a CT tank for me. Got a chiller off of craigslist and have been collecting everything we need to complete it (Lowes, Home Depot, aquarium/pond stuff, etc). We are at the final assembly and trim stage. He just finished spray foaming the stock tank on Friday afternoon before we took off for the weekend. It helps that he is very creative and also has a maintenance background.

The CT tank will be kept in the 53-56 degree range. As much as I hate to bring it inside, I dislike the native mosquito population even more. It will reside in either our master bedroom or breakfast room (have a dining room, dont need both!).

He isn't a follower of the protocols here, but supports me in my endeavors . The tank will be placed into an enclosure and finished on the outside with cedar. It will aslo have castor wheels built into the base for easier moving.

Will post pics when done...

P.s. Our pool's water temp was 82 degrees today.

### JMO — 2015-05-11T03:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163803>

I have an aquarium chiller and use it inside on my master tub. I tried outside in a stock tub but it couldn't handle the outside temp in the summer.

### fitness@home — 2015-05-11T03:16:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-163804>

> [JMO said:](/goto/post?id=163803)
>
> I have an aquarium chiller and use it inside on my master tub. I tried outside in a stock tub but it couldn't handle the outside temp in the summer.

My chiller is currently being used to cool water in the bathtub upstairs. It is a shallow tub though. Can't wait for the big CT tub to be done.

### Danco3636 — 2015-06-10T02:54:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pool-design.14400/post-167307>

Would love to see it.... been considering getting a top loader freezer, putting a rubber liner in it and keeping that at a cold temp this summer. My concern is cleaning out the water from time to time.... I guess a siphon hose may work. Any suggestions?

# Thread: Can you help me help my girlfriend with her health problems?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mr.Bliss — 2015-05-08T16:13:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/post-163608>

Hello friends,

My girlfriend and I want to ask you for help on what to do about a few problems she’s currently struggling with. We’re both planning to do the leptin reset and get a Magnetico and shielding equipment eventually. But right now she needs help mostly with her digestion in order to do the leptin reset, since it requires the digestion of so much protein and fat.

Right now she is struggling with the digestion of literally all foods. She is experiencing this feeling of constant dull pain in the center of her belly. Her belly is really hard in some areas when you touch it. She feels uncomfortably full after every meal, even though she eats small portions. Often times she’s having bad digestion, some constipation, bloating, and heartburn. Right now she’s eliminating once a day and the consistency is a bit softer than normal. And even to just go once a day she has to be totally relaxed in the morning without any stress and rushing, otherwise she couldn’t go at all the whole day.

She’s taking digestive enzymes with HCl and Oxbile ([http://www.nowfoods.com/Super-Enzymes-180-Capsules.htm](http://www.nowfoods.com/Super-Enzymes-180-Capsules.htm)) but they don’t seem to do very much. Before she was taking Aloe caps ([http://www.nowfoods.com/Aloe-Vera-5000mg-100-Softgels.htm](http://www.nowfoods.com/Aloe-Vera-5000mg-100-Softgels.htm)) which also didn’t really help much. Over the last 6 month she has tried high quality probiotics ([http://www.prescript-assist.com/products/](http://www.prescript-assist.com/products/)) to help her digestion.

These problems are existing for half a year now. The weird thing is that no matter what she eats it gives her these problems. At first it was just animal fats and lots of protein and with plant foods she seemed to do better, even though it doesn’t make much sense. For some reason she was doing better with a more plant food diet with just supplementary animal foods. She was doing better with non-paleo gluten free grains and legumes and even some dairy in her diet, even though she knows it’s not paleo and therefore not good to eat.

She never felt good with 100% paleo and always needed some gluten free grains for her digestion for some reason (fiber).

But for two months in the beginning of 2014 she was eating a ketogenic diet. During this time her digestion worked really well but she had really low overall energy. Also she experienced heart palpitations and couldn’t sleep. She woke up several times during the night and when she tried to fall asleep her heart was pounding really fast so she had to stop the ketosis.

From age 17 to 22 she was eating vegetarian. From 20 to 22 she was eating on and off vegan and also raw vegan for the last year. During this time she didn’t experience digestive problems but acquired nutrient deficiencies (lack of B12, iron, bad brittle finger nails with horizontal ridges since then) and felt really weak during this time. Since age 22 (now she’s almost 25) she was eating a ‘balanced’ glutenfree diet with meat, fish, eggs, some dairy and just trying to eat everything organic and high quality.

Overall now she is feeling exhausted everyday, has joint pain (which became better during the 2 months of ketosis), an unstable feeling in joints, weakness in muscles, low endurance and strength. She also has inflammation in her skin, not acne, but ingrown hairs and very itchy dry skin. She uses tons of coconut oil on her skin to moisturize it but it gets dry very fast a few hours later.

She has poor circulation with her extremities always being really cold and she often times feels lightheaded when standing up.

Often times she feels really stressed, has moderate anxiety and experiences mood swings and intense emotional highs and lows and slight depression sometimes. She’s not taking any medications but her sister and her mother are taking anti-depressants (Paxil).

So her and I totally know that many of her problems could be fixed with an epi-paleo diet, leptin reset, low emf & blue light exposure etc. and we are planning to move outside of the city and get rid of our phones. We also want to buy a Magnetico soon and also want to shield our sleeping place with an emf shielding canopy.

But right now the most crippling thing for her is her digestive problems. In this state a leptin reset wouldn’t be possible. So we have to fix or at least improve this first. This is the most important factor right now.

So do you know what to do in this case? Should she go see a doctor?

Any help would be really appreciated. Thank you so much!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-08T16:54:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/post-163615>

sounds like she has developed and AI. She needs to see a doctor and get tested.

### Mr.Bliss — 2015-05-12T13:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/post-163945>

She is from the US and right now she is visiting me in Germany for a couple of months. We want to move together later this year. So the problem for her right now is that she doesn't know if her health insurance is covering these costs. We already assume that she has AI conditions because she already has allergies. So is there anything to be done other than testing for AI?
I forgot to mention that she also has had Epstein-Barr since she was 2.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-12T14:17:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/post-163947>

She needs to get assessed for the basic AI labs by a doctor. She could wait until she returns to US

### JC1980 — 2015-05-13T02:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-you-help-me-help-my-girlfriend-with-her-health-problems.14401/post-164025>

Sounds like you have some good gunk built up in her liver, small intestines/gut. I've had some good success doing saltwater purges... it sounds like she is in a great deal of discomfort.

# Thread: The Composer Who Sold His iPod
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 53
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Paul T. — 2015-05-08T20:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-163632>

Hi everyone! New to the forum, but not new to epi-paleo or Dr. Kruse's site. So fortunate to have 'stumbled' across this wonderful community a year ago and hope to contribute in some way or another. Just wanted to introduce myself:

I'm a composer/conductor/producer/songwriter/one of those music jerks with perfect pitch born and raised in Chicago. Just a little background on my career's environment \- lived and worked in major cities my whole life (Chicago, Los Angeles, Nashville, NYC), I've written, produced, and sang on over 10 albums including one that won 2 GRAMMYS (16+ hour days in recording studios - no windows, no clocks, just bathing in nnEMF), toured the world from Estonia to Japan (long flights galore), done Carnegie Hall (some of the worst sleep ever has been in NYC high-rise hotels), Ravinia, led clinics with choirs and orchestras across the USA, go to the GRAMMY's, all that stuff that seems like a dream job. Add the music culture of late night sessions/parties/galas, drug usage, long stretches of career struggle, alcohol abuse, and you've got a perfect storm in the 21st Century (as you all know).

Been on a 6-year journey with my health as I finally admitted to myself (it's incredible how the ego can deny something so obvious) something was wrong. When I truly listened, the truth about my health became unbelievably vivid. Couldn't have done it without Dr. Kruse and this community.

Going to post multiple lab results from the past couple years in 'My Optimal Journal' or 'Optimal Labs' section, but the focal point of my journey? Stress. Kills. Even though it was stress I thrived in (high-pressure, strict deadlines in studios, live performances, publishing contract negotiations), chronic stress kills. No supplement, no diet ever made me optimal.

Dr. Kruse has absolutely changed my life (for the better, of course). What's scary to me are the absolute parallels in epi-paleo/quantum health and how I've approached music throughout my career. Perhaps it's Quantum Interpretation for Producers and Conductors? Just an example - the two pieces of imagery I've used for years - way before I knew about epi-paleo - to connect technique (quantum physics of singing?) with empathy (emotional intuition/quantum biology of emotion?) of the singer are Light and Water. Fluidity and continuum - critical in effortlessly opening the voice. Maybe I'll figure out a way to incorporate magnetism to vividly capture and develop their craft. I've also railed against the use of technology for years in developing artists (particularly young singers), that it leads to the inability to be in the now, which leads to fear of embracing true emotional/spiritual moments in our life, which leads to a lack of empathy for the 'real world', which means you don't actually listen to the music. With this site, I'm beginning to understand the quantum context of why that happens. A rebirth, another Renaissance is coming in music. In my vocal studio, there is nothing but the student, the score, and me.

Looking forward to being part of this community. Thank you all for your continued passion and incredible work with finding the truth. That's what I do in music - I don't have 'tastes' in music, I have 'truths' in music, across all genres. When you sing/play/perform the truth, from hip-hop to Classical, I am moved. When you're alone and actively listen, your ears become your eyes, and sound becomes vivid. It's life-changing.

Thanks for reading my ramblings, folks.

Paul

### Paul T. — 2015-05-09T18:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-163720>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=163701)
>
> Welcome Paul....
>
> Love to hear your thoughts on 432hz vs the current tuning of instruments...

Pleasure to meet you, Shijin13! That is an incredibly fascinating discussion, one that has gone highly contested in the music world - particularly with early music/classical folks.

Scientifically/quantum healthwise - I haven't done enough research (yet) to either endorse or refute the 432 hz tuning standard as a performance practice (context is so crucial here). If anyone has links, I would love to read them. All I've read so far is hyperbole - nothing has been put into practice.

Contextually/anecdotally - There has never been a true universal standard in tuning. Not until the 19th (not even, really) and 20th Centuries did major orchestras begin to embrace a standard, and even now, there are orchestras on the east coast of the US with a 443 tuning and orchestras in the Midwest with 440. Before the 19th Century, there were organs in churches of the same town that didn't even match in tuning (tuning an organ is an incredible skill). Again, context is a huge clue. With perfect pitch in the 20th and 21st Centuries, the concert A that I permanently hear in my brain 24/7 is A 440. I can tell immediately when an orchestra or choir is above or below the frequency. However, does that cause stress to me? Hell no. I've been moved by baroque orchestras in 432 tuning and bawling like a baby listening to Bruckner Symphony No. 8 in A 443. I've performed in multiple tunings and have noticed no difference in openness of my voice, tension, vibration of my body (cells), how it moves me, etc. I've had life-changing moments listening to A440.

Perhaps I'm not entangled enough to realize the truth of 432 hz? We shall see as we dig deeper into this journey.

What does bother me when listening to a piece of music? When I stop believing in the artists. When does this happen? First symptom is when they go flat or sharp within their respective tuning. THAT is what creates stress to the ear and the brain. It's a mismatch between their empathetic process and technique. The focus stops and the performance is just... there. Tension ensues, followed by mediocrity.

Context is key; melody is king. This is one of the first mantras I give to my students.

### Paul T. — 2015-05-09T20:12:02+0300

Thank you for the kind words! Pleasure to meet you on this medium. Excellent question on cold and sound! If anyone has any research on that, please send it this way. Perhaps I'll lead a choir rehearsal outside in January next year in Chicago, throw them in Lake Michigan and report back. ;-)

You're on to something, though. When I embraced epi-paleo, I kid you not, my hearing - particularly when envisioning (audibly? Yikes) a piece of music I'm about to write or produce - became even more sensitive/clear. My voice opened and deepened/resonated even more. I didn't have to believe anymore in epi-paleo; I knew.

### prAna303 — 2015-05-09T20:47:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-163727>

>
> There has never been a true universal standard in tuning. Not until the 19th (not even, really) and 20th Centuries.

Welcome.

Hope you will enjoy this trip, and what a trip it can be...

First thing that comes to my mind is, tuning for me is adjusting to the environment... This should go on forever and everything changes all the time. Each performance is new, new people who share your music. This would in my mindset mean that you must be able to adjust to everykind of situation, just like life in the whole is.

Also, old fashioned instruments were often based upon material made from nature. They change constantly, air, light, humidity, the list goes on. Just like the different things in life.

Therefor, fixing something is also making things locked up and quite boring. Things that should be able to resonate goes away, new things can not be created anymore because, everything should be possible but not predictable.

For me, and for those on this forum who has followed my ideas know my passion for the great arts...

Music, acting, painting, vintage wines, list is long and on top of it is dance.

Once again, welcome and do pound us with your ideas, we need it.

### Paul T. — 2015-05-12T20:10:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-163989>

Shameless artist plug - if anyone is remotely interested in listening to any of the junk I've written, produced, conducted, performed on, etc. over the years, please feel free to listen at the link provided below. From classical to EDM, I've been involved. Also, I welcome any discussion in music, and I mean any discussion. Along with the 'neat' stuff I do in music, advocacy has been a major part of my career. Having a quantum context with advocacy in the arts is going to be my secret weapon.

If anyone has any quantum articles/context of perfect pitch, please, please send them my way. So many damn questions (why me? is this photographic memory of the ear? a mutation of entanglement? My father may have had it and not known it or at least had excellent relative pitch, is there epigenetics at play?). I get tested all the time - people put me in the corner of a room, play a pitch, I respond with correct nomenclature, people gasp and/or clap, then judgment/envy ensues. I used to be the pitch pipe in my college choir and gave every starting note. Trust me, may be a neat party trick, but it's not fun when it's done all the time and peers change from envy to anger because I'm trained to hear everything that's 'wrong' (moody, ego-maniacal artists getting called out = interesting rehearsals). Became very isolating as a young student at times. Expectations were almost unattainable with certain instructors and ensembles. You can probably see where this is heading now (chronic stress + perfectionism).

<iframe width="100%" height="450" scrolling="no" frameborder="no" src="[https://w.soundcloud.com/player/?url=https://api.soundcloud.com/tracks/40612485&amp;auto\_play=false&amp;hide\_related=false&amp;show\_comments=true&amp;show\_user=true&amp;show\_reposts=false&amp;visual=true](https://w.soundcloud.com/player/?url=https%3A//api.soundcloud.com/tracks/40612485&amp;auto_play=false&amp;hide_related=false&amp;show_comments=true&amp;show_user=true&amp;show_reposts=false&amp;visual=true)"></iframe>

Background on track linked - Wrote this at age 18, produced a 'mockup' a couple years ago when up to score a film (mockup - your pitch/waste of time to the Director/Audio Dept. of a film/tv show/video game; the days of playing your theme on the piano in a Warner Bros. exec's office before recording it to a live orchestra are mostly/sadly over, now everyone has to produce something with technology first). I'm sure you can spot a few major influences if you're a a film/tv soundtrack buff. Thanks for listening!

Linkin Park has been an interesting band with their progression/transition/philosophy of sound. One of their most underrated tracks is 'Robot Boy' off A Thousand Suns. ### Paul T. — 2015-05-12T23:05:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-164007>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=164002)
>
> I love lots of music but when I am CTing I have a few go too's. Breaking Benjamin So cold and Breath are two. Apocalytica is another go too.......as its Scott Davis piano renditions of metal music........crazy but it works.

'So Cold' - Boom. Drop C tuning (Drop CT Tuning? Ha!) in overdrive that hits hard on the down and upbeats - massive foundation in the mix, gives me a presence of an ancient power, that 'Anything is possible' feeling. All of those, especially Apocalyptica, make perfect sense. I owe a lot of my musicianship, particularly guitar, to Metallica. \\../ \\../

Huge connections between the metal and classical worlds. Huge.

There's a reason that works composed a thousand years ago are still performed and revered today, and it's not because they're easy to sing.

Palestrina's 'Missa Papae Marcelli' and a glass of red wine? Ultimate therapy in the classical/choral community.

Also, for therapy's and art's sake, I highly recommend getting into the non-accompanied (a'cappella) choral world, and I'm not talking about your little, nice 'church choir' singing 'Be Thou My Vision'. I'm talking deep/ancient/old school singing of a 45-minute concerto, splitting into 16 parts with Bass sections that go inhumanly low. It's truly remarkable, moving and badass. I'd kill to have Russian Basso Profundo's in my ensembles. I describe listening to choral music as activating a primordial level of listening - you're not listening for a huge drop, crash or dirty hook to quantize, you're actively listening to the environment and how 10, 20, 30 people can come together and form a unified sound that blows you to the back of a hall. I've heard a group of 6 professional singers shake cathedral sanctuaries. The 'mix' of the choir is truly epigenetics meets environment. Overtones galore. Recordings will never do justice, but they can come relatively close.

I've had epiphany after epiphany when listening to the highest level of choral singing live. It is truly rare to hear choirs these days to find such truth in a piece as difficult as Schnittke's 'Choir Concerto' (remember fear and the inability to be in the now? A plague across 21st Century musicianship)

Schnittke - Choir Concerto, Movement 1. Russian State Symphonic Cappella, Valerie Polyansky, Conductor

### Paul T. — 2015-05-17T05:18:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-164497>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=164368)
>
> Paul, have you tried listening to a choral piece under a particular colour of light (say purple...).

Interesting question, and nice to meet you on here! Thought about this for a while. I realized, subconsciously, that I may have. Not literally replacing light bulbs in between concerts/listening, but here are my thoughts:

Have a couple theories on why evening performances have a unique energy to them, or, at least, performances in a more natural environment (Churches, Outdoor Festivals (NOT Coachella), dimly lit orchestra hall).

Context - this is not about religion, this is about the history of music/singing, which, for thousands of years, was inseparable from religion (Masses were entirely sung for 1000+ years, most choral works before the 1920's have a religious text or at least religious/otherwordly context).

Epigenetics of musician (gotta have an angelic voice and incredible ear to be even remotely considered to sing for the church, at least before 20th/21st Centuries) + Timing and Environment - Morning and Evening prayer, particularly evening (Vespers) entirely sung + awe-inspiring stone, marble and glass cathedral ([http://spectraphonictable.com/?](http://spectraphonictable.com/?)) yields haunting acoustics + non-standardized tuning + many of the great cathedrals were built with the sanctuary facing east/west (8am - 11am and 5pm-7pm sunlight for Morning and Evening Prayer, yes they varied a bit depending on your point in history, changing from evening hour to sunset hour) perfectly piercing through openings in the roof and/or \[stained\] glass windows and permeates the entire sanctuary). Seems The Church had their own biohack (deliberately positioned healing sunlight + healing sound) and didn't even know it. Or maybe they did? We don't even have to worry about nnEMF here.

Anecdotally - I had the privelege of hearing Rachmaninov's Vespers - truly one of the greatest choral work ever written - in Tallinn, Estonia in 2004 sung by the Estonian Philharmonic Chamber Choir - one of the best professional choirs in the world. It was sung at sundown in a gorgeous cathedral in Downtown Tallinn. I'll never forget the performance. Still a very vivid memory. Hairs raised, goosebumps - truly moving. Not a peep out of the audience. They recorded it and produced an album (excerpt can be found below). When the Basso Profundo began the work, it rang throughout the entire sanctuary. The journey had started.

Basso profundo - unbelievably low-range basses in a choir. Some of them don't even sound human. They're a conductor's dream. Notorious in Eastern Europe/higher latitudes (Russia, Estonia). Ultimate magnetism from constant CT and DHA?

I have a lot of work to do now in the quantum world...

### Sue-UK — 2015-05-17T20:33:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-164539>

Hi Paul

I first became interested in the effects of sound when I went to my first meditation class, the man who ran it decided one evening to tone the chakras. Have to say I thought yeh right when I heard what he was about to do. I was laying down flat on my back and he started ….. He started at the top chakra, and the first tones…. nothing, Then he toned a deep tone and I found myself curled up in the foetal position. Lesson learned ......

A couple of years later I was taking a subtle energy course, and we had a guest lecturer who spent the day talking about sound, light, and the sacred space. How they built churches and cathedrals to the exact proportions to suit the voices of the monks etc…. walls as frozen music …. How they could influence the congregation by the use of stained glass., for example using purple stained glass to direct light onto the congregation but yellow light directed on the choir and clergy.

If I go on holiday I like to find local churches, when services aren’t being held, and see how they’ve created the space …. Sometimes it’s through the dimensions and acoustics, sometimes it’s through light, sometimes it’s both. If it’s right, although it is silent I can “feel” the acoustics, sometimes I can’t feel it but think it’s the wrong time of day, it might “feel” differently in the darkness with candlelight. Perhaps some places need the choir singing for it to be special, others have a specialness anyway.

My other thought is lyrics …. Another lecturer we had on the course talked about body prayer. He had re-translated passages from the Bible from the Aramaic, very different to the King James version. We read out the new translation as a body prayer, and then read out the Aramaic text. Very interesting how differently the revised words, and the different language, resonate through the body. So, like the music itself, perhaps a good lyric is one that moves from the ears/brain through the whole body. But then sometimes it could be that the person can't dissipate the sounds through the body..... their own instrument isn't working properly. When my health crashed I found I couldn’t listen to music, and lyrics were almost meaningless….it all became just noise in my head. I couldn't feel it in my bones any more (lack of DC current?)As my energy improves I do find myself humming more …. Might seem weird but as my skin improves I want to sing and hum more …. Perhaps melanin in the skin can convert photons into phonons …. (Well if DHA can convert light into a DC current and DC current into light its maybe not so far out …..).

I also love how different areas of a sacred building are in language…. A sacred place can in-spire, the alter(ed states of consciousness), en-trance me, a-tone-ment (or at-one-ment)…. Perhaps it’s a question of re-creating that either as a real place in which to play a recording, or to reach it as a state of mind first, an ability I have lost (hopefully temporarily).

I’m looking forward to the day I can appreciate music again …..

Thank you for sharing! Love what you've posted. Great to see people thinking this critically with music, and with lyrics/text as an addition? Awesome. Fluidity, fluidity, fluidity. Latin text in a Mass (such as the Kyrie, Gloria or Credo)? Fluidity. French chanson? Fluidity. Italian madrigal? Fluidity.

Why does English work so well in Top 40? Because it adapts to anything with a pulse.

A singer-songwriter that nailed that fluidity with Alt Top 40, lyrics, instrumentation/orchestration and a hauntingly beautiful voice? Jeff Buckley (Rest his soul).

### Paul T. — 2015-05-19T00:18:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-164645>

>

>

Pärt has a major influence on my choral works; he's expressed his own truth on paper (score) and in sound. Truly a remarkable composer, and he's famous and living. I mentioned I got to hear the Estonian Philharmonic Chamber Choir in Tallinn, Estonia which is where Pärt is from. That choir is basically his choir. They premiere, perform and record his works regularly. They actually performed at the Met Museum's Temple of Dendur with Pärt's 'Kanon Pokajanen' - a true exercise in minimalism and timeless music - with the composer in attendance.

[http://www.npr.org/event/music/3166...rt-live-from-the-met-museums-temple-of-dendur](http://www.npr.org/event/music/316654406/music-by-arvo-p-rt-live-from-the-met-museums-temple-of-dendur)

### Daina — 2015-06-04T18:49:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-166561>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=163644)
>
> Welcome Paul. I noticed that I could sing better fairly soon after I started eating more seafood.
> I like what you said about light and water, and music. Magnetism also equates to cold. Does music sound differently when it is cold?
>
> Like the title of this thread.

Hi Paul, welcome! I am a newbie too, but haven't gotten to making my profile pic and introduction. I play musical brass horns, not so much the last 10 years, but I remember in high school and college that there was nothing that felt better to me than a warm horn and a cold mouthpiece. Sometimes a cold horn is comforting too. Instruments produce a 'sharper' sound when they are cold. When blowing warm air into the horn, it flattens the sound. The embouchure (placement of the mouth and tightness of the lips can control sound), as well as the speed and rate of air traveling through an instrument(at least 70-90 mph). Modern horns have tuning slides to adjust to be in tune with the rest of the group, but the earliest versions of horns had no such devices (see bugle and hunting horn). Adjustments were made by the player using air capacity, embouchure, body, hands, muffles of cloth, etc. Just my two cents. I absolutely love reading about music and your observations of music and how it plays into science are a revelation for me. I also like that you added links to illustrate. I have sent this blog to my son, who is in the Navy, but wants to be a composer. Thanks again for sharing, you have an easy way about you and I can see why you would be successful at workshops; complex but understandable. Daina

### Lahelada — 2015-06-21T16:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-169141>

Applause ..."make the ear as vivid as the eye" love it!

Good morning Paul T . I was just going to say hi and see how you are doing as I just tagged you in my post and I find your statement 'make the ear as vivid as the eye"
I agree that we need to go away from the seeing (think tv ipod, advertising) but go back to really hearing. We have visual overload in our daily lives and modern music is often auditorial overload (wham bam thank you Ma'am school of music ) playing on just one frequency of emotion,reducing us but not expanding us . Sorry for rambling in your journal,some very undevelopped thoughts here but for what it's worth.

### Paul T. — 2015-06-22T23:42:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-169279>

> [Lahelada said:](/goto/post?id=169141)
>
> Applause ..."make the ear as vivid as the eye" love it!
>
> Good morning Paul T . I was just going to say hi and see how you are doing as I just tagged you in my post and I find your statement 'make the ear as vivid as the eye"
> I agree that we need to go away from the seeing (think tv ipod, advertising) but go back to really hearing. We have visual overload in our daily lives and modern music is often auditorial overload (wham bam thank you Ma'am school of music ) playing on just one frequency of emotion,reducing us but not expanding us . Sorry for rambling in your journal,some very undevelopped thoughts here but for what it's worth.

Love it. Thank you for sharing, and I 100% agree. There are two elements - one leading into the other - that I teach to students and professionals that I believe is the reason we don't actually 'listen' to music anymore: Fear (not 'I'm scared and nervous', but the inability to embrace and empathize true human emotion and condition because we live in a world of constant stimuli) and the inability to be in the now (with fear leading to the inability to be in the now). More on that later.

Here's another look into my world - this morning's listening session and score study: Vaughan Williams' 'Fantasia on a Theme of Thomas Tallis'

Master the environment - let the (antiphonal) strings truly breathe; let the body vibrate in such a great hall, where this piece was intended to be played. The fluidity of the hall's acoustic will reward your active listening.

Master the ego - embrace your fears, your anxieties, your darkest thoughts and channel them into the requisite energy and emotional intensity that is the music of Vaughan Williams - his work is a primordial connection.

Visually stunning; audibly sublime. Sir Andrew Davis. The BBC Symphony Orchestra. Gloucester Cathedral. No recording, no performance is ever perfect, nor do I ever use that word in music. This may be an exception. Life-changing performance. Take 17 minutes out of your day to do nothing but watch and listen. I guarantee you at 1:26, if you actively listen, something will change within you. @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) - if you love big strings, this piece should be right up your alley.

### Lahelada — 2015-06-23T13:58:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-169353>

Thanks Paul . I will save that for the weekend as a start to my two week vacation. You reminded me though of a performance in Waltham Abbey of the Tallis singers for the 800th anniversary of the abbey. It was magnificient and as it was october I think cold thermogenesis was included.  If you are ever going to England make a visit ,it is not very far from London 50 miles at best . magnificient when you catch a performance at the same time . I was there 30 years ago and still remember it fondly.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-06T04:16:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-171056>

**From FB tying music to magnetism:** "A teaching lesson about numbers: Numbers explain physics better via colors. colors is all about frequency. Colors for me and hopefully for this group is not about what we see it is about what they are.........packets of vibrational energy. Consider red light: has a frequency of 436,803,079,680,000 oscillations per second. That equates to the same energy in a high C note in music. That note has an oscillation frequency of 4,096 osc. per sec. Xrays, microwaves, cosmic and gamma rays all have these numbers and colors tied to them.........but when you read them they make no sense to you........but color has a qualia you do get. Your grandma's book.........the pic has meaning.........to you. You know what it means to me? Magnetism has the HIGHEST oscillatory rate we know of in nature. Few people know this. It is 18,446,744,073,709,551,616 oscillations per second.........IT is hard for the mind to grasp that many things vibrating that many times in a second........But guess what.......last week on the cruise.....everyone else did there own thing in Playa Del Carmen on the cruise........and What did I make sure to do? I went into tthe crater where those Oscillations have existed for 65 million yrs.........Patrick asked me how I expended all the energy I did every day and night on the cruise.......and not get tired. I remained ketotic and at once and day and drank a ton of water........why? Because that water assimilated all those oscillations from the crater to renew me.........I knew I could give a lot.......because I was going to get a lot more in my time in the crater. For those of you bad at math: That number above is 18 quintillion, 446 quadrillion, 744 trillion, 73 billion, 709 million, 551 thousand, 6 hundred, and 16 oscillations per second of vibrational energy. That my dear is physics 101. Get some"

### JoeBranca — 2015-07-27T21:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-173250>

> [Paul T. said:](/goto/post?id=164007)
>
> Also, for therapy's and art's sake, I highly recommend getting into the non-accompanied (a'cappella) choral world, and I'm not talking about your little, nice 'church choir' singing 'Be Thou My Vision'. I'm talking deep/ancient/old school singing of a 45-minute concerto, splitting into 16 parts with Bass sections that go inhumanly low. It's truly remarkable, moving and badass. I'd kill to have Russian Basso Profundo's in my ensembles. I describe listening to choral music as activating a primordial level of listening - you're not listening for a huge drop, crash or dirty hook to quantize, you're actively listening to the environment and how 10, 20, 30 people can come together and form a unified sound that blows you to the back of a hall. I've heard a group of 6 professional singers shake cathedral sanctuaries. The 'mix' of the choir is truly epigenetics meets environment. Overtones galore. Recordings will never do justice, but they can come relatively close.

Great stuff, Paul. Years ago I dipped my toes into the more weighty a cappella's with an album 'Images of Christ', which is John Rutter and the Cambridge Singers

[http://www.amazon.com/Images-Christ-Edward-Cuthbert-Bairstow/dp/B0000031I4/](http://www.amazon.com/Images-Christ-Edward-Cuthbert-Bairstow/dp/B0000031I4/)

By far, the shining piece in there is Rachmaninov's Vo tsarstvii Tvoyem (The Beatitudes)

I need to find more! The only other a cappella I currently have are albums by the Bulgarian Women's Choir - quite different. And John Taverner's Funeral Canticle based on how Malick implemented it in The Tree of Life.

I just ordered the Estonian performance of All-night Vigil based on the clip and your reflections. Can't wait to sink my ears in

### Paul T. — 2015-11-18T19:22:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-composer-who-sold-his-ipod.14402/post-182266>

A (quantum) mantra that I teach ensembles and my students about the art of interpretation:

Context is Key; Melody is King.
Intelligence is Key; Intuition is King.
Confidence is Key; Resilience is King.

# Thread: At what point do you stop living in fear and begin to live?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-what-point-do-you-stop-living-in-fear-and-begin-to-live.14425/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-09T17:35:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-what-point-do-you-stop-living-in-fear-and-begin-to-live.14425/post-163707>

-   Isn't every moment a choice, a new reality awaiting?

-   Transformation occurs when you begin to think divergently.............

-   Disagreement is now harmony to me. Disagreement by itself is proof life is an open system. We need conflict to maintain............the recycling of life. Conservation without evolution is extinction. Evolution without conservation is insanity. This is the most critical characteristic of biologic evolution and our ecosystem.
    Ten yrs ago my thinking was controlled by "my society" I allowed to infiltrate me. No longer. Nature controls me now. She consumes me.

    The control she has over me, however, is weak.............but she controls me and guides through the collisions I need to have with the right amount of light.
    Don't copy anyone............build something new that is you. Exemplars: an admired person or thing that is considered an example that deserves to be copied. Nothing should be copied because in an open system everything that is a copy still is unique. Agreement is not required.
    Different roads sometimes lead to the same castle when you know you're right.........the key is showing others you are.

    This is how paradigms crumble. Copying is an exemplar move. Rhyming is the more accurate way of nature. When she copies she always adds some new twist because the environment requires it. No two environments remain the same because all things in the universe are moving.

### CTforlife — 2016-06-30T09:15:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-what-point-do-you-stop-living-in-fear-and-begin-to-live.14425/post-197559>

I chose to stop living in fear the minute I had the sudden realization that I was in control of my whole life. Words cannot and never will describe the amount of love you have gave to me to overcome my fears. My life's epiphany is that someone, just about anyone, really, can change the world, if they gives themselves the power to do so. I think we both know where this power originally comes from. I always have and always will be in dept my entire life to you. At times I felt powerless and now I'm powerful,and it is only because of the love and education that made me dig deep, think harder, and realize I am in control. Single handedly, you sir, have changed the course of human trajectory forever and ever. I will speak for the people of earth that will be forever touched by your quantum entanglement, that we are grateful you shared your thoughts with all of us. Your thoughts created actions that changed the dna of the human species that will live on way longer than me or you will. Hopefully that dna sparks some thoughts that propels us past where we are now. But until then I'm truly blessed to have crossed paths with you, even on the internet. I can confidently say you breathed life into my body. You gave me the knowledge of the sun, which gave me everything. I love you Jack! At some point in the future I dream of humans that are no longer viruses to the planet, humans that have the knowledge to create better lives for every single organism on the planet. We can and will live in harmony in the future, with the knowledge that we didn't have in the past. We may have been healthier in the past, but now as a species, because of you, we will be wiser in the future, and this will lead to better tomorrow's for all, not just humans.

# Thread: Uncoupled From Light: Complete inability to sleep linked to gut issues

**Subforum:** Beginners Area

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jacoby — 2015-05-09T18:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-163711>

> There is a lot of context here and I am greatly appreciative to anyone who takes the time to read through this. I’ve read some things by Dr. Kruse before and thought it was interesting but never really dived in.... it’s only been in the last few days (ubiquitination12) where I’ve read some blog posts and started to connect some dots with my difficult health situation.

Background on me: early 30s, chronic insomnia 14 years, IBS most of life, impaired glucose tolerance, Insulin Resistance, peripheral neuropathy, cold hands and feet..... fit, slightly underweight. Normal fasting glucose is in the high 90s, but I keep it suppressed with cinnamon, curcumin, inositol.

Diet: I eat a mostly paleo diet with the exception of rice, mainly because I crave carbs and have huge appetite (leptin resistance? And also yogurt because I noticed years ago I slept better with it.

At the core, my biggest issue that I must solve is the **complete inability to sleep**. It has happened in 3-4 day stretches 6 times since February when I got some type of bad cold/flu. It happened one other time, a 4- day stretch last July after 16 days on an autoimmune paleo diet, limiting carbs to around 100g a day (4)

It’s also improved to 1-2 day stretches lately as I have figured out that **treating my gut with certain probiotics (L. Rhamnosus from yogurt)** and sups for anxiety (theanine, bacopa, niacinamide) help get me back to my normal 4-5.5 hours. I’m an insomnia vet, every single night for many many years…..but that has always been the **inability to stay asleep**, for the most part….**this new thing is clearly something else** as I turn off the **lights I don’t feel anything in relation to the darkness**, it feels really strange…sometimes my heart starts racing out of nowhere, and my mind follows shortly after. Neurologist did an MRI looking for a brain lesion (FFI) but it was negative. It doesn't fit the presentation of mania and I no longer freak out about it...I've kind of accepted it, but I am not sure how much more my heart can take.

> **Major Event #1**
> The pretext is that I’ve had chronic insomnia for nearly 15 years, first developed in high school when I lost metabolic homeostasis under stressful, anxious circumstances, and had a very sugar rich diet, went long periods without real food.
>
>
> This essentially caused my hands and feet to go cold, anxiety to get worse, and started incredible unending hunger…it also marked the start for the first time in my life of sleeping problems.

> **Major Event #2 Urine Therapy**
>
> Now, a significant turning point happened 6 years ago, in a desperate naïve attempt to improve my long term stomach issues and insomnia, metabolic issues…..I resorted to salt water flushes and then the big one, drinking my own urine…yes, I know breathtakingly stupid, but at the time I had limited function and could not understand why I could only sleep 4 hours a night no matter what and only go to the bathroom 2-3 times a week, and have to eat 6 times a day.
>
> What happened almost immediately after the urine thing is my fat digestion changed and I also had severe cramping in my back, in many different spots, especially the flank. My BMs changed and started floating and I noticed it correlated with fat intake somewhat. I took every electrolyte I could thinking I caused an imbalance. It eventually resolved (mostly) but with a permanent flank pain that can be felt when I twist my torso. More pronounced on left side. My stamina exercising went from incredible to nonexistent. I felt like I had no air….I mean dramatic shift.
>
> I attributed this to the fact that my cellular hydration changed. I used to drink 2 glasses of water 2 hours before exercise and feel like I could run all day. After the experimenting, I couldn’t pump enough water at any time. My sleep also became more erratic.

**Getting help**
Fast forward from 2009 to 2012 and I finally had the means to get medical care and get everything checked out- nothing really stood out to me, other than CBC, low wbc and platelets. Ultrasound of abdomen showed nothing, Colonoscopy was unremarkable. What is interesting is a few months after the Colonoscopy my BM went from floating/loose to extreme constipation in the form of pellets. A complete 180. Again, I noticed it was correlating with fat intake. I began using more yogurt and artichokes and magnesium and that helped mask it somewhat if I limited fat.

> Gut Diversity
>
> I got my got sequenced in 2013 and it showed remarkably low gut diversity (pic).
> Dr. Kruse discuses this is ubiquitnation12, which I will need to re-read many times to remotely understand. What's confusing to me on the surface level is that I have an abundance of firmicutes yet seemingly can no longer digest fat. The big question I have is what did the urine (ammonia?) (bacteria colonies) due to my gut and can I reverse it?

(4) After reading I attributed this to a sharp change in the gut microbiome (higher carb to low carb) and reading about the importance of carbs in shuttling tryptophan into the brain for conversion to serotonin. It took a couple of days of higher carb to come out of this swing, I was also heavily supplementing, fish oil, b vitamins, mct oil. The lone difference between this period and the recent ongoing ones, is I would start to fall asleep here and then an adrenaline surge would kick in, heart and mind racing, eyes bolting open.

I have begun eating fish every day, canned sardines and salmon. I am doing morning walks and eating mostly protein first meal. I’ve started cold showers based on the CT rx, and I didn’t think I could as I run chronically cold, have heater on when it’s 70 degrees- also something that worse after UT.

Supps currently on: 450 mg theanine, 300-600mg pantathine, 570mg dha with meal with fat, niacinamide, glycine, 400-600mg magnesium. And I recently stopped bacopa as it was giving me vague testicle pain.

Most sups I want to take give me an energy boost and cause me to not sleep, ie 6mg of caffeine early in the morning in the form of dark chocolate, coQ10, ubiquinol, iodine, ALC, chlorella, and on and on. That's why I am desperate, because every direction I turn I run into a snare, like you wouldn't believe.

Adrenal Stress Index? Okay, I've been thinking about looking at my cortisol for a while, just not sure it was worth it as I know my cortisol is high at night.
I haven't heard of the salivary melatonin lab, will check it out. I do not respond well to melatonin at any dose, I've experimented a lot over the years and it's much more likely to give me a bad night of sleep.

I got a sleep study last month, I have the 4-page report right here. This was on one of my normal nights and I had light sleep for about 6 hours, with 40 awakenings and 65 arousals. It felt like I didn't really sleep. Majority of time was spent in stage 2. Average oxygen saturation 96%. I don't see any alpha wave measurements but I will call them and see.

This was like one of my bad **regular** nights, this new thing is **weird** and in many ways totally different: there is no sleep pressure, even after not sleeping 2-3 days. When I am in my normal state, if I have a bad night and only sleep 2-3 hours, I am fatigued as hell and usually rebound. There is no rebound with this thing. It's like day/night feel the same.

Thank you very much.

### kovita — 2015-05-10T07:30:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-163753>

You say nothing about wherre do you live and what do you do for living. My best recommendation is go on and clean your environment from blue light and nnEMf as much as you can. If you live in the city, start to explore your options to go to countryside. Give it a try and make a holiday sleeping in the countryside ideally outdoors on the earth, see what happens to your sleep. You have a long history of health problems, this will unlikely clear overnight, but you stay great chances to transform your health in mid-longterm range (think about years). You should adjust your lifestyle to epipaleo rules - DHA, timing of meals, light, contact woht nature, CT.... It is pretty much what everyone her does or did for his/her health and it pretty much helps and improves all peoples different health issues and/or goals.

You are not alone. There are millions of people who have exactly the same issues like you, it may feel you are the only and it is unfair, but you should never think this way, beacuse this will prevent your progress. You should look at you at as someone very special and accept the challange in it's wholeness and don't give up on the way. This will eventually convert you into the one who went back to live and joy and the others to stay waiting for salvataion in the old lives. Very valuable personla lesson I got here as I turned completely every stone in my life.

Your sleep study is suggestive HRV will be trashed pretty much. High cortisol at wrong times suggest the same. most of the poeple have poor to almost nonexistent parasympat. activity on their HRVs nowadays, even children, it looks we continuously live on sympatit. This is clearly connected to the environment, light and EMF. There are devices for parasympathetic stimulation available amd work pretty well for very lost cases, but you can do the same creating new lifestyle and mindset. I have excellent HRV now, fantastic sleep HRV, young adult profile...there were no deep phases a year back (lyme changess dramatically serotonin metabolism - my case).

You say most of the supps give you even worse sleep, including CoEnQ10. Jack already told you here your redox must be very low. With decent redox CoEnQ10 with bpp at night will improve your sleep. The same could be said about going very low carb - if your redox sucks you will not sleep well. You say you already started to change your life, so hang up on there and be patient! I am pretty sure you will see results however uncer

My health has taken a turn for the worse and I'm wondering if anyone can give me some hints. A few weeks ago, my face started twitching uncontrollably as I was trying to go to sleep, it lasted 2 nights and has not stopped, it's all over my body....went to neuro, had brisk reflexes, no babinski, but left biceps abnormal, mild cross abductor....so ALS is now in the picture and a lot of things are pointing in that direction.

I want to throw myself in ketosis as a last ditch attempt, but I failed miserably to get into ketosis last year, zero carbs means zero sleep and 30-50g a day of carb was seemingly too much to enter.

Mitochondrial support, CT, ketosis? Is there anything I should be looking more closely at?

### Penny — 2015-06-02T23:54:31+0300

A few ideas...

The Berberines... Oregon Grape Root - 1 after every meal - truly the stuff is awesome on so many levels - including killing bugs which might wake up and play when you're trying to sleep... It raises superoxide, regulates glucose -
[http://www.life-enhancement.com/magazine/article/2901-the-berberine-story-gets-better-and-better](http://www.life-enhancement.com/magazine/article/2901-the-berberine-story-gets-better-and-better)

Epsom Salt baths before bed - stay in hot water for 20 minutes - don't take baths? Try a foot soak... Then, take an ice pack to bed and read under a red incandescant light bulb - truly, the red light puts me to sleep faster than the dark ever did...

Take one rhodiola in the morning for energy ... I have seen people brought from the brink with the stuff - especially people who live on the computer...
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19170145](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19170145)
[http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light)

You also might add P5P before bed - but there might be an issue using it together with a berberine so research it -

The full moon is when bugs replicate so diatomaceous earth might be a hot idea too -

### Jacoby — 2015-06-10T20:53:33+0300

Starting to get a lot of lab work back...

Calcium is at 10.4 - range 8.8-10.2 - up from 9.9 a few months ago. This worries me the most as I learn about calcium homeostasis from Jack's writings.
BUN is at 21, and CREAT is at 1.08, giving me a horrifying 1:21 ratio. (I have only drunk RO water for years, btw)
Albumin is at 5.2, a tad over the range.

Another abnormals:

Neutrophil % is low
Eosinophil % is high
Neut# is low
Eosinophil Count Abs is high

WBC 3.3
Platelets 111

These last 2 have been abnormal for at least 3 years.

I'm currently killing the lights very early, using a red bulb in my room, but still having the complete inability to sleep on many nights. What happens is I will start to doze off, but not quite, and it's like if I don't catch it at just the right time, I'm re-booted and alert for good. Also, it's very clear if I don't get the right amount of carbs (serotonin?) and probiotics (gut inflammation) on any given day, I have no chance. This conflicts as I am trying to do the Leptin RX and end the absurd carb/hunger cravings.

What happens is I will start to doze off, but not quite, and it's like if I don't catch it at just the right time, I'm re-booted and alert for good. Also, it's very clear if I don't get the right amount of carbs (serotonin?) and probiotics (gut inflammation) on any given day, I have no chance. This conflicts as I am trying to do the Leptin RX and end the absurd carb/hunger cravings.

Jacoby, it doesn't matter where one's starting place is, it's the persistence and grit we use that will bring success. You're so lucky to be in the only place on the net that can really help you reverse engineer your health to optimal. Sure, for some of us it's a long journey but reversal does happen.
Keep at it looking forward to watching your progress(and that always means ups and downs).

### Jacoby — 2015-07-25T17:11:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-172974>

Update

EMG turned out clean, I don’t have ALS. My excessive muscle twitches, vibrations can’t yet be explained. Dehydration on cellular level? This correlated with my calcium and BUN rising (BUN was already rising though)

I am sleeping better with the addition of many new things but still experiencing these sleepless episodes triggered by the dark.

-   I asked about the alpha waves voltage and my doctor pulled up the study and plotted a few points and it showed 25 or 26 microvolts? At each point (Alpha Waves)

-   Looking at doing the salivary melatonin but need to wait a few more weeks because I read from you that you need to have been off melatonin sups for 2 months- I was experimenting with melatonin a bit in May and early June.

-   I have been busy the last few months selling everything I own just about and purchasing a mild HBOT chamber and core magnetico. I have also been on NAC the last 5-6 weeks, 600-1200mg in the morning with protein/fat breakfast. I'm doing about 8 hours a week in the HBOT right now. I want to do 2 hours a day.

-   The good news is I have put together some really good days, in fact, a few weeks ago I had a breakthrough for 2 days, where I fell asleep normally, woke up 2 hours later, but astonishingly and calmly fell back asleep for another 4 hours, before sleeping again for another 60-70 minutes. This is unheard of.

-   I’m keeping track of what I do, but perhaps not as detailed as I need to be, as it was likely some combination of HBOT, NAC, and possibly a turmeric supplement, that gave me these results. I have been unable to replicate but I have been better off than before.

-   I still fall into these episodes, and they are very distinct….I know that the yogurt / probiotic regiment that I am on is the #1 factor of importance. If I don’t get enough of this particular type of yogurt, or the yogurt has been opened in the fridge for a while I will have this absurd reaction, where I am about to go to sleep and the the brain just slams ON in the dark.

-   I am now beginning to look at a correlation with Fat- it already correlates with my chronic constipation, and now I’m beginning to suspect it playing a role in these episodes- I’ve seen some studies on high fat diets and messed up gut biomes. I was trying to get myself into ketosis for a month last fall before this thing started.

Still taking cold showers, building up for winter and attempting cold thermogenesis. Don't have bathtub or ice machine, but I might have to try compression shirt and buying ice somewhere.

Looking at doing Saunas (heat shock proteins, protein folding correctly) or Cryo therapy (electron boost, cold therm adaption)

### Penny — 2015-08-25T01:55:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-175585>

**[http://microbe.net/2015/04/27/gut-check-the-ecology-of-keeping-time/](http://microbe.net/2015/04/27/gut-check-the-ecology-of-keeping-time/)**

**Gut check: the ecology of keeping time**
[April 27, 2015](http://microbe.net/2015/04/27/gut-check-the-ecology-of-keeping-time/) [Sean Gibbons](http://microbe.net/author/sean-gibbons/) [Bioinformatics](http://microbe.net/category/major-topics/bioinformatics-2/), [DNA sequencing](http://microbe.net/category/major-topics/dna-sequencing/), [New Papers](http://microbe.net/category/current-topics/new-papers/), [News](http://microbe.net/category/current-topics/news/), [Research](http://microbe.net/category/major-topics/research-2/)
There are few constants in this world. One exception, however, is the passing of day to night, which has gone on without fail since life first emerged on Earth. Early life quickly learned to anticipate changes associated with light and dark. This ability to tell time – to peer into the immediate future – was an enormous advantage. As a result, from bacteria to buttercups, many organisms have evolved biological clocks in sync with the spinning of the globe.

Humans are no different. Our light-driven clock is linked to the central nervous system, and orchestrates the production of a number of proteins that influence our behavior. This internal clock is coupled to a separate clock in the liver, which governs metabolism during sleep and wakefulness. When people are jet-lagged, or start working nights, this disruption to sleep can lead to weight gain due to a decoupling of these two clocks ([McHill et al., 2014](http://www.pnas.org/content/111/48/17302.short)).

The function of our internal timepiece does not stop with our own bodies. The ticking of our clocks also depends on the microbes that live in and on us. The genes bound up in the complex assemblage of single-celled organisms living in our nooks and crannies outnumber the genes in our own genome by a hundred fold. We have come to rely on this reservoir of alien genes to help us thwart disease, digest our food, and carry out many other day-to-day tasks that maintain our health and well being.

Recently, scientists at the Weizmann Institute found that the gut microbiome in mammals (like us) reconfigures itself on a day-night cycle ([Thaiss et al., 2014](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867414012367)). They found that the lack of a good night’s sleep upset this daily cycle of gut microbes. Thaiss then transplanted the muddled microbiome from sleep-deprived mice into healthy mice on a normal sleep schedule, which upset the internal clock and led to weight-gain. Thus, jet-lag was transmissible via the microbiome. In a follow-up study, another group of researchers at the Salk Institute found that diet-induced obesity had an equivalent effect on biological clock function as sleep disruption ([Zarrinpar et al., 2014](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1550413114005051)). In both sets of mice – those deprived of sleep or those fed on a high-fat diet – the outcome was the same: the clocks in the nervous system and the liver decoupled and the mice got fat.

Last week, in the journal _Cell Host & Microbe_, [Vanessa Leone](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Vanessa_Leone2) and [Eugene Chang](http://www.uchospitals.edu/physicians/eugene-chang.html), at the University of Chicago, identified _how_ a healthy gut microbiome interacts with the mammalian clock ([Leone et al., 2015](http://www.cell.com/cell-host-microbe/abstract/S1931-3128%2815%2900123-7)). They found that the daily cycling of a particular set of bacteria was disrupted by a high-fat diet, along with the cycling of small molecules known as short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs). Mammals cannot make SCFAs, and must rely on their gut microbiota to produce them. Leone and her colleagues found that a particular SCFA – butyrate – rose and fell in abundance over the course of the day, along with the microbes that produced it. She then injected butyrate into sick mice with dysfunctional clocks – at the correct time of day – and brought them back to health. These findings display how intimately our metabolism is connected to our resident microbes and may eventually lead to new microbiome-related treatments for people with metabolic disorders associated with obesity (e.g., diabetes).

We are just starting to understand how intertwined we are with our microbiota. Unfortunately, we have not been gracious hosts. We have altered our diets and lifestyles dramatically in recent millennia. We have also introduced broad-spectrum antibiotics to treat disease. These advances have saved countless lives and extended our lifespans, but have eroded our microbial partnerships. We can see the fallout in the prevalence of autoimmune diseases and obesity in industrialized nations. We must recon with the fact that our bodies are ecosystems, just like a rainforest or a coral reef. Medicine in the 21st century should take a more ecological approach to healthcare, where disease is not always caused by a single bad actor, but by an imbalance in our internal ecosystem.

### Penny — 2015-08-25T01:57:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-175586>

**The type and quantity of dietary fat and carbohydrate alter faecal microbiome and short-chain fatty acid excretion in a metabolic syndrome ‘at-risk’ population**
F Fava, R Gitau, B A Griffin, G R Gibson, K M Tuohy and J A Lovegrove

**Abstract**
INTRODUCTION AND OBJECTIVES:

An obese-type human microbiota with an increased Firmicutes:Bacteroidetes ratio has been described that may link the gut microbiome with obesity and metabolic syndrome (MetS) development. Dietary fat and carbohydrate are modifiable risk factors that may impact on MetS by altering the human microbiome composition. We determined the effect of the amount and type of dietary fat and carbohydrate on faecal bacteria and short chain fatty acid (SCFA) concentrations in people ‘at risk’ of MetS.

DESIGN:

A total of 88 subjects at increased MetS risk were fed a high saturated fat diet (HS) for 4 weeks (baseline), then randomised onto one of the five experimental diets for 24 weeks: HS; high monounsaturated fat (MUFA)/high glycemic index (GI) (HM/HGI); high MUFA/low GI (HM/LGI); high carbohydrate (CHO)/high GI (HC/HGI); and high CHO/low GI (HC/LGI). Dietary intakes, MetS biomarkers, faecal bacteriology and SCFA concentrations were monitored.

RESULTS:

High MUFA diets did not affect individual bacterial population numbers but reduced total bacteria and plasma total and LDL-cholesterol. The low fat, HC diets increased faecal Bifidobacterium (P=0.005, for HC/HGI; P=0.052, for HC/LGI) and reduced fasting glucose and cholesterol compared to baseline. HC/HGI also increased faecal Bacteroides (P=0.038), whereas HC/LGI and HS increased Faecalibacterium prausnitzii (P=0.022 for HC/HGI and P=0.018, for HS). Importantly, changes in faecal Bacteroides numbers correlated inversely with body weight (r=−0.64). A total bacteria reduction was observed for high fat diets HM/HGI and HM/LGI (P=0.023 and P=0.005, respectively) and HS increased faecal SCFA concentrations (P<0.01).

CONCLUSION:

This study provides new evidence from a large-scale dietary intervention study that HC diets, irrespective of GI, can modulate human faecal saccharolytic bacteria, including bacteroides and bifidobacteria. Conversely, high fat diets reduced bacterial numbers, and in the HS diet, increased excretion of SCFA, which may suggest a compensatory mechanism to eliminate excess dietary energy.

**Control of decoherence with no control**

[http://www.nature.com/articles/srep02746?WT.ec\_id=SREP-20131001](http://www.nature.com/articles/srep02746?WT.ec_id=SREP-20131001)

“Noise is a source of disorder. White noise, whose spectrum has equal power within any equal interval of frequencies, is the extreme of disorder (in comparison with coloured noise). Over a decade ago, people began to notice in classical systems that noise leads not only to nuisance but also to advantages. A remarkable example is that an external coloured noise can suppress the intrinsic white noise”

White noise = full spectrum light

Colored noise = blue light

### Jacoby — 2015-09-01T01:49:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/uncoupled-from-light-complete-inability-to-sleep-linked-to-gut-issues.14426/post-176135>

I appreciate all the helpful tips and insights. Thank you everyone.

In the last month I started a low fat diet, around 30g a day, as well as 150mg Magnesium Threonate (thanks Jack) along with doing some other things. Sleep has improved, chronic constipation is gone, and chronic cramping in hand and foot is gone.

I have found that consuming the yogurt with rhamnosus in it works best if I have it earlier in the day. I can't have it for breakfast, only protein, because I have been on leptin reset for a while. The yogurt works better than probiotics with rhamnosus in pill form. I have tried a couple because I don't like consuming dairy. Still searching.

I have taken 10 different magnesium supplements and never noted anything special, other than some pushing things through faster. This Threonate is special.

While I have gotten this under control as of late and been sleeping 5-6 hours most nights, (it's been incredible) my muscle twitches have increased and are essentially non-stop all over my body. I don't know if this is from the low-fat diet and developing ALS or what. I am still concerned that my Vit. D levels are dropping and my calcium was rising, last I checked.

Jack,

Can I take 2500iu a day, as well as get as much sunlight as I can, and not worry about my calcium levels rising further? I'm looking at papers like
[**Calcium: the darth vader of ALS**](http://www.researchgate.net/publication/11876261_Calcium_the_darth_vader_of_ALS)

**and I'm curious because these twiches increasing big time seem to correspond exactly with the serum calcium increases (mild hypercalcemia), parathyroid normal.**

Hi,

My options are:

\-"normal" bedroom on third floor (far from ground)

\-laundry room in basement that likely has mold (I can be grounded onto slab)

\- computer room in basement (finished, probably not moldy, higher emf, within 20 ft of wifi router)

\- back yard (pollen kept making me wake up with symptoms and very dense stuffy nose)

I went from my normal room, to the laundry room, to the back yard, and back to my normal room because of the pollen. Would I be getting more benefit by just dealing with the allergies in the yard for now? Or will the immune activation slow me down a lot (it sure makes me suffer).

Any ideas on what might be best while working towards optimal (for someone with high cortisol, overactive immune system, leaky gut, imbalanced gut flora, etc.)

Thanks,

\- Matt

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-14T20:18:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-is-the-better-sleep-spot.14432/post-164273>

lower to ground best.......especially at sea level

When you go higher away from Earth’s core, even by an inch, gravity, and the Delbrück effect on light, becomes more magnified. This is why elevation in a microwaved world is a really bad idea. It is also why living in a high rise, flying frequently, being a pilot, or an astronaut is a horrible idea for your biology.

In 2010 scientists at the National Institute of Standards and Technology went further in proving my insights might be spot on. Their experiments showed that just at 1 foot elevation, a clock ticks four-hundreth-quadrillionth faster per second. I would remind you all most humans stand erect and are 5-6 feet tall. Now we have proof why the SCN must run faster than organ clocks. Physics proves it. This relationship cements why any altitude is deadly for biology.

Well... pathogenic bacteria.. IDK  I am not afraid of it.... Just keep the meat exposed to air (I keep mine on a wooden plate in the fridge, uncovered), and you will be fine, and get high quality meat like grassfed.
You will be fine

It is so convenient to eat stuff raw. I sure enjoy a good rare ribeye steak once in a while... and some lightly cooked fish here and there but seldom. I mostly eat all raw because it is so easy....lol Super fast, super delicious, and good for me  I use lots of ACV on my raw seafood, and sometimes on beef I use it too, or balsamic vinegar (natural organic ones without sugar). It is super delicious!
If I feel like eating something cooked I do that and digest it just fine for sure. Yesterday I had lots grassfed duck... baked in the oven... that was yummy too
and one day my boss offered me her home made meatballs as I was working in their home and they even had flour in them but I ate them anyways because it made her so happy and I had no issues at all, that made me happy too for sure!
My belly is super kind to me even if I feed it weird stuff all the time..lol it digest about anything
But I get cravings for raw animal food if I have cooked fish or meat for more than one meal.. go figure! My body demands it and my taste buds too

Today I had raw herring, 2 big oysters, raw salmon, raw grassfed organic butter, raw egg yolks, some raw organic salami, some raw grassfed beef and a smoothie with nettles and seaweed

and a raw cacao drink.. I made it with raw organic cacao powder, added body temp water, and mixed it with a tiny amount of stevia (do not normally use this) and raw butter and raw coconut oil
yummy
