### Psyche — 2015-07-06T20:12:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-is-the-better-sleep-spot.14432/post-171164>

I have the same question, essentially as Matty\_M and would love some feedback regarding best sleeping place. My current context:

I rent an older (1950's?) house in a rural area 50 miles east of Asheville, NC (Rutherfordton, NC). Closest neighbors are 2/10 mile either way. Street is on a mild ridge line compared with surroundings.

I feel like I have a ridiculous amount of EMF exposure for being in a rural area!

EMF Sources:
Outside of the house- Smart meter on one corner of the house. Above it on the roof is the satellite for internet (no cable offered on my street). There is an antenna on top of the house and I have no idea why. DirecTV satellite in yard (next to my bedroom). There are power lines running across my yard, connecting to the house. The transformer (assuming it's for my house) is across the street. Also- I used the web to find out there are 9 towers and 63 antennae's within a 3-mile radius of my address. Even though I have no close neighbors (5 houses on a 2-mile stretch of road) all the houses have satellite dishes.

Inside the house- microwave, wi-fi router, 3 LED TV's, laptop computer.

Currently, I sleep in my bedroom with everything unplugged and darkened. Smart meter is located at opposite end of house. Bedroom is above the basement.
When the sun goes down, my blue blockers go on and my little cave goes dark. I stay in there until I go to sleep. I sleep with a black lab doggie, too)

My question is, where is the best place to sleep given this scenario? I would LOVE to sleep outside in my yard in a tent (perhaps taking out the plastic bottom), but I'm wondering if it would be an improvement given all the EMF generating stuff around my house.

Comments?

Not through supplementation, but from the sun. For instance, we're planning our move south. Based on Jack's latest response to me, I'm assuming getting more vitamin D during the night is going to help me improve (along with CT, lots of seafood, etc, etc) and finally get my health back. So, how do I know I'd be getting enough vitamin D in northern FL?

Do we know how much vitamin D we can get from the sun in different parts of the world? Obviously the sun won't be as strong over winter, but how do I figure all of this out?!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-11T19:08:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-we-monitor-vitamin-d-intake.14442/post-163842>

tibial compression test of a blood test

### lookingforanswers — 2015-05-11T19:18:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-we-monitor-vitamin-d-intake.14442/post-163846>

Awesome. Thank you. I just did a "self" tibial compression test and passed. Something tells me that over the winter my shins were sore though . . . hmmm, more evidence that the sun this summer is working it's magic

My original question, though, was more about the ability of the sun to give me enough vitamin D based on location. I guess I don't quite know how to ask, but obviously the sun in PA is not strong enough over the winter to give me vitamin D. How do I figure out how far south one needs to go over the winter months to get the same vitamin D from the summer sun in PA? Does that make sense? I hope I'm asking it correctly.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-11T19:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-we-monitor-vitamin-d-intake.14442/post-163854>

how your redox look over the yr gives a good clue.

# Thread: Are carbs ever beneficial while working towards optimal?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Matty_M — 2015-05-12T18:58:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-163981>

My journal describes my context- im a male, 15, with
\- high cortisol (based on labs which are in lab section)
\- leaky gut I think (stuffy nose; itchy throat, eyes, etc. from allergenic foods and pollen, dust, etc.)
\- flora imbalance (constipation/gas/some gerd symptoms) - a genova diagnostic showed a parasite and I have since taken an antibiotic with functional medicine doctor
\- carb cravings (almost gone I think)
\- bad memory, few/no dreams (?)
\- wake up fairly exhausted after plenty (8-9 hours) of sleep (slowly improving while sleeping outside)
\- I still get a little tired after not eating for a bit, but do not feel hungry

ALL _improving_ with these interventions:

Diet:
\- I've been 90% epi-paleo for about 7 months (on and off paleo for a year before that- due to carb binging...)
\- I finally made it through the Leptin Rx in the last 6 weeks after significantly upping fat- like 1 stick of butter daily at peak, which has decreased a lot now (I've noticed more energy and less carb cravings)
\- Currently eating epi-paleo + Leptin Rx + autoimmune protocol for allergies
\- I take the supplements for hypercortisolism (l-carnitine, b-complex, d-ribose, ubiquinol, mg), most of those in leaky gut rx (coconut oil, probiotics, mg, zn, ubiquinol, bone broth), betain HCl w/pepsin, l-glutamine
\- I do CT daily (recently around 30 minutes at 60 F- looking to get a mini ice machine which can make 30 pounds of ice daily)
\- I ground a ton and get sunlight at the same time (outdoor desk)
\- I wear my red laser goggles at night so the only light I get is red and some yellow, or I just avoid light altogether
\- I have been trying to spend more time doing things I like to increase dopamine
\- I'm getting a tent to sleep in my backyard (and could afford a magnetico if it would be beneficial while already sleeping on the ground outdoors)

I'm just wondering whether including some seasonal carbs might have a benefit? I'm obviously young, not overweight at all, and I think I've become leptin sensitive. If I want to continue the leptin rx just to be sure before I leave for Bosnia and Herzegovina for a year abroad, must I keep my carbs low for it to be effective?

In the last few days, I have included some berries and sweet potatoes, noticing only a slight increase in mood and energy from ketosis during the leptin rx. I used to get acne from starchy carbs, and I am still monitoring for that.

Any thoughts are appreciated greatly!

\- Matt

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-12T19:18:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-163983>

yes, in longer natural light cycles.

### Matty_M — 2015-05-12T19:43:22+0300

Thanks doc. I'll slowly ramp them up going to summer and monitor for symptoms.

BTW, I really appreciate and am inspired by your work. No doubt I plan to follow- I think I might want to be a quantum physicist/biologist.

### Matty_M — 2015-05-12T20:59:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-163995>

Another Q:

If I am still noticing that I feel tired in the early afternoon (7 hours after breakfast), even after eating a small snack (grass fed bison bar- 11g protein, 12g fat, 7g net carbs), even after the time with the Leptin Rx and the other stuff I am doing, _does this indicate that I am leptin resistant?_ Or unable to burn fat optimally, are they the same thing?

If so, I guess I should definitely continue with the Leptin Rx- but would the protocol remain effective _if I include seasonal carbs (up to 30% of calories, allotted 50% in morn, 25%, 25%, generally)?_
thanks a lot.

### Lahelada — 2015-05-13T00:42:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-164018>

I wondered if it was this one. it sounds to me as if you have not switched to be a fatburner. The tiredness/ need to snack window you describe is exactly then for a sugarburner. I note that you seem togive yourself a calorie allotment. The reset specifically states that you should not count. I deduce that from your idea that you need to reduce the fat again after losing the carb cravings. I would bet that your BAB is too small and or fat deficient and that this is why you lack energy sometimes.
Also do you drink sufficient good water ?

As far as the bar is concerned I would ditch it and go for one without aded sugars,fruits and fruit juices. That is added to keep you coming back for more. The vinegar powder is added to balance the taste so it is not overtly sweet. Almost 20 % of its weight is sugar and that is not low sugar in my book,only compared with a candy bar it is.
I know you said that you do not eat it very often but just saying.
Are you leptin sensitive? My two criteria would be if you can comfortably go from breakfast to dinner without cravings ,energy slumps or obsessing about food of any kind . Food should feel coincidental to you .
Another marker is your inflammation levels. Do you know what your status is on this?

I do know that many months ago, before I took an antibiotic and did all those other things (besides being paleo + unintentional carb binges- I had been here for almost a year at that point) my Genova Diagnostics stool test detected "low inflammation" (shown in a picture type thing) but I was not given the number. I will ask my functional doc of we can order bun/creat ratio and HS CRP in my next blood test.

In the last few days I guess I convinced myself that I had become leptin sensitive (because I had followed the protocol very strictly for 5-6 weeks) so I could include seasonal carbs I felt "obligated" to.

The one thing is that I do certainly notice less carb craving, no doubt. However, the tiredness in between breakfast and dinner troubles me, but it does usually go away (maybe my cortisol spikes and my body slowly is able to tap into fat stores?).

Please pardon my referencing grams and what not of food. I do not count calories at all. I am not trying to back off, it is just happening somewhat naturally now. My BAB today was a bowl of beef stew (probably 30-40 grams of protein) and some leftover salmon patties (maybe 15-20 g protein).

The thing confusing me now is whether I should include carbs or not.

I drink a gallon of freezing cold spring water daily.

Thanks so much for the help.

### Lahelada — 2015-05-13T03:07:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-164034>

I tink the message is do not be afraid of carbs but employ them judiciously.
In your shoes I would ditch all prefab food,include veggies in season but no starchy vegetables until you go to Bosnia. Itb is still early in the season so play with spring veggies but a sweet potato is a height of summer veggie. I assume you are going to Bosnia in summer. There you will probably need to branch out in terms of eating but until then you can build the framework.

Rea\\rding your Bab do you feel full after it ? Also,stick with eating fat . I suspect that your diet is fat deficient as you lovingly ogle the carbs. Your preference sould be protein and fat before carbs. Carbs are a garnish in long light days.

oh,oooh errr how are your Bristol scores ? Is your number 2 consistent in its consistency ? Sorry about the embarassing question..

### Matty_M — 2015-05-13T17:21:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-164131>

I guess the question I'm trying to ask with this thread is:

in which situations would seasonal carbs provide benefit (in combo with good fat, as always)?

I'm under the impression that high fat primarily provides benefit by increasing electron flow and current strength, but I can definitely eat carbs and stay high fat (I probably eat more fat with carbs because I load them up with ghee/coconut oil).

Which scenarios warrant the avoidance of carbs (fruit/starch) completely (ketosis), and why exactly the strict avoidance (to be in ketosis), even if it's a circadian mismatch? By what mechanism does this benefit act?

\- Matt

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-14T07:16:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-164193>

> [Matty\_M said:](/goto/post?id=164131)
>
> I guess the question I'm trying to ask with this thread is:
>
> in which situations would seasonal carbs provide benefit (in combo with good fat, as always)?
>
> I'm under the impression that high fat primarily provides benefit by increasing electron flow and current strength, but I can definitely eat carbs and stay high fat (I probably eat more fat with carbs because I load them up with ghee/coconut oil).
>
> Which scenarios warrant the avoidance of carbs (fruit/starch) completely (ketosis), and why exactly the strict avoidance (to be in ketosis), even if it's a circadian mismatch? By what mechanism does this benefit act?
>
> \- Matt

As usual, the answer is: We got no clue as to whether seasonal carbs are good for a particular individual . Self experimentation is needed.

The general rule is that your gut needs lots of protons with no oxygen. If those conditions hold, then carbs will likely not upset the light balance in the gut to lead to dysbiosis.

Personally, when I was living in Melbourne and experiencing lots of gut issues, there was no such thing as a good time to eat carbs. Gut issues always followed after, though there were some periods when carbs were eaten and I didn't experience issues => tells you that there are other much more important factors at play in my case.

\----

A high fat metabolism actually decreases charge on the inner mitochondrial membrane, while increasing the amount of ATP being produced, and hence increasing the potential for hydrophilic water-protein interactions to build EZ water. EZ water can carry more charge.

We need to make this distinction because carb metabolism at Complex 1 will increase charge over the inner mitochondrial membrane, creating high-powered electrons, some of which will leak to generate Reactive Oxygen Species. This is in conjunction with less ATP to unfold proteins and expose water binding sites.

So when are carbs useful? When your body already has enough capacity to make EZ water through other means (like sunlight), and where signalling between components is the limiting factor to health (ROS are signalling molecules). I do not know how to measure if this is the case.

\----

The cautious person would stick to ketosis when stuck in a bad environment

\----

The only way to partially circumvent a bad environment seems to be direct charging of the mammalian battery using Red light (650nm and 850nm) and UV light (not yet user of the wavelengths). I do not know how to do this yet, though I'll probably begin with some experimentation in the coming weeks.

\----

Finally, everything I said in this post in the Ubitquitination #12 thread applies too -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568)

....

### Matty_M — 2015-05-15T04:21:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-carbs-ever-beneficial-while-working-towards-optimal.14462/post-164325>

This is in conjunction with less ATP to unfold proteins and expose water binding sites.

>

>
> The cautious person would stick to ketosis when stuck in a bad environment
>
> \----

> Finally, everything I said in this post in the Ubitquitination #12 thread applies too -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568)
>
> ....

Alright,

I used to get a puffier face (cortisol I think), after eating carbs. Also, I would notice myself more anxious and, conversely, depressed. I would definitely become more dehydrated.

I have not noticed the mood symptoms, nor the dehydration, althout I am skeptical about whether my cheeks are looking a little more swollen. After reintroducing them, I notice that I really do crave them, though not as much as pre leptin reset. I am not sure if this is due to the light cycles more, or to my environment more. If I feel it's an issue, I will jump back to the ketogenic leptin reset.

Even with carbs, during gym class (I go pretty easy for now), my lungs get pretty sore. Also, I get slightly tired 6 or so hours after breakfast. I also get pretty tired quickly when exercising. I think these symptoms make me confident that going ketogenic + leptin reset might be the way to go until I leave for Bosnia.

# Thread: Photosensitivity/migraines/seizures
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jeanne — 2015-05-13T00:06:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/post-164014>

I wanted to post this in the "Ask Jack" but my Gold Klub membership status is not working right now. My 35 year old daughter with 4 natural born children and 8 adopted kiddos started having migraines approx. 15 years ago after a car accident. (She did not have a head injury according to the docs) The migraines have gradually worsened and can be triggered by flashing lights or even sunlight through trees while driving, etc. Very recently, she has experienced photosensitivity seizures and was referred to a neurologist who wants her on meds and referred her for an MRI and EKG. She lives in a rural area and has a farm so I recommended feet on the earth, seafood, increased water and mitigating cell phone use. Does anyone have any experience with this or suggestions? She doesn't want to go on meds. And I certainly don't want that either.

### nonchalant — 2015-05-13T02:44:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/post-164027>

The MRI sounds like a great idea. Dr. K really likes them for the wealth of info they provide.
Your suggestions to your daughter sound good. Would she be open to the idea of cold? Maybe start with ice packs to her neck (carotid arteries) to cool the fluid in the brain?

### Penny — 2015-05-13T03:04:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/post-164033>

magnesium, magnesium and more magnesium - mag malate + epsom salts... avoid glutamates - figure out what in her environment is causing calcium to efflux - it could even be the car - is her cell phone on in the car? GPS? Antenae nearby?
If she is in the countryside, the cell phone power will have to be increased and thus be more toxic - as Dr. K discovered the hard way... seafood will help a lot -
[http://www.emfsse.com/uploads/1/9/5/2/19527747/calciumefflux.pdf](http://www.emfsse.com/uploads/1/9/5/2/19527747/calciumefflux.pdf)

> The MRI sounds like a great idea. Dr. K really likes them for the wealth of info they provide.
> Your suggestions to your daughter sound good. Would she be open to the idea of cold? Maybe start with ice packs to her neck (carotid arteries) to cool the fluid in the brain?

I think she would be open to anything that would help at this point!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-28T04:30:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/post-165604>

Heard today from Chrissy: I found Dr. Kruse (this page, the website, forum, book) very recently (2weeks ago) thanks to my mom. I had been to a local neuro for tx for my migraines which had suddenly developed a seizure-like component. He wanted to put me on meds and didn't want to discuss causes. He ordered an MRI and an EEG. My migraines are triggered by strobe-effect lighting. I was not excited to attend that appointment.
I immediately read the forums, listened to hours of podcasts, asked questions, and implemented everything I could. Epi-paleo, tons of spring water, copper water bottle, grounding, sun time, blue blocking, emf eliminating, tons of fish, CT... Everything I read to do.
I went today for the tests, fully expecting the strobe lights in the EEG to trigger a full blown migraine for days... And I'm fine. Totally fine. No headache. Not even an aura.
This is completely ridiculously absurdly awesome! I've had migraines triggered by florescent lights in a store with only 30 seconds exposure. This was intense, focused strobe lights in a dark room. It's rather unbelievable to me still, but I have Dr. Jack and all of you guys to thank for answering my questions and sharing information so freely!
Thank you!!

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/page-3](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/new-here-link-me-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/page-3)

### Scompy — 2015-11-30T19:58:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/photosensitivity-migraines-seizures.14463/post-182886>

I posted some thoughts in my Journal this morning...

Because of the Japanese Seizure event ([https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dennō\_Senshi\_Porygon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denn%C5%8D_Senshi_Porygon)), we know:

1\. Purple light + Red light PULSED at the same time (about once/second) messes up the translation of the information in the brain and can induce a seizure.

Because of Dr. K, we know:

2\. The body can leak red light.

Premise... **The eye itself can leak red light inside of it.** So what if it's just additive? Environment adds purple light + pulsing. The body creates the red-light component. The combination triggers a seizure.

Initial thoughts...Maybe negative changes in the RPE allows more leaking of redlight in the retina, such as cases with meningitis?

Hi guys!
I'm currently drinking out of the fire hose on this site- listened to 4 interviews via podcast yesterday after seeing a neurosurgeon Monday who diagnosed me with "new onset seizures" of the photosensitive variety. He laughed at me when I declined medication even though this is negatively affecting my daily life. He has scheduled me for an MRI without contrast and an EEG. The idea of doing the EEG with the strobe lights terrifies me because it always triggers a very bad migraine. I'm already trying to figure out a good excuse to cancel that appointment.
Enter research, finding Dr. Kruse, listening to podcasts and trying to make a plan.
If there are links you think I should read, please post them for me.
I've done 23&me (homoz mthfr), familiar with keto adapted diets, live on a farm outside of town, homeschool (so I'm home most days), garden (in the soil and sun), but don't eat much seafood and unlike my two neighbors, we have city water (they have well). I'm going to add more seafood and figure out better water asap... But I would love to have better info specific to the photosensitivity thing.
Thanks in advance!
Chrissy

### NeilBB — 2015-05-13T16:00:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-link-me-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/post-164105>

Normally, in my conventional work, someone like you would be admitted to a special unit in our hospital called a "seizure monitoring unit" hooked up to EEG continuously for 1-5 days (with an IV inserted for emergency treatment of a large seizure if it were to occur). We would attempt to provoke and witness the events with video and EEG in place to obtain a definitive diagnosis before proceeding to next step. This would be the first step in conventional management to distinguish epileptic seizures from migraines from other possibilities. We would also perform a high-resolution 3-Tesla MRI as a routine matter. There would be many treatment options and possibilities, both conventional and "alternative" depending on the specifics of the case. My personal opinion is that most of the things Jack recommends in his prescriptions would be likely to help all of these conditions in various ways, but without knowing more details, hard to say a lot more than that at the moment. I do think drinking clean water, lots of seafood, and icing the brain with something like a cryohelmet would be great general advice for anyone with any "brain disease." Also a ketogenic diet is of proven benefit to patients with seizures in both conventional and alternative world, although very few people that I see in clinic are willing to consistently follow it...

### NeilBB — 2015-05-13T16:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-link-me-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/post-164113>

I used to do a lot of brain-mapping in the OR to assist our neurosurgeons who specialize in brain tumor resection. I would directly stimulate various areas of the brain with electrical current during the procedure while talking to the awake patient to assess function of various brain regions while we "disabled" them with electricity. This would help guide the surgeon as to which areas were vital not to remove.

But what few have seen is this... Sometimes, this electrical stimulation would induce a seizure immediately. And when that happens, do you know the quickest way to stop it if it doesn't stop on its own? The neurosurgeon irrigates the surface of the open brain with ice-cold water. The seizure almost always stops immediately. Food for thought...

Each foot higher we are in altitude means a clock in your SCN has to run four hundred-quadrillions faster per second to keep up. The take away is that your head ages faster than your feet do naturally.....so the taller you are the more your SCN clock is off and that height difference is what leads to a shorter longevity and could result in higher seizure activity. To keep up the retina has more DHA than the brain to drive the DC electric current to make this occur.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-14T15:58:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-link-me-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/post-164241>

More anomalies to consider: electric currents inside the ionosphere are unidirectional. Why would most of the important chemicals in life be helical, like collagen and nucleic acids? Bonded atoms exhibit strikingly similar behavior, called vibrational motions, that enable things to adapt to action of springs. Potential energy is often thought of as “stored” kinetic energy. This means that as things (cells or mitochondria) can remain stationary in a potential field such as an electric or magnetic field. At elevations, you can’t do this well even in sleep. This stillness only happens in mitochondria when we sleep, and it helps when this is at sea level. It does not happen when we are awake because the sun’s oscillations vibrate our cell membranes too much. This is exactly what happens at elevations 24/7. Mitochondria can normally hold the vibrations of photons, electrons, and protons stable in place by controlling electrical and magnetic forces. At elevations you have less control over electrical and magnetic fields because the increased energies of the electromagnetic force.

DHA turns light to a DC electric current = electrons = hydrophilic proteins = large EZ = leptin sensitive

Life is all about collecting and keeping negative charges. everything is redox.

### nonchalant — 2015-05-15T02:17:35+0300

>
> ... And yet another failed attempt to get help from a traditional MD-- I saw my optometrist last year for optic migraines where I go blind in one eye for 30 min to an hour. No solution offered. If blue blocker lenses could help that....

Chrissy, welcome. I had two optic migraines in one day several years ago. No pain, but completely blind in both eyes for about 20 minutes each time, with another 20 minutes of fuzzy vision. This was long before I knew about epi-paleo and CT. Optometrist did several tests, but no discovered problems or solution offered. An internet search suggested magnesium, and I found it helped. At least the blindness never repeated itself. Another suggestion would be face dunks in icewater.

Now, of course, I eat lots of seafood, wear blueblockers faithfully, and I've been getting vision improvements. And definitely no repeat of the optic migraines.

Huge.........a protonated atmosphere is not good for those with seizures, anxiety, mood disorders, bipolar etc. This is why bad weather affects them more than those with lots of DHA within their heads.

[Pseudohypoxia](http://www.cell.com/abstract/S0092-8674\(13\)01521-3) = low O2 = Low NAD+/NADH ratio = NAD+ drops in [people with blue light exposure](http://caloriesproper.com/artificial-light-regulates-fat-mass-no-bueno/) \= elevated ubiquitin rates = low levels of electrons = electron density in tissues is a function of the DHA concentrations = low EZ size in cell water = dehydration = higher positive charges (protons) in proteins making them less hydrophilic = low intracellular pH = low redox potential = cell and mitochondrial swelling (cyto c release) = lowered magnetic and electric fields in mitochondria = low ATP levels = a lot of carbs and protein electrons on ECT = altered serotonin and dopamine levels in the frontal lobes = NT release tied to calcium efflux = calcium controls voltage gated channels, NMDA, and glutamate excitotoxicity= low DC electric current = low tissue DHA = altered perceptions of reality and depression/anxiety.

More DHA in head = More intuition. Do you see and feel the whole of things? These hunches are creativity trying to tell you something. Are you listening? Intuition is the clear conception of the whole at once that cuts out all the routine processes of thought and leaps straight from the problem to the answer. Practice intuitiveness. Intuition becomes increasingly valuable in the new information society precisely because there is so much data to sift. Most of this data is bad. So subtraction has now become intuitive in our modern world.

Dr. Jack-
I've been super strict epi-paleo since finding you May 13. I've been grounding daily, spending lots of time in the sun and drinking a ton of spring water that I pour from a copper bottle. I'm eating fish every day. I'm limiting blue light and emf exposure as best I can and I've tested almost all areas of my home with a trimeter. I'm typing this through a bluelight filter while wearing my cold helmet.

Today was my EEG and MRI day.

Dr.Jack... I didn't get a migraine after those strobe lights. Not at all. Not at ALL.

This day has been circled on my calendar out of sheer fear for two weeks now. I've dreaded it and wasn't really sure how smart it was to even go if it was going to trigger a multi-day migraine. I've been so scared of this day.

There is absolutely no other explanation to my lack of migraine today other than the changes I've made through your information. Two weeks ago I would have been in bed, nauseous, unable to think clearly for a very long time. I guess you won't be surprised by this information, but I felt I needed to share it with you.
Thank you so much,
Chrissy Jensen

### jwhb77 — 2016-01-05T23:54:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-here-link-me-to-photosensitive-epilepsy-info.14491/post-185507>

>
> I've read somewhere that Julius Caesar was an epileptic. They didn't have artificial light at the time so how was it possible to get this disease at this time?
> And what makes that two people living in the same environment will have different diseases if nnEMF/blue light effects are the same?
>
> By the way Chrissy, any update since may?

Arborescence, to answer your question here about Caesar, epileptic episodes can be triggered by a number of things, not just artificial lighting or nnEMF exposure... e.g. trauma to the head, or simply living in a chronic, high-distress environment like Caesar faced most of his adult life, in a state of perpetual warfare &/or political intrigue.

2 people in identical environmental circumstances (including identical nnEMF/BL exposure) can respond differently for many reasons, e.g. different histories of environmental exposure, differing genetic metabolic traits, etc. Hope this helps

### chrissysdozen — 2016-01-12T23:44:40+0200

Hi! Yes... I had my MRI and EEG and nothing crazy came back! I've been trying to be more consistent with my epi-paleo and especially water (super difficult for me for some reason). I've recently started getting more exercise and am becoming more aware of how my environment (especially stress) throws me into a tailspin. I still get migraines if I'm exposed to flickering light like a fluorescent bulb that's going out or sunlight flashing behind trees when I'm driving, but I'm not having as many mystery migraines. My weird heart "thing" is hopefully just an annoyance as it appears to be genetic and not an actual cause for alarm... so... overall I guess I'm just becoming more aware and trying to be consistent in taking care of myself.

I'm going to add more seafood and figure out better water asap... But I would love to have better info specific to the photosensitivity thing.
> Thanks in advance!
> Chrissy

I realize that your post was 10 years ago but I am researching for Deborah the adult daughter of my family freinds Joyce and Bill . Deborah has suffered with seizures for most of 53 years and I was asking the mother Joyce to consider a Keto diet high in fish and seafood and to ask Deborah's doctors about trying that as Deborah have run out of drug options to try. While researching I ran across this article from CHADWICK OPTICAL which might be more related to your photosensitive variety selzures. Joyce said that Deborah'sdoctors are not sure of exactly what the 'root' variety of or triggers for Deborah's seizures are.

I may not understand article correctly but they use **blue tinted lenses** to help seizures... specifficly they say "**Z Blue, AKA called z1 blue or noir 26**)". Blue Tinted Lenses are not the amber Blue Blocking Glasses which we believe to be helpful blocking screens and night lights. This does not make sense to me.

**I would like to know what Dr. Jack Kruse thinks of this.**

**Can Eyeglass Tints Help With Seizures?**
[https://chadwickoptical.com/resourc...halmic-solutions/eyeglass-tints-for-seizures/](https://chadwickoptical.com/resources/resource-articles/epilepsy-ophthalmic-solutions/eyeglass-tints-for-seizures/)

CAN EPILEPSY GLASSES HELP ALLEVIATE SEIZURES?

Living with epilepsy can be scary, especially when seizures happen without warning. But thanks to advances in science, technology, and healthcare, there are more ways than ever to manage and even prevent these episodes. Doctors and researchers are also learning more about [how epilepsy can affect vision](https://chadwickoptical.com/resources/resource-articles/epilepsy-ophthalmic-solutions/vision-affected-by-epilepsy/), which helps them come up with better tools to stop seizures before they start.

One helpful tool is epilepsy glasses. These special glasses have been shown to reduce certain types of seizures—particularly those triggered by flashing lights (photosensitive epilepsy). By blocking or filtering out certain light frequencies, epilepsy glasses with Z blue tinted lenses can offer people with photosensitive epilepsy a safer, more comfortable way to go about their daily lives.

Until these lenses, there were only two other methods of controlling seizures in epileptic patients: drug therapy and lifestyle changes to avoid light stimuli, both of which come with their fair share of challenges. We are surrounded by screens daily, all of which are emitting artificial blue light. Unlike UV radiation, blue light can penetrate the eyes and cause significant eye strain, headaches, blurred vision, and seizures. It can even mess with your circadian rhythms and affect your sleep. Add to that blue light exposure from the sun and you begin to quickly realize there is nowhere you can go without running into some sort of potentially harmful blue light. Tinted lenses offer a handy solution that travels with you and blocks that blue light wherever you go.

HOW DO EPILEPSY GLASSES WORK?

Through clinical research, [studies](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16529617/) have found that particular shades of blue lenses (specifically Z Blue, AKA called z1 blue or noir 26) are beneficial for photosensitive epilepsy patients. These blue shades work to interrupt the patient’s peripheral vision in a way that allows the brain to receive information such as spatial distance without competing data, thus helping the patient to operate more normally. In effect, they reduce the patient’s photoparoxysmal response (the abnormal response that comes from being exposed to flashing lights) by filtering out disruptive lights and patterns, particularly flickering dot patterns. An optometric technique called colorimetry gauges the perceptual effects of ophthalmic tints, optimizes them, and then prescribes the correct color to integrate into tinted eyeglass lenses. Add the color to your lenses and voila! You now have a useful and convenient tool to help prevent seizures (as well as a range of other irritations and eye issues).

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-14T00:00:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-valves-and-calcified-arterial-plaques.14493/post-164167>

Valve disease and atherosclerosis cause turbulence and turbulence destroys the EZ of water in plasma.......93% of blood is water. If the turbulence is bad enough surrounding vascular proteins lose electrons and become hydrophobic changing their redox and favoring calcium deposition from efflux...........the more calcium you collect the more turbulence you make the more turbulence you make the more energy inefficient one becomes. Energy is a function of flow. Rayleigh Benard convection plays a major role here along with many other fluid dynamic principles. As calcification increases you need to watch diameter of the valve. As valve diameter increases or mass of valve increase energy efficiency craters.......and pressures radically change.

-   inflammation = positive charge accumulation = loss of electrons from proteins.

-   When proteins lose electrons water cannot charge separate.......when this happens redox drops. In plasma this is measured in a drop of the zeta potential. When that happens RBC clump and forms clots.

-   everything is redox.

-   Life is all about collecting and keeping negative charges.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-is-worse-trains-or-planes.14494/post-164275>

Why? Ubi 1-3 answer it.

When you go higher away from Earth’s core, even by an inch, gravity, and the Delbrück effect on light, becomes more magnified. This is why elevation in a microwaved world is a really bad idea. It is also why living in a high rise, flying frequently, being a pilot, or an astronaut is a horrible idea for your biology.

In 2010 scientists at the National Institute of Standards and Technology went further in proving my insights might be spot on. Their experiments showed that just at 1 foot elevation, a clock ticks four-hundreth-quadrillionth faster per second. I would remind you all most humans stand erect and are 5-6 feet tall. Now we have proof why the SCN must run faster than organ clocks. Physics proves it. This relationship cements why any altitude is deadly for biology.

# Thread: Cosleeping with a newborn on magnetico?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosleeping-with-a-newborn-on-magnetico.14495/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2015-05-14T02:40:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosleeping-with-a-newborn-on-magnetico.14495/post-164185>

So I am very much hoping our baby can sleep with us on the magnetico. Have any of you experience with it? I will be calling Dr Bonlie too but wanted to ask here as well.

I know I can't do one of those bassinets where you attach it to the side of the bed bc of the field....but I wonder how safe it is to have a newborn sleeping with/in between you? Or maybe I could ask DH to sleep away for a few weeks?

Trouble is too our MBR is super tiny so I can't really put a regular bassinet in our room outside of the field.....but the bed is huge (king)...and all organic, all wood.

TIA!!

### bionaut — 2015-05-16T17:19:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosleeping-with-a-newborn-on-magnetico.14495/post-164440>

About a year and a half ago my wife and I purchased a 10G magnetico and we got pregnant a month after. I reached out to Dr Bonlie and I decided to keep my wife off of the mattress until post breastfeeding, he mentioned that the body excretes metals at a higher rate in the beginning of the use of the magnetico so it would be wise to avoid use if just starting while getting pregnant or if starting while breastfeeding. Bonlie has heard of positive results from individuals that have previously been on the mattress post the adjustment period getting pregnant and then carrying the child to term and then continuing with it.

Also, I had concerns about orientation of cells on a fetus if exposed to a magnetic field at the beginning of gestation so I opted to put them on it after they were born.
I purchased two 5G magnetico's because I have twins and they sleep decently on them. They have been using it since their first day out of the womb.

A few observations in the time we have had it on the twins, if we placed the kids on the 10G to take a nap they slept like a rock, no issues on the 5G. My son had a perforated lung and skin rashes coming out of the hospital also it took him a little longer to make eye contact and start connecting with people. I can't say that it was the mattresses but over a couple of months of a lot of DHA and sleep for mom and the kids and he was right as rain.
They are peculiarly smart, I know I am biased as a parent but they began to crawl and walk early and are do not get upset often. I have a boy and a girl, my daughter and son have both been a little lower in weight than the median for kids and my son is about in the top 5% for head size. Overall, a very positive experience for us.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-16T17:25:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cosleeping-with-a-newborn-on-magnetico.14495/post-164441>

Momma needs lots of DHA.....to fill her boobs and her butt to make the baby pre loaded with DHA.............and then mom and dad need to keep baby away from light especially the fake shut. This is why babies sleep so much folks........they are putting DHA to work in their CNS and PNS........you stupid baby photos make them worse off.

When you get here hopefully you will understand controlling light at night and bad light during the day is what speeds up biology by increasing ubiquitination.

-   Beginner's this is for you consideration now: Since you had an epiphany about light........recently let me give you another one. Light works directionally. Cathode to anode. What happens to light as it approaches Earth? What do photons run into initially when they see Earth? What does this initially do for life?
-   It hits the magnetosphere 90,000 km away from you..........and it is the biggest and best filter of really damaging solar radiations that raise ubiquitin rates the most..............So when you live on the tectonic plates of a planet whose surface now resembles more of a cathode than an anode.........because the world is blue lit and microwaved and lit up over the last 135 years........what does that do to magnetic sense in your mitochondria? Do you understand why your magnetico is your new protective magnetosphere in your bedroom to use at night now to slow down your SCN and lower ubiquitin rates? Macro meets microcosm but the physics are exactly the same.......the scale is the only difference. Blue light signals destroy melanopsin to liberate Vitamin A which destroys all heme based photoreceptors. This dehydrates tissues and alters the photo-bioelectric signals everywhere on the system to reduce it electrical reistance. It has to do with the mass of the magnet. With the magnetico there are many magnets together. With that many magnets, the overall mass increases and the distance to which the gauss starts to decrease is increased.
The Magnetico does work as advertised as I have measured the gauss with a simple gauss meter that I borrowed from work (I should really buy my own)
[http://www.magwerks.com/thumbnails/11-03-01-06.jpg](http://www.magwerks.com/thumbnails/11-03-01-06.jpg)

The thing that I like about the Magnetico is that once you've purchased it and installed it, you're done! You will heal without ever having to think about it or plan it (like you do with water, meals, CT etc.)
I'm not saying to not do those other things. But sleep is extremely important. If you can speed up the healing process by increasing the gauss, I'm in!
I'm sure the pad is great for those people who have such a bad redox potential that they have no motivation to help themselves and constantly give in to modern temptations, regardless of the consequences (like my dad and sister who are both disabled from mental illness). It may improve it just enough to pursue more wellness.
Even if you don't notice anything from the pad it will greatly increase the effectiveness of any future therapy you do.

### PaulG — 2015-05-25T12:23:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetic-mattress-sweet-spot.14496/post-165279>

> Jack Kruse said:
>
> "If Bonelie could make a 1000G and it wasn't a huge amount of money I would buy one."

I don't understand this recommendation, given our current bodies have been entrained under current earth magnetic field of 0.5 gauss and from evolutionary perspective Earth maximum magnetic field reached a peak of 2-3 times present strength, 3000 years ago. See [this article](http://www.wired.com/2010/12/magnetic-copper-slag/)

Life on Earth has never experienced such a strong constant static magnetic field. Jupiter has a magnetic field of 1000 gauss (100 milliTesla = 1000 gauss)

This [paper](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC312950/pdf/i1355-8145-003-02-0079.pdf) suggests a magnetic field of less that 1 gauss causes a stress response although this paper [Effect of a 100mT Static Magnetic Field on Hsp70 mRNA in HL-60 Cells](http://www.ursi.org/proceedings/procGA08/papers/KP2p1.pdf) did not find an effect
This [paper](http://www.fasebj.org/content/13/1/95.full) says **Magnetic fields increase cell survival by inhibiting apoptosis via modulation of Ca2+influx**

> In conclusion, we show here that MFs greater than 0.6 mT (far lower than those present, i.e., at the tip of hand phone antennae) exert a significant effect on the frequency of apoptosis; thus, exposure to fields of this strength may lead to a disturbance of cell homeostasis ([32](http://www.fasebj.org/content/13/1/95.full#ref-32)), with clear implications for human health. On the one hand, our data suggest that magnetic fields locally applied to tissues undergoing degeneration (i.e., neuron degeneration, autoimmune diseases) might help to slow down the pathological degenerative processes associated with at least some cell types (actually, ‘magnetotherapies’ belong to a byway of medicine hardly recognized as official since Mesmer's times toward the end of 18th century). On the other hand, the findings described here warn against the presence of magnetic fields where it is required that all cells damaged either accidentally (i.e., sun exposure, mutagens contamination, etc.) or on purpose (cytocidal therapies), die. It will be most important to understand the basis of the various sensitivities of different cell types to Ca2+ fluxes. Indeed, this could be the reason why MF exposure seems to affect only some specific types of tumors, as is revealed by comparing many scattered epidemiological studies.
Confused?

I understand what you are saying about having more magnetism than what we have evolved with.

I'm also a bit confused about the so called "harmful positive return field". As far as I understand there is only one field that runs in one direction in a loop: from north to south and through the magnet (also called the magnetic 'circuit').
If the direction is the problem, then that would mean that you could not roll over in your bed because the direction into your tissues would reverse.
But a return field would cancel out the earth's native field however.
Regardless, a field that goes up say through your chest and then back out through your feet would definitely be unnatural.

# Thread: Case of my father suffering from Mylodysplatic Syndrome
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/case-of-my-father-suffering-from-mylodysplatic-syndrome.14510/>

### Jainendra Tripathi — 2015-05-14T10:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/case-of-my-father-suffering-from-mylodysplatic-syndrome.14510/post-164217>

Hi Dr. Jack Kruse,

Thanks for helping patients world wide thruogh your blogs.

I seek your advice and recommendations on my father's case.
He had complaints of weakness and easy fatiguability in August-September 2014. There was also history of intermittent fever which was low grade.

He is suffering from a condition in which his platelets stability is compromised, so a bone marrow biopsy was performed and the doctors here have diagnosed him to be suffering from MDS \[Mylodysplastic Syndrome\] RAEB1, IPSS Int2 with evolving platelet refractoriness and ATT induced hepatitis.

Hence I am requesting your guidance.

He is additionally suffering from Tuberculosis that was diagnosed on the basis of bronchoscopy culture test that was conducted on him.

Doctors recommended to start with lenalidomide tablets. But after starting these, his platelets count started dropping drastically, later doctor’s recommended Azacitidine therapy on him. As part of treatment we have completed four cycle of Azacitidine therapy for him. His platelets counts kept dropping even after doing these therapy. So he required frequent blood and platelet transfusions.

From last two months I am following your blogs and recommendations on balanced diet, meditation and other things. After doing these life style changes I saw some improvements in my father's condition, But again I see there is drop in his platelets count from last 15 days. On 10-May-2015 his platelets count were 60 thousand.

I am worried about my father's condition and look forward for your valuable advice on this.

I have also attached the discharge summaries for your kind perusal.

Modern humans are undergoing active continuous cognitive de-evolution because of electric currents that fuel our use of modern fake light technology and our comforts. This is why now everything must be short, to the point, soundbite form and be transmitted in 140 characters or less on social media. No one is capable of thinking critically anymore..........why? The evidence is OVERWHELMING but a species in decline is hard pressed to see "their asteroid" I understand you want to help your Dad but only he can help himself. When you are facing an iceberg on the Titanic you must do things 180 degrees opposite what you have done before. You can help your Dad by first helping yourself Jainendra. If it is dense you have to focus and concentrate and assimilate. Can you do that with a bad battery in your head? ### Haley — 2015-05-14T18:09:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-in-curing-sibo.14516/post-164261>

There are a few forums as well as Jack's latest blog post mentioning SIBO, but I would love to hear from those who have dealt with it. I have been trying to fix my digestive issues for over 5 years and the more foods I eliminate, the worse I get. Since starting the leptinrx//epi-paleo diet and modifying lifestyle factors 5 months ago, my symptoms improved dramatically and I have been able to loose a little weight. But like with every protocol I have tried in the past...it stops working (symptomatically, anyway). My diet is narrowed down to a handful of foods: seafood, seaweed, leafy greens, avocado, coconut (butter & oil). So based off my experience and the knowledge on this site ("A circadian mismatch in the proper functioning of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and SCN, is the real cause behind much of the gut illness humans face today") I know diet is not the solution. I am doing the best I can with time spent in the sun, limiting EMF and sleep is great...10pm-6am solid.

So, beyond just hearing from those who have experience with SIBO, I am looking for direction as to what my next step is., even if its just treating the symptoms in the meantime. I would like to go to some kind of professional who could run labs. Which labs should I have run? A SIBO breath test? I'd be willing to take antibiotics if it will help eliminate these symptoms. I can't take the constant, chronic bloating any longer. That is the worst thing I suffer from. Bloating and fatigue.

thanks all

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-14T20:26:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-in-curing-sibo.14516/post-164276>

You need to get rid of bad light and add a ton of DHA and water to match your previous environmental exposure to bad light that got you SIBO.

Having SIBO is a blessing in a disguise. It is a small problem that might lead to a cancerous one years from now if not addressed.
When you are sick and tired of being sick and tired it becomes the ideal time to take matters into your own hands and learn why you are ill instead of entrusting people to get you well. Ubi 12 begins this for SIBO, GERD, and IBS. All are tied to light and nitrogen issues that cause a pseudohypoxia. It is an environmental issue altering your flora. They are looking inside of you for a defect when the real answer is likely outside of you in your environment causing it. This ubiquination series on my blog tells you that. It is about time you take the time to read it. Here is my TRUTH BOMB from ubiquitination 3 blog post: I now live by a new personal rule for the last decade: No human being itself should be considered impaired innately, instead there are environmental short comings that cause the impairment. Thus, it is incumbent on the on the clinician to recommend treatment of the environment their patient is in. People react to an inferior environment, way before their genome is altered. That is what the science of epigenetics is telegraphing us, but the modern paradigm is not listening.

The best leaders inspire greatness in others through their words and actions. Trailblazers take people as far as they will go, not as far as they would like them to go. Trailblazer’s have the ability to unlock the potential talent within people to improve them all. Energy correctly applied can accomplish anything, in my opinion.

Any one who believes in genetic determinism, the most common modern paradigm, in my opinion, also believes in unicorns, the tooth fairy, and bigfoot. The genome is an ‘organ of the cell’, nor its dictator. This is a very controversial point today, but this series will make my case for my new beliefs.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-14T20:29:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-had-success-in-curing-sibo.14516/post-164277>

Yew, one of our members did.

Here is his words, "As usual, the answer is: We got no clue as to whether seasonal carbs are good for a particular individual . Self experimentation is needed.

The general rule is that your gut needs lots of protons with no oxygen. If those conditions hold, then carbs will likely not upset the light balance in the gut to lead to dysbiosis.

Personally, when I was living in Melbourne and experiencing lots of gut issues, there was no such thing as a good time to eat carbs. Gut issues always followed after, though there were some periods when carbs were eaten and I didn't experience issues => tells you that there are other much more important factors at play in my case.

\----

A high fat metabolism actually decreases charge on the inner mitochondrial membrane, while increasing the amount of ATP being produced, and hence increasing the potential for hydrophilic water-protein interactions to build EZ water. EZ water can carry more charge.

We need to make this distinction because carb metabolism at Complex 1 will increase charge over the inner mitochondrial membrane, creating high-powered electrons, some of which will leak to generate Reactive Oxygen Species. This is in conjunction with less ATP to unfold proteins and expose water binding sites.

So when are carbs useful? When your body already has enough capacity to make EZ water through other means (like sunlight), and where signalling between components is the limiting factor to health (ROS are signalling molecules). I do not know how to measure if this is the case.

\----

The cautious person would stick to ketosis when stuck in a bad environment

\----

The only way to partially circumvent a bad environment seems to be direct charging of the mammalian battery using Red light (650nm and 850nm) and UV light (not yet user of the wavelengths). I do not know how to do this yet, though I'll probably begin with some experimentation in the coming weeks.

\----

Finally, everything I said in this post in the Ubitquitination #12 thread applies too -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568)"

Read his journal..........its an eye opener for SIBO

\----

Being in ketosis is not good or bad per se, and neither is eating carbs. The key point is how to match external inputs (diet) to the environment to produce the correct signalling in the body.

Good signalling is best achieved when you have lots of NAD+ (Sinclair 2013 -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867413015213](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867413015213)).

NAD+ works because of the nicotiamide ring, which contains a hexagonal pyrimidine group (containing Nitrogen), which helps with light transport (specifically of UV light, likely between 270nm and 380nm). Lulz, we're back to Nitrogen and light

# Thread: Neurons, protons, and lactate
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-05-15T03:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/post-164321>

When the energy in ATP is utilized during cell work in a process called ATP hydrolysis, protons are naturally produced. In REM sleep no ATP is used. Water EZ is the key to energy generation. Let's jump down this rabbit hole. With ATP use, the mitochondria normally incorporate these protons back into ATP via the ATPase, thus preventing buildup of protons and maintaining neutral pH. High pH forms larger EZ's and low pH destroys EZ's. If oxygen supply is inadequate (hypoxia or pseudohypoxia), the mitochondria are unable to continue ATP synthesis at a rate sufficient to supply the cell with the required ATP. In this situation, glycolysis is increased to provide additional ATP, and the excess pyruvate produced is converted into lactate and released from the cell into the bloodstream, where it accumulates over time. The brain hates superoxide. It also despises fatty acids. It's a bit ambivalent about glucose, contrary to popular dogma. It does not reject glucose, but it is built to recognize there are better fuels to be used.

Is there any fuel the brain does like? Well, ketone bodies seem to be reasonable, but what the CNS/PNS really seems to like is lactate. Glucose is often fed to neurons, but is always pre digested by the glial cells, as lactate. This is not well known. The FFAs are fed as ketones, yes, the glial cells in the brain are ketogenic. They replenish NAD+. The brain is not just the onlt tissue that does this, the liver that does this too. This is why blood glucose really rises in pseudohypoxic low NAD+ states........it is not a food or fuel story. It is a lack of oxygen story. What atoms is your terminal electron selector is a real big deal. When O2 is lacking you can't make free radicals and you cannot release ELF UV bio photons well to signal properly. Your cells begin too release too much light. When you release too much light, it is not monochromatic. Too much light release correlates with higher ubiquitination rates. While increased glycolysis helps compensate for less ATP from oxidative phosphorylation, it cannot bind the protons created resulting from ATP hydrolysis. Therefore, proton concentration rises and causes acidosis. The EZ is destroyed. Lactate doesn't need any glycolysis to take place simultaneously in neurons themselves. Lactate is the most pure substrate for complex I with low NAD+. This is why the brain and heart love lactate fuel. Lactate usage appears to be the best way of postponing apoptosis. Apoptosis is not something you want if your a neuron or cardiomyocyte is it?

### nonchalant — 2015-05-15T19:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/post-164369>

"Lactic acid is [chiral](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chirality_%28chemistry%29) and has two [optical isomers](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optical_isomer). One is known as L-(+)-lactic acid or (_S_)-lactic acid and the other, its mirror image, is D-(−)-lactic acid or (_R_)-lactic acid."

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-16T01:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/neurons-protons-and-lactate.14517/post-164393>

Oh well, @[Shijin13](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/969/) made me post this in the QH Facebook Group , so I thought I'd post it here too.

\----

The way I have been taught to view the world as a programmer and an obedient Chinese boy is that "things do not have a reason"; There are a given a set of constraints, and Nature finds a way to work with those constraints.

The major constraints on this planet are that:

**(1)** The contents of the atmosphere only allows a narrow band of light to be used -- the higher energy particles get blocked from the organisms of this earth. ie: UV radiation is more scarce that IR radiation.

**(2)** Water happens to exists in huge abundance, and when charge-separated on this planet happens to have a huge absorption peak at 250-270nm

If I were God, I would see these constraints, and design my "replication activator" to be sensitive to the scarce photons that can be transferred and emitted by water.

\----

**All 4 DNA bases contain a pyrimidine ring, which will absorb the 380nm band.** This absorption is fundamentally \*\*excitatory\*\*.

To make use of a piece of DNA for protein transcription, the strand of DNA needs to become "more un-condensed" by unwinding and exposing the correct sequence of bases. This explains why you need weak signals to un-condense DNA slight for protein synthesis, but strong signals leave you with completely un-condensed DNA that is functionally destroyed and unusable.

\----

**EZ Water absorbs light very well with a 270nm wavelength. During this process, it also emits light (fluorescence)**. I can't find exactly what wavelengths are fluoresced, but the general rule of thumb is that the photons lose some energy, and the fluorescent photons are of longer wavelength.

If you look at the absorption spectrum of EZ water, it tapers off somewhere in 330nm range. This likely means that any photons emitting by water have to be from 330nm and upward.

The short proximity of water and 380nm-absorbing compounds containing a nitrogen-containing hexagonal ring mean that information transfer using 380nm light is pretty efficient. Also note the slight blip in absorbance at around 370nm.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

\----

**270nm is mitogenic**. "Mitogenic" just means "activates mitogen" and therefore cell division.

If I were God, I would recognise that membrane-bound EZ water would increase the size of that EZ significantly when exposed to (relatively scarce) 270nm light. This increase in EZ would increase cell membrane potential, increase viscosity, increase optical density (more red-shift; ie -- toward the 380nm wavelength), and exert extra pressure on the cell membrane.

I would let this be a "cell division" signal, just for the arbitrary reason that the effects of 270nm light can amplify the factors I mention in the previous paragraph (given the constraints on this planet)

\----

**Molecular carcinogens absorb 380nm photon and re-emit those photons at different wavelengths. DNA also relies on emission of this 380nm to signal other nearby DNA to do the appropriate thing.**

This is very confusing to an emitting DNA -- very likely, the 380nm photons they emit are entangled to the local electrons present in the pyrimidine rings. The pyrimidine rings are also the functional groups needed for protein transcription.

The only way you know that a signal has been received is to get a signal back. In this case, the emitting DNA strand will get a signal back by virtue of absorption of its emitted entangled photons by another DNA strand.

Carcinogens will disrupt this process by absorbing those emitted entangled photons, and then re-emitting mixed signals. In any case, lacking the same magnitude of negative feedback, emitting DNA strands ramp-up their activity -- uncontrolled growth.

....

### PaulG — 2015-05-15T04:48:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-your-thoughts-on-the-use-of-smart-drugs-nootropics.14518/post-164327>

I have just ordered [noopept](http://examine.com/supplements/Noopept/), [aniracetam](http://examine.com/supplements/Aniracetam/) and [phenylpiracetam](http://examine.com/supplements/Phenylpiracetam)

Would be very interested in other peoples thoughts on the use of Smart Drugs and their experiences.

Perhaps they can be beneficial in certain specific one-off situations such as revising/taking exams but not good for long-term use.

>

Dave Asprey downs many a pill. See this 5 minute video

### deallen7 — 2015-05-16T17:16:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-your-thoughts-on-the-use-of-smart-drugs-nootropics.14518/post-164437>

I have experimented with both piracetam and aniracetam. Didn't squeeze out any improvement in my cognitive function using piracetam, but I loved the improved focus, creativity and anti-anxiety effects of aniracetam. To echo Jack's quote, I would definitely advise cycling the aniracetam or only using it occasionally. I found myself becoming emotionally distant/robot-like while on it regularly.

Some other nootropics worth experimenting with are creatine monohydrate, sunflower lecithin and lion's mane mushroom:

-   Creatine seems to provide an instantaneous cognitive boost. No negative experiences while on it.
-   Sunflower lecithin seemed to improve my memory at times, while giving me headaches at times.
-   Lion's Mane Mushroom seemed to really improve my organizational skills. I also found myself occasionally surprised at the things I was remembering while on it.

Interested to hear about your experiences with [noopept](http://examine.com/supplements/Noopept/), [aniracetam](http://examine.com/supplements/Aniracetam/) and [phenylpiracetam](http://examine.com/supplements/Phenylpiracetam). Please share.

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-29T02:57:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-are-your-thoughts-on-the-use-of-smart-drugs-nootropics.14518/post-165744>

Here is what I think is the likely mechanism.

We clearly see that 2-Pyrrolidone (sometimes called 2-Pyrollidinone), which is the active group of the recatams, up-regulates CAMKII activity

\- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0169328X0100331X](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0169328X0100331X)
\- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0169328X0300281X](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0169328X0300281X)

As to why exactly 2-Pyrrolidone affects CAMKII, I need to give more thought to that mechanism.

I think that the variable action of the different racetams is due to the relative paramagnetic nature of the rest of the compound. For example, Aniracetam has a 6 carbon aromatic ring and a bunch of "border oxygens", which give it a good dipole moment to be transported to brain microtubules ([http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aniracetam](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aniracetam))

This fits perfectly with the Orch-OR model that Hameroff and Penrose put forth, whereby CAMKII provides a 6-leg kinase binding domain which precisely match the structure of the alpha and beta tubulin lattices found in dentritic microtubules -- [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1571064513001188](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1571064513001188)

Binding of CAMKII to the microtubule pushes Ca2+ ions into the microtubule (through phosphorylation). I personally suspect that this generates more topological edge states where the quantum and classical worlds meet, thereby allowing more moments of conscious perception.

(Sidenote: this likely will not increase the amount of available superpositions, which is the main governor of creativity)

Regardless of the exact mechanism, I think this is good evidence for a 2-Pyrrolidone mediated over-expression of CAMKII, resulting in more moments of conscious perception per unit time.

There is a cost to this, as it inevitably affects Ca2+ balance within the body. IMO, this results in unnaturally high Ca2+ turnover rates, without the necessary negative feedback mechanisms in place. It is completely possible that with the over-stimulation of CAMKII mediate Calcium influx, that un-naturally high Calcium Efflux also starts to occur as a direct feedback mechanism. Because systemic negative feedback is not affected, this Ca2+ efflux remains high even after the 2-Pyrrolidone stimulus is gone. A recipe for disaster if you ask me.

....

### Jill1 — 2015-05-16T05:46:03+0300

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-16T07:13:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-h302.14519/post-164410>

You need to tone down your absolutism, and not mis-represent other people's views.

Pollack clearly says from 28min onward, "there are a lot of waters out there .... nobody knows which ones are the best ...." (he then mentions Alkaline water, Reverse Osmosis, etc ) .... "Some group needs to test these waters comparatively to see which ones are good for your health". He states that he would need about $3-4 million to get this research done earlier in the video.

He does not say "drink H3O2" , he's being a good scientist and saying that maybe RO water may not provide enough EZ.

\----

He states at around the 35min mark that maybe the body wants to get rid of protons. This may be true for the eukaryotic cells in our body, but it is not true of the prokaryotes in our gut, which absolutely love protons (and low pH).

I talk about why you want lots of protons to feed the gut microbiome in the absence of oxygen in this post -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/sibo-gerd-or-ibs-ubiquitination-12-is-live.14378/#post-163568)

\----

Pollack has **speculated** that drinking water that has a lot of EZ in it may help your cells hydrate better. This potentially makes sense, because the charge gradients between EZ water and your cells will help drive water inside.

However, water from your gut needs to pass through your intestinal lining, into the serum, and then be carried into your cells. This is all amidst high temperatures and acidic conditions, both of which Pollack himself has shown to destroy EZ water. Thus there is good reason to believe that drinking water that is already in the EZ state is not exactly the key factor.

The key criteria for good water is the **potential** for Water to form strong hydrogen bonding networks. **This means that the most important factor is the H-O-H bond angles in each individual water molecule**.

Read Tensegrity #6 -- [http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-6-hydrogen-bonding-networks-water/](http://www.jackkruse.com/tensegrity-6-hydrogen-bonding-networks-water/)

Then read Martin Chaplin's page on EMF effects on water molecules -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic\_electric\_effects.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/magnetic_electric_effects.html)

He states (with some of my comments where indicated):

An electric field also changes the molecular O-H bond lengths (25x109 V m-1 causing ~±6% change in a lone water molecule), **H-O-H bond angle (25x109 V m-1 causing ~+1%/-0.2% change in a lone water molecule)**

This will affect the hydrogen bonded network in an anisotropic manner.

Static magnetic effects have been shown to cause strengthened hydrogen bonding, as shown by the increase in fluorescence of dissolved probes.

Also, magnetic fields affect the infrared spectrum of water (showing its effect on water clustering) and these effects remain for considerable time after the magnetic field is removed.

Surprisingly, even very small magnetic fields may affect the solubility of gases in seawater (solubility increasing with magnetic field (20-50 µT), probably by their effect on the clathrate stability. **This reinforces the view that it is the movement through a magnetic field, and it associated electromagnetic effect, that is important for disrupting the hydrogen bonding.** \[**TYW comment**: hence one of the reasons I am skeptical of vortexing water while it is placed in a strong magnetic field\]

Magnetic fields can also increase proton spin relaxation, which may speed up some reactions dependent on proton transfer.

It appears that electric and magnetic fields have opposite effects on water clustering

Unstructured water with fewer hydrogen bonds is a more reactive environment. Any factors that reduce hydrogen bonding and hydrogen bond strength, such as electric fields, should encourage reactivity.

**Water clusters (even with random arrangements) have equal hydrogen bonding in all directions.** As such, electric or electromagnetic fields that attempt to reorient the water molecules should necessitate the breakage of some hydrogen bonds; for example, **electric fields have been reported to halve the mean water cluster size as measured by 17O-NMR**

Electromagnetic radiation (for example, microwave) has been shown to exert its effect primarily through the electrical rather than magnetic effect.

Extremely low frequency electromagnetic fields (ELF-EMF) have significant and lasting effects on liquid water. Using a weak field, adjusted to give a magnetic field of 45 µT, on glutamic acid solutions causes changes in the pH shifting towards the de-protonated species. \[**TYW comment**: remember that you want "protons without oxygen" for the gut\]​

EMF that causes changes in the H-O-H bond makes the water molecule behave differently. What is important is that the H-O-H angles of water molecules are congruent with the rest of our biological processes, which happen to rely on the Schumann Resonance as a resonance yardstick.

**Important point**: We don't care about claims like "a larger H-O-H bond angle is magic", which some water sellers like to make. **Again, what is important is that the water molecules present in your body know how to cooperatively form strong Hydrogen Bonding networks given the solute and electromagnetic environment that the water molecules find themselves in.**

We evolved on this planet, and the Planet's Natural Magnetic field set the constraints on how water should behave. Water also happens to have a good memory of the environment that it was in. So why would it come as a surprise that spring water is good for us? This water has been exposed to the natural magnetic field of the Earth, and contains the natural amount of dissolved solutes.

ie: if life could have evolved on another planet, with a different magnetic field, but with the presence of water, the "optimal H-O-H bond angle" would be different.​

Until the people who make these supposed "high-energy waters" start to examine these factors (which are part of an insanely complex system), I highly doubt in the ability of humans to engineer water that provides these effects.

\----

I will make the claim that the difference @[Josh (Paleo Osteo)](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9815/) experienced between Australian RO Water and Italian Mineral Water is precisely because the individual water molecules in the Australian RO Water has altered H-O-H bonding networks (due to the Ozone hole) that cannot be fixed until exposure to strong natural magnetic fields. The Italian mineral water did not suffer from this effect.

It is unfair to demonise the Reverse Osmosis process, when there are clearly factors at play beyond which the RO process can correct.

A fairer test would have been between Italian mineral water, and the same Italian mineral water that has been through the RO process

....

### yewwei.tan — 2015-05-16T07:37:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-pollack-h302.14519/post-164412>

I have also claimed that there is more favourable water at the location where I am at in Australia.

Can be summed with this image -- [http://media.web.britannica.com/eb-media/78/118378-050-00F2D6F0.gif](http://media.web.britannica.com/eb-media/78/118378-050-00F2D6F0.gif)

The Australian North is mainly fed by Tropical monsoon rains. The mean rainfall where I am at during the monsoon months of Dec-Apr is (175.9 + 394.3 + 448.9 + 424.2 + 198.3) ~= **1,639mm.** Then during the other months of May-Nov, the rainfall is (91.4 + 45.6 + 29.2 + 26.7 + 33.4 + 46.0 + 93.7) ~= **363mm**.

Water brought by the tropical monsoon is less affected by nn-EMF from the ozone hole, and likely brings water that is more congruent with our biology. This basically means that the Aussie North is being fed with "better water" than the Aussie South where Josh is (for now .... Earth's atmosphere is changing on a global scale)

Note that while my district does not fluoridate water, which is also a benefit, it is probably a secondary factor to the factors I discussed above; The body can get rid of fluoride, but it is forced to make use of whatever water molecules it is being fed with.

....

People need to realize the cell is fully capable of doing what it does to water if you get out of its way and make the environment it senses well............**

### yewwei.tan — 2015-06-01T07:59:52+0300
