Hi Dr Jack,

Thanks for enabling access to website from Bangalore, appreciate it.
One of my relatives have a small child \[ 2 yrs\] who is diagnosed with WEST syndrome,
Could you advice ?

Regards
Sami

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-06T01:41:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/west-syndrome-help.15132/post-166716>

I have not seen a patient with this affliction. Maybe NeilBB has.

### NeilBB — 2015-06-06T05:49:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/west-syndrome-help.15132/post-166744>

Traditionally, the "infantile spasms" associated with this syndrome have been treated with corticosteroids or their precursor hormone, ACTH. The relatively new anticonvulsant vigabatrin has also been used a lot. I don't see babies currently but I do know also that this is one of the childhood epilepsies that is quite likely to respond to a ketogenic diet. Depending on the underlying cause, this is also something that some will grow out of. But the long term prognosis really depends on whether their are other things wrong with the structure of the brain.

My bet is that is what is behind this.

# Thread: Journey away from night time destruction
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 160
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-05T18:31:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-166698>

Hi, everyone, I am Deverie. I am 38, married, mother of 2, and a chiropractor. I'm currently 252 lbs with suspected bad redox. My hormones are whacked. Despite knowing how to be “healthy” and helping other people change their lives, I have been unable to do that for myself. I’m here for the hard truth and ready for a paradigm shift.

Here's my story...

I've been a night owl since I was 11. It started as a form of rebellion. I would stuff towels under my door or hide under the covers with a flashlight, hiding the light from my parents while writing poetry or reading. By high school, I was rarely in bed before midnight.

In college, my major was chemical engineering. It was stressful and I was a huge procrastinator and would often stay up late to get homework and projects completed. It was common to pull all-nighters. And I had a terrible diet – SAD and I rarely ate any fresh food.

At 19 I saw an endocrinologist due to acne, facial hair, and irregular menstruation. I was diagnosed with PCOS, started on the pill, and told I would be on it for life and would never have kids. This launched me into a depression.

After college, I worked as a chemical engineer in the computer disk drive manufacturing industry. My health declined even further. I gained 65 lbs by binge drinking, binge eating, and staying up until 3 or 4 am. I had a dysfunctional, co-dependent, relationship with a co-worker. I was on call with a beeper and would get beeped at all hours. My job was largely unrewarding. I was a mess.

Eventually I decided I needed to make some changes or I would die. I saw no future for myself in that life. I wasn’t able to help others or myself. After much soul-searching, I decided to go back to school to be a chiropractor. I went to a naturopath, who told me that I was pre-cancerous. I was floored and he lit a fire under me. Thus began my healing journey. I stopped all my meds. I started doing cleanses and detoxing, parasite zapping and gallbladder flushes. I discarded all of my cleaning and personal products and replaced with organic versions. I ate vegetables and coconut oil. I went gluten-free, dairy-free, sugar-free, alcohol-free, fruit-free – I was pretty hard-core for 8 months. I went all-in. I was very determined to not be a fat doctor!

I lost 73 lbs in a year by changing diet only and I never felt better. I went to chiropractic school and started learning so much about the human body and how amazing it is. I found ways to help people improve their lives through chiropractic, diet, and lifestyle. I made huge leaps on my spiritual path. Of course, this was college, so I studied and partied, studied and partied. Lots of late nights and stress, but overall was able to maintain my major changes.

I met my husband near the end of chiro school. Pretty soon, we had a little baby surprise us with his existence. I was deeply grateful and I knew the changes I had made in my life had regulated my hormones enough to allow my pregnancy. I graduated, got married, and had a baby all within 6 months. The following year, my husband graduated, we moved, opened a practice together, and had a second baby. I was stressed out to the max. I started stress-eating and I didn’t really lose any weight after baby #2. My adrenals burned out.

This past March, I topped out at 268 lbs. I am always gluten-free and soy-free, often dairy-free, sometimes grain-free, but totally addicted to sugar (they make a lot of gluten-free, organic CRAP food!). I am sad that I am a fat doctor, but I’m stuck on the cycle of losing a few pounds and gaining it all back plus some more. My bedtime is usually 2:00 am after spending hours on the computer! I have lots of work to get done and it often happens at night. But I have been a cranky mama and bitchy wife. I am sick of myself and my negative attitude. I set a goal to lose 93 lbs but deep down I wondered if I really had the tools I needed. Every once in awhile, I thought, “I bet it would help me if I got more sleep.” But never did I think my night time habits were the major cause of my problems. My friend introduced me to jackkruse.com and I finally feel I have some answers! Now I know I probably caused PCOS by screwing up my body clocks!

So I’m gearing up to do the Leptin Reset. I will have initial labs drawn next week. My current symptoms are obesity, sugar cravings, acne/rosacea, and joint pain (leg pain really flares with grains!). I made the following changes to my environment: got a tri-field meter and blue-blocker glasses, installed Stetzer dirty electricity filters at work and home, fabricated and installed smart meter guard, turning off wi-fi at night, switching cordless to corded. I am doing CT a little, eating seafood 1-2 times per day + seaweed several times per week, getting a little more sun and barefoot time in grass, and bedtime is getting earlier. I’m excited to make more changes!

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-11T19:18:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167646>

Sun is setting here about 8:30. Is it true that my bedtime should be by 9:30? Within an hour of sunset? That seems really impossible for me. Does anyone have any tips for transitioning from night owl to morning person? I've done it before when I got up for 5:30 am boot camp last year (which wrecked my knee after 6 weeks) and I really loved the atmosphere of the morning. But I was never able to get as much done in the morning as in the quiet of night.

I keep thinking "what is wrong with me!?" But I know I'm not alone. My sister is a night owl, too. She's been struggling with her weight for as long as I have. Last year, she started a workout routine and built tons of muscle and strength and spent a year with a trainer. She didn't lose any weight (I know, muscle heavier than fat, but she didn't lose any fat, either!) I think I see now that maybe it was her environment that stunted her success in that area. I'm really hoping that what I learn here and eventually am able to put into practice, I can share with her. My Dad also is often up in the middle of the night - his problem, though, is that he can't stay asleep, and gets up at 3 am to work on the computer. "It's in the family" - but I feel confident that I can overcome my genes!

My husband and I have our own chiropractic office. I see patients three days a week, then other times blocked off to take care of employee issues and various business things, and then I have my kids all the rest of the time. I have more I need to do, usually, so I work from home some. I don't want to be with the kids less in order to get my other stuff done. I'm trying to not let them just sit in front of screens all the time, but it happens when I need to be productive. Some days I feel like I need a schedule "makeover." And I might, if I am going to be done with dinner as early as recommended!

For now, I have set a bedtime for myself of 11:00; I know it's maybe not as early as it should be, but it's a helluva lot earlier than 2 am. I'm having a difficult time being successful, though. I find I'm trying to finish one more thing, read one more post, check out one more Kruse blog. \*sigh\*

Any tips will be helpful!! Thank you!!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-11T19:32:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167651>

Night owls repair themselves when they add more DHA to all their tissues and not just their brain.

### thisbirdhaswings — 2015-06-11T21:04:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167668>

I work from home, too. The key to earlier bedtimes is getting off the screens at night. I know it is harder with the longer light cycles, but one key (aside from DHA) is stopping and realizing that whatever is not done today will be (can be) done tomorrow. Spend your evenings taking a candle-lit bath and/or having quality time with your husband instead

### Peertje80 — 2015-06-11T23:02:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167688>

If 11 is your bedtime, you should try to unplug much earlier. Maybe try unplugging at 10. You should not only put your body to rest, but your mind too. If you don't slow your mind down, all the blue blockers in the world ain't gonna help you sleep. Have a transition period between activity and bedtime. Do some light household things or something, no new things, no complex thinking... I love folding laundry at that time. It is really mind numbing hahaha. I don't go at it like crazy though. Just relaxing pace. If I don't finish all the laundry, that is ok too. Or set everything ready for morning ritual. Meditate. Something like that, which requires hardly any light.

There is no must. You need more sleep? Try something to find something that works for you. If you believe you have to be in bed by 9 pm, but can't make it? It will only stress you out. Try to set small goals. If 11 pm is too early now. Try 12 first for some days. And build on that. Going from hardly any sleep till 5 or 6 hours good uninterrupted quality sleep is a major win already. Believe me, I've been there.

### WereBear — 2015-06-12T00:28:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167699>

Remember that the Internet is, for practical purposes, infinite. So don't try to get to the end.

Second, you should put on Amber lenses glasses if you can't quit the screen. Sunset is my salt lamp and sun readers time. No matter what I'm doing.

This, GET A SLEEP MASK. There's one out there for you. It took me six tries, but it was so worth it. Our brains won't get top sleep unless it's total darkness.

Fourthly, set a new goal. Getting great sleep! The first morning you have true, refreshing, rejuvenating sleep it will feel so incredible it will be worth every schedule juggle you have to do to get it.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-12T19:13:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-167843>

Ok, I like all these ideas. I love taking baths and meditating, it's funny how I forget to do these things. Read a book in candle-lit bath. More quality time with my husband - I'm sure he will be on board with that one. =) I often do housework at night, I should try to just do more of that instead of online. I have the feeling that if I start actually waking up early, I will be able to the online reading then. And my brain is so much more efficient after sleeping than when tired. Thanks, everyone!

### WereBear — 2015-06-16T02:58:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-168309>

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=167842)

> Good idea for a sleep mask. My room is pretty dark - there is a streetlamp outside my window, so I have several blankets hooked up. I've never been able to sleep with any light. But some still leaks in here and there. And my kids come in in the middle of the night and people leave the bathroom light on, which wakes me up (I recently changed that fixture to amber bulbs). What sleep masks have you tried? I have a Tempur-pedic one that is super soft, but it's thick and pushes on my nose when I lie on my side. Thanks for the suggestion.

There's a great selection on DreamEssentials.com. I had their Bamboo Breathe for over a year and was very pleased with it. I mainly sleep on my side, so those thick ones don't work.

For the last few months, I've found an even better combination of thinness and light blocking, called the Vibes Up sleep mask. The satiny cover is easy to wash and dries faster than the Bamboo Breathe. (I always get two if I like a style enough.) There's this flexible, lightblocking insert that really puts me in the bottom of a coal mine at midnight! I tried going back to the Bamboo Breathe but I could tell the difference.

That's how sensitive to light I've become, but I appreciate it, because it really has a good effect on a night's sleep.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-17T10:06:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-168554>

so tonight is one of those nights...

Yes, this post is at 2 am.

I had an argument with my husband this evening, the recurring kind where I basically end up feeling like all the problems in his life are my fault. His life would be great if only I did this or didn't do that. After that, I spend a few hours hurting myself with food and wine until I can't feel that pit in my stomach anymore. Then I go to bed and my heart is pounding. I feel like an idiot for so many reasons. So I lie there and try not to think about how sucky this is. I don't want to live like this. Maybe I'm just feeling sorry for myself.

Eventually I get up and do something. Sometimes I write in my (paper) journal, sometimes I cry. Other times I walk around the block or wash dishes. Sometimes I get up and drink some more wine because it did not effectively cause me to pass out earlier. Sometimes I accept that I have a lot of faults and am trying to change so much at once and that I shouldn't be so hard on myself. And I shouldn't blame _him_ for how I react to things and end up awake so late at night.

I don't intend for this to bash my husband. He's a good guy, a really good dad. We just suck at communication. And I need a new coping mechanism because my current one is ruining my health. Ugh.

### Sue-UK — 2015-06-17T10:57:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-168556>

Deverie, "how sucky this is" is probably right ......You've lost electrons, they were sucked or leaked .....or both. As time goes on and you are better able to hold on to the battery charge, the same situation might have unfolded differently, or not at all. If my battery is weak, I am vulnerable, less confident, willing to take the blame for everything... As I get better at holding a charge, I am stronger, less vulnerable, willing to take response - ability for my actions, but I don't take any blame for shit that isn't mine. It has ruffled a few relationships, but the people that matter do get it......

I use a particular meditation sometimes to bring back lost electrons .... Gotta go out, but I can pass it on later if it would help ...

> Yes, this post is at 2 am.
>
> I had an argument with my husband this evening, the recurring kind where I basically end up feeling like all the problems in his life are my fault. His life would be great if only I did this or didn't do that. After that, I spend a few hours hurting myself with food and wine until I can't feel that pit in my stomach anymore. Then I go to bed and my heart is pounding. I feel like an idiot for so many reasons. So I lie there and try not to think about how sucky this is. I don't want to live like this. Maybe I'm just feeling sorry for myself.
>
> Eventually I get up and do something. Sometimes I write in my (paper) journal, sometimes I cry. Other times I walk around the block or wash dishes. Sometimes I get up and drink some more wine because it did not effectively cause me to pass out earlier. Sometimes I accept that I have a lot of faults and am trying to change so much at once and that I shouldn't be so hard on myself. And I shouldn't blame _him_ for how I react to things and end up awake so late at night.
>
> I don't intend for this to bash my husband. He's a good guy, a really good dad. We just suck at communication. And I need a new coping mechanism because my current one is ruining my health. Ugh.

Empathy comes from seeing things from their perspective and not yours. Too often we are wrapped up in ourselves to see others suffer. We must learn to regard people less in the light of what they do or omit to do, and more in the light of what they endure. Sooner or later in life, we will all take our own turn being in the position we once saw someone else in. Empathy however contains a curious balance, help someone, you earn a friend. Help someone too much, you make an enemy.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-18T01:20:24+0300

> Deverie, "how sucky this is" is probably right ......You've lost electrons, they were sucked or leaked .....or both. If my battery is weak, I am vulnerable, less confident, willing to take the blame for everything... As I get better at holding a charge, I am stronger, less vulnerable, willing to take response - ability for my actions, but I don't take any blame for shit that isn't mine. It has ruffled a few relationships, but the people that matter do get it......
>
> I use a particular meditation sometimes to bring back lost electrons .... Gotta go out, but I can pass it on later if it would help ...

### Sue-UK — 2015-06-18T11:32:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-168726>

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=168638)
>
> Your explanation makes sense, my battery is very depleted and I feel vulnerable and defensive all the time. I would love to learn that meditation! Please send it over when you are able. I didn't realize that you can get electrons back by that method. Thank you.

With this variation of the meditation I am not passing judgement, forgiving or being forgiven. It is not about dealing with real trauma .....It is letting go of a particular situation that isn't serving me, sometimes just because it is stopping me sleep. When it comes to the "send love" bit, it can be more about sending love into the universe, if that's more appropriate to the person or situation, but the physiological benefits of the resonance of the word love through my own body is up for grabs.

here goes .....

Drink some water, sit comfortably, take a few deep breaths to relax the diaphragm. With eyes closed, picture yourself sat comfortably, cross legged, or standing upright, surrounded by mirrors. Clothed or naked doesn't matter. The mirrors allow you to see yourself from all angles. As you look you notice what look like thin cords attached to various parts of your body and floating off, disappearing through the mirrors to reappear on the other side on their journey to their source/destination in space and time, the cords being a bit like umbilical cords. They bring in, they take out. Pick (for example) the cord that represents the argument with your husband (just the argument, not your relationship). You have a pair of silver scissors in your hand. Reach round and just before you gently cut the cord, say the words (in your head or out loud)

"I give back to you what is yours. I take back from you what is mine."

Feel the exchange taking place, (the words you didn't deserve going back, (which your DH may wish he could take back anyway), any of his energy you grabbed in the confusion, feel that going back as well as feel the electrons you lost returning), and as you gently cut the cord, send love, and observe the cord floating away. As i become experienced with this, I can change the words slightly to exactly match what I want, (which isn't just to heap what caused the argument back on DH ) but its a place to start. Take a deep breath and then look at another cord. Sometimes I might let my mind follow a cord and see what is at the end of it , sometimes I know already .... But mostly I don't need to look in the rear view mirror .... if something or someone is draining my energy without my consent I can just do the "I give back to you what is yours. I take back from you what is mine...." Cut the cord, draw in love and send love to the universe..... Pick the next one. If the cord from the argument with your husband pops straight back, do it again .....and again if you need to ...When all the cords are dealt with, come back to the moment ....

I am not giving my shit to someone or a situation, but I'm not taking theirs either .....I am not stealing electrons from anyone, but they can't take mine without permission. It doesn't mean I am forgiving an abuse from my past, I don't need to replay the video over and over, I'm just not letting it suck electrons for the rest of my life. I believe its possible to bring electrons back from the past, and this is a way I do it.  A bit of quantum tunnelling through the mirror to a different space and time?  If I can't put right in the present a mistake I've made in the past, I can send a message that I'm sorry ......and send love. If an argument is my fault and DH is asleep, I can say I'm sorry through the cord, cut it, let go and then snuggle up ... It's what I make of it, I can say sorry again in the present in the morning. Its a tool.

Sometimes a particular cord comes back, but (assuming I have chosen not to have say counselling, or let go of a relationship that is constantly sucking my energy), I can go through the sequence again. Sometimes it has given me a series of short term breathing spaces whilst I take the time to make longer term decisions ...... Sometimes it just helps me get to sleep .....

Hope you had a better sleep last night.

### WereBear — 2015-06-19T02:03:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-168826>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=168594)
>
> WereBear, I really would like a sleep mask that doesn't hold in heat. I found a link for the bamboo one:
> [http://www.dreamessentials.com/product-79/bamboo-breathe-eye-mask](http://www.dreamessentials.com/product-79/bamboo-breathe-eye-mask)

[http://www.dreamessentials.com/product-79/bamboo-breathe-eye-mask](http://www.dreamessentials.com/product-79/bamboo-breathe-eye-mask)

That's the one! It's very breathe-able; people in humid climates were especially pleased. It's quite comfortable and thin and soft -- that's what attracted me to it.

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=168594)
>
> When I go on the Vibes Up website, I can only find the Divine mask. Is that what you are talking about?
> [http://vibesup.com/shop/products/Divine-Mask.html](http://vibesup.com/shop/products/Divine-Mask.html)
> It doesn't mention anything about an insert.

That is also the one. When they talk about their special material, that is the insert that is fitting inside the mask and blocks the light so effectively. However, it's not as breathable as the Bamboo Breathe. I'm not in a hot climate and the extra light blocking turned out to be more important to me.

I URGE everyone to consider a sleep mask. So many people say to me, "Yeah, my bedroom is pretty dark," but unless you are in the middle of nowhere and you've pulled the breaker... it's not dark enough.

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=168554)

>
> I had an argument with my husband this evening, the recurring kind where I basically end up feeling like all the problems in his life are my fault. His life would be great if only I did this or didn't do that. After that, I spend a few hours hurting myself with food and wine until I can't feel that pit in my stomach anymore. Then I go to bed and my heart is pounding. I feel like an idiot for so many reasons. So I lie there and try not to think about how sucky this is. I don't want to live like this. Maybe I'm just feeling sorry for myself.
>
> Eventually I get up and do something. Sometimes I write in my (paper) journal, sometimes I cry. Other times I walk around the block or wash dishes. Sometimes I get up and drink some more wine because it did not effectively cause me to pass out earlier. Sometimes I accept that I have a lot of faults and am trying to change so much at once and that I shouldn't be so hard on myself. And I shouldn't blame _him_ for how I react to things and end up awake so late at night.
>
> I don't intend for this to bash my husband. He's a good guy, a really good dad. We just suck at communication. And I need a new coping mechanism because my current one is ruining my health. Ugh.

Many years ago when I first started having problems with depression (probably caused from radar at the airport) I read a book called "Feeling Good" by David Burns. There was a section in there about emotional "distortions" that I found very helpful with communication even to this day. It explains that when you are charged with emotion, and it doesn't matter what type of emotion, we view the world somewhat "distorted" and we can say things that are equally distorted.
It's worth a read anyway, even if it's just that one chapter.

### Peertje80 — 2015-06-23T07:33:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-169313>

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-23T07:55:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-169316>

### Lahelada — 2015-06-23T12:53:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-169327>

Deverie,if you have carb cravings it could simply mean that your Bab is not big enough. That little lo-cal lo fat voice has been singing for a long time and it takes time to chuck it out of the choir. Your Bab should be big enough for you not to want anything at all straight after,not even a delicious cream cake.The fullness should border on if I eat one bite more I will bust my pants.If you can eat it comfortably you are probably not eating enough. I hope I described this feeling sufficiently well

### caroline — 2015-06-23T13:38:57+0300

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-25T00:15:12+0300

Oh my goodness! My BAB probably is not big enough! I started my BAB in early May, eating 3 eggs in coconut oil + bacon fat, 3 pcs bacon, 4 sea scallops in butter = 54 g protein. Then I increased that a bit last week so now I'm up to 70 g protein (+ butter, coconut oil, and bacon fat). But, you're right, I'm not totally stuffed. I feel like it's a good breakfast and I'm able to be physical at my job an hour later and I'm not super hungry for lunch. I'm nervous that if I eat more, I will be so stuffed that I won't be able to work! Perhaps as I shift my schedule, I will get up earlier and be able to do CT after my BAB and digest a little more before work.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-06-30T02:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-169983>

I'm ready to jump into the Leptin reset. I've been piece-mealing it and I think I need to go all-in. Jack said I needed to work on my sleep first and foremost, so I've been getting better at that over the past 2 months. I eat seafood once or twice daily. Water intake is variable, minimum 3 L, more when at work. AM sunlight and grounding (barefoot in the grass) plus more outside time in general. Blue blockers on in the evening, replaced lights w/amber bulbs, reducing all the nnEMF (smart meter shields, grounding pads, etc.). I've been dabbling in CT for a while now, nothing consistent, but I love cold and it's not too difficult for me.

I really need to set a schedule for myself or my days will disappear. I work 3 days a week so it looks like I can have a schedule #1 for work days and schedule #2 for non-work days.

Is it effective if I only do CT four days a week?

I have most of the Leptin Rx supplements and I'm not sure when to take them. (And yes, I understand that supplementation is just for the reset and that eventually my body will be better able to get the nutrients from food and I won't need all this - I am looking forward to it.) This is what I have so far (see my questions in bold):

1.  DHA – I use Green Pastures fermented cod liver oil (it tastes SO disgusting but I feel really good later) - I take with lunch or dinner
2.  Iodine – 3 drops Lugol’s 2% – take w/selenium 200 mcgs/day (or 1 Brazil nut) - morning
3.  Iron – 325 mgs/day – take w/vitamin C 1000 mg - lunch
4.  B-complex (Thorne w/folate not folic acid!!) – take with SAMe 400mg **- lunch?**
5.  Vitamin D3 – 10,000 IU/day, sometimes I take less if I've had mid-day sunshine (here in WI we can only get Vit D from the sun from 10-3:30 at this time) **\- I don't think it matters when I take this?**
6.  K2 (Thorne MK-4 not MK-7) – 4 mg/day **\- breakfast?**
7.  PQQ – 20 mg/day - **when?**
8.  CoQ10 (Ubiquinol not Ubiquinone) 100 mg - **when?**
9.  Magnesium - I currently take 400-600 mg magnesium citrate at night. I want to switch to L-threonate and someone recommended to take 2 capsules mid day and 2 capsules 2 hrs before bed (totaling 2000 mg mag threonate = 144 mg magnesium) **\- does this sound good? does this replace the mag citrate? I'm afraid it won't be enough (when I don't take any other supplements, this is the one I never miss).**
10.  MSM (2000 mg) - **when?**
11.  Epsom salt baths - I take one or two a week in the evening
12.  Multivitamin **- I have been taking Rainbow Light prenatal, but just noticed it has folic acid not folate, so I need to find a different one - suggestions?**
13.  DHEA - I have the regular version (5 mg) and I just ordered the 7 keto version (30 mg) - **take in morning?**
14.  Melatonin (2.5 mg) **– take 2 hrs before bed? with the magnesium l-threonate?**

Am I missing anything essential?
Thank you in advance for any input on this!!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-27T01:09:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-173155>

Although the accumulation of excess body fat is a complex process with many variables, one variable could be light effect on circadian rhythms. Studies have shown that prolonged dailylight exposure (beyond the normal daily exposure we experience) can increase fat mass through an increase in adipocyte or fat cell size.

Kooijman et al. (2015) conducted a study where three groups of rats were placed under three different daily light conditions to test the effects on fat mass. Rats were exposed to 12, 16, and 24 hours of light each day while caloric intake remained constant. Kooijman discovered that rats exposed to 16 and 24 hours of light had an increase in fat mass despite there being no change in lean mass. Kooijman explained that this was likely due to the lights effects on circadian rhythms.

Circadian rhythms have been known to regulate a wide variety of physiological as well as metabolic processes in the body. These processes range from digestion and hormone release, to the onset of sleep and eating patterns.

While natural light during normal hours of the day is benefical, we should aim to decrease our exposure to artifical light at night, which most of us now get via the use of computers, TVs, cell phones, and tablets.

I've been really liking sitting outside in the evening in natural transitional light. My husband and I get so sleepy that we fall asleep on the patio sofa!

### thisbirdhaswings — 2015-08-06T03:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-174057>

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=173616)
>
> I've been going to bed pretty early (in the 11:00 range, which is awesome for me!!) and I am tired, but when I lie down, I can't fall asleep. I journal or read or meditate or engage w/my BOB/husband, but any of that doesn't necessarily help. If I take melatonin, the problem seems to be worse! What's going on? If I just go to bed later, I seem to fall asleep easier. My room is very dark, I wear a sleep mask. There is an alarm clock across the room with red numbers. Is my body just needing time to adapt to my new routine?
>
> I wear blue-blockers in the evening. I use amber lenses after 7:00 and then red DeWalts after 9:00. If I am still on the computer or watching TV, my eyes might be protected, but does the blue light affect my skin? Could that be enough to keep me awake?

How about dinner? Is it happening 4-5 hours before bed? (finished by 6 or 7 if trying to go to bed by 11?).

Another thing that helps me if I'm laying awake for more than a few minutes: doing a few minutes of exercise. I just do wall push-ups and/or squats and it seems to do the trick.

Finally, if my head is racing, I use an old energy medicine trick: hand on forehead to bring blood to the front of the brain. That usually settles down the restlessness

### thisbirdhaswings — 2015-08-06T03:47:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-174058>

> [Deverie\_Night Owl said:](/goto/post?id=174049)
>
> I was awakened by my daughter at 3:00 am last night. I took her to use the bathroom, and on the way I peeked into our living room and it was really bright in there. All kinds of little lights blinking and flashing off all those devices. EMF mess in there. I didn't put my DeWalts on when I got up to get her, so all those lights were obvious. I laid in bed and couldn't fall asleep for an hour after that. My head is spinning with all the EMF remediation I need to do in that room! And I probably f\*ed up my sleep with all that light. Bummer.

Think of each thing that happens like this as information...neither good or bad...just information. Then use the information to adjust. Consider that before becoming more aware you wouldn't have even noticed how bright the lights were or made the connection between the artificial lights (and EMF) and the quality of your sleep that night. Funny how what looks like a setback could even be considered a step forward.

### Scompy — 2015-08-15T04:23:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-174711>

Unless you have an EMF Rf meter, you might be surprised to find out that some devices still emit a Wifi signal even when it is TURNED OFF! For example, the Xbox360 continually searches for wireless controllers even if it's still plugged in and Xbox itself is turned off! I was flabbergasted to see that with my own eyes and my own meter. So I highly recommend physically unplug some of the devices that could emit an RF signal. Airplane mode on phones appear to work fine, but you need to know what you're doing to be sure. I'd suspect that some laptops might continually search for a signal even if you have an ethernet cord to it. The only way to know is with a meter.

This is more than losing weight. This is saving my life. This is pulling me back from the edge that I wanted to leap off of last fall. This is giving me hope for a future, for a long life with lots of time do what I need to do and want to do.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-10-19T06:34:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-179840>

I started doing keto/epi-paleo diet with a couple of friends on Sept 1. I felt really great for 5 weeks! I realize that I am not yet an optimal "fat-burner" and am using more protein to transition from sugar-burning. Anyway, it was helpful to start off with other people. They didn't do it very long, for their own reasons.  But it was a great jumping-off point for me. I really needed an impetus to get my butt in gear. I coupled this with more/better sleep. I'm still learning to sleep more, still struggling a little to transition from doing my work at night to doing it in the morning.

I've had a stressful time the past two weeks. I hired a new employee in August and after 5 weeks of training, I had to fire her. I am such a people-pleaser and I really want to believe the best about people; I just kept giving her more chances when I knew within the first few days that she wasn't the right fit. In fact, she sucked. She messed up my books. I've spent the past two weeks fixing all of her mistakes. I've had to be in front of the computer many more hours per day and that pisses me off since I'm trying to spend less time there.

On top of that, my other employee gave me her 2 weeks notice. She's been with us for a year and I knew she was over-qualified when I hired her, and that she would move on eventually. I'm just disappointed in the timing of it, since she had been training the new employees and I was hoping she would continue.

My husband and I are both chiropractors and have our own office. We now need to hire two assistants and I get to do all the training. The past two weeks I have been stressed out. But now I'm starting to look at it as an opportunity to get some fresh energy in the place! I'm excited for a positive change.

I've always liked change. It's scary, but exciting. The more I travel on this road to optimal health, the more I'm overwhelmed with how much more changing I need to do in my own life. It's really holding up a mirror to me, making me look at myself, how I think, how I react, how I don't act.

Lots of wine last week plus extra blue light toxicity gave me some sniffles this weekend. I need to get more CT in. CT is not regular in my schedule yet, and I see that it is the first thing to drop out when I get stressed out. That definitely needs to change!

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-10-19T06:47:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-179842>

I want to change my schedule. I'm in a rut and I can't see my way out of it. Maybe you all can help. =)

I'm self-employed, so I really can set my own schedule. (It's a huge blessing and I am grateful!!) Here's my current schedule:

I work short days MW (9-1) and I pick up my kids from school those days. I work long days TTh (9-6) and my husband gets the kids those days. I don't work on Fridays but run errands, catch up on stuff at the office, and sometimes do self-care (get a massage!) and I pick up the kids that day, too.

So my long days, I work 9-1, lunch break 1-3, then see patients 3-6. Since I'm doing the BAB, I rarely am hungry before 2 or 2: 30, so I usually eat a rushed meal at 2: 30 just to make sure I eat. One day I pushed it back and ate an early dinner when I had a small break at 4. That was great! I'd love to eat at that time all the time, but don't think I could see patients full throttle from 9-4. When I get home, sometimes my husband cooks delicious food and I feel compelled to eat it since he's really proud of it and I'm grateful that he put the effort into it. It's often after 7, though, so I need to make it clear to him (and to myself) that that is too late to eat. Working on that.

My short days, I work 9-1 and get the kids at 2: 30. Sometimes I eat before then and sometimes around 3. Then I make dinner for everyone else and I usually end up eating again at 6: 30-7. I want to stop doing that, it's really too late. I've always had trouble listening to my body's signals, so I'm not actually sure if I'm hungry or tired or just programmed to eat. I'm learning to listen.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2015-10-19T07:01:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-179843>

I love how some people's journals are so introspective. I feel so mechanical in comparison. I'm just trying to get to bed, get all my tasks done, eat the right stuff, mitigate my crap environment, make sure my kids are still alive. I feel like there's no time to contemplate, there's no time to help my kids thrive. This is not how I want it to be.

I'm not even "in" my body. I'm so disconnected. I think that has been part of my problem the last 5 years. I don't look in a full-length mirror. EVER. I look at my head in the bathroom mirror, and I'm like "that looks ok" and I ignore that giant ass shaking behind me. I don't hate my body. I don't even know my body. So I'm trying to connect my brain and my body. I spent some time naked in front of the mirror. I took a bunch of photos of myself. First, wow, I didn't even realize I looked like that. I was horrified. I used to be so sexy. So then, I decided, I am still sexy in my head, and took some more photos with a different light. That's better.

I can't bring myself to be naked outside in the morning. I wear a thin robe and quickly flash the sun. But I realized that my thoughts were along the lines of "I don't want to disgust the neighbors." How awful is that! I don't want to think that about myself.

Worse yet, I don't want my daughter to remember me this way. This is not the legacy I want to leave.

### thisbirdhaswings — 2015-10-19T16:22:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-179862>

Change yourself first. Let the ones who are resistant come around on their own as they watch you change or sink their own ship.

The stress of your own personal changing will fade in time as you tap into greater energy reserves. Life will still be stormy, but you'll be more like the tree that is deeply rooted in the earth that is strengthened by those storms rather than knocked down because of them.

As for scheduling, remember that the greatest productivity is found in blocks of 90-120 minutes. Try to schedule a break for yourself (even a short one) at least that often. As you train your new employees, you might consider building breaks into their days as well, to help them be happier (and more productive). You can buid these principles into your office routines as well as your personal ones.

You're on the right track. Just keep going.

### Scompy — 2015-10-19T19:41:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-179881>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=179866)
>
> My family says the same thing and now that the DD is a nurse she is brainwashed into the traditional medicine teachings. I just say if you don't want to learn about what I am doing don't criticize and just let me do what I need to do.

This topic relates to me, it's a game that's needs to be played CORRECTLY. This video explains it all. Read between the lines.

When a person comes to expect you to talk about highly-detailed, seemingly-crazy-to-the-layman stuff, always think about STEALTH TACTICS, especially WAITING and GAUGING! This is key!

Take aways I've learned:
1\. You're always solving a puzzle to figure out the best timing for introducing the info.
2\. One's significant other and close friends that I've shared this information with will come to expect me to go into deep, uncomprehendable territory they don't understand because they simply lack the knowledge base. Plus, even when trying to explain stuff in laymen's terms, they usually don't see the significance, let alone believe it to be a possibility unless a meter is shoved in front of them: i.e., they'll slowly label you as nutty if you VOLUNTEER information too much (often for the benefit of their own lives, right?!)
3\. Maximize waiting and gauging for the perfect moment to introduce something, and just implement things without asking if you have to. Don't mention why you're doing it. Just do it if it has little impact on the person/people you live with. If they
4\. Be more silent like a ninja. Don't ever start up conversations about it unless there's a great transition.
5\. Just stealth-install and implement things to help YOUR LIFE rather than trying to CHANGE THEIR LIVES.  Over time, if others see the positive changes in YOUR LIFE, they'll become even more curious and probably want to CHANGE THEIR LIVES.
6\. Just be matter-of-fact about it all, being an emotionless-stealth-ninja. The more emotion that plays into decision-making, the less an onlooker will think you're living in reality. Based the decisions on matter-of-fact science and show that you're willing to change when the science appears to be faulty. Like it's easy to pop on some 'amber glasses' in the evening without it influencing anyone else. Tell others you're trying them out for the next two months to see if it has any benefits on sleep and health for yourself, and say, "I'll get back to you in 2 months to see if anything has improved."

It's easy to personally-mitigate with little impact on others you live with and when they see positive changes, they'll be begging you for answers in the long run. The less you share in the short term probably works out for the best, and if you're already in the pattern of giving an entire knowledge dump onto another person on a common basis, back off. Be polite and don't do it in general unless asked, and when you present, also ask the other, "Do you want a simple answer or a more in-depth answer?" It's VERY HARD for the detailed thinker to try to keep conversations short when many of these topics can be endless.

### Deverie_Night Owl — 2016-03-14T16:46:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-190825>

I'M PREGNANT!!!!!! Holy shit, I didn't think it was going to happen again!!! I have to say a big THANK YOU to Dr. Jack Kruse.

In the fall, I was pretty sure I wasn't ovulating, and verified such with ovulation strips. I wasn't trying to get pregnant or anything, I just want my body to work correctly. So I went hardcore leptin reset using "epi-keto" with a concentrated effort on fixing my light cycles and thus fix my messed up hormones. After four months I was ovulating normally with a normal length cycle and BAM! BABY! Apparently we had been using my PCOS as birth control for 5 years.

WOW. I'm very excited for Baby #3, due Sept 15. I'm also a little nervous, since I was hoping to lose more weight (50 lbs down would have been nice). Now I have the pleasure of worrying how my high inflammation is affecting this baby. Due to the lovely pregnancy hormones, I've been quite nauseous, not able to eat as much seafood (down from 10 meals per week to about 2 meals per week), eating more grains, and not doing any CT!! I'm disappointed in that. On the positive side, I'm sleeping so much more and I feel such ease in saying I'm not a night owl any more!!! I get so tired, I just want to sleeeeeep.

I've been off of this forum for awhile because it seemed counter-productive to me to spend so much time on the internet while trying to reduce my blue-light and other EMF exposure. But I wanted to share my good news (and I needed to change my avatar since I don't wear my contact lenses AT ALL anymore). Thanks for reading my update!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-16T21:04:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/journey-away-from-night-time-destruction.15133/post-190999>

Deverie quantum hint for you........N/V comes because your baby is trying to shrink the respiratory proteins the baby got from you to match the environment it senses from your blood plasma.........so the baby makes you puke because it cells sense the geometry of the respiratory proteins are too spread out to match the nuclear genome it has in their cells........why does it do this?

Your babies cells know one of the best ways to reduce the geometry on the respiratory proteins is to fast. When you get nauseous you dont eat and this allows the baby to perform some heteroplasmy reduction because it inherited its bad mitochondrial DNA form MOM only. This is why morning sickness is a signal to me about Mom's environment.............Also tells me the baby is dialed in as it grows. Do better........is the message from the fetus. FYI.

Congrats.

I was usually sound asleep on the floor first.

Aaaack!! Of course, my nausea was a sign of a problem with my environment! I should know better. I just thought it was a normal pregnancy thing, but your explanation is very informative, thanks Jack. I'm sure my mitochondrial DNA is crap. Poor baby. Although, I had been doing a few things to make it better last summer/fall, but I fear it was not enough for long enough.

I guess I went back to making excuses for myself. "Because" I felt ill, I had to stay up late and eat a snack. "Late" now means 10 pm and not 2 am (thank goodness!) but still, I shouldn't be eating then. I was awake later than I should be in the winter months! It helps that the sun is finally shining...damn Wisconsin.

Ok, no more excuses. Back to CT, morning sun, and seafood!! (And I'm working on my husband to move because we live very near an airport and are 1/2 mile west of the flight path - that can't be good!!)

# Thread: Progesterone In Men
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Bill1 — 2015-06-06T01:55:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-166719>

Not on any BHRT or supplements - just wondering how or why? would progesterone in a man be extremely high? Saliva testing revealed this:

**Progesterone 9899 pg/ml**

range for men on this test is: anything under 94 pg/ml

Saliva E2 was 1.26 pg/ml - normal is anything under 2.5 pg/ml

saliva cortisol - ok WNL

saliva Test 153 pg/ml

Did suffer a mild TBI a few months ago - MVA related. Any effect?

How in the world do you **lower** pregesterone in men?

Um? Very strange? Any help would be great - thanks

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-06T02:05:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-166720>

that test must be repeated......its a likely lab error.

### Bill1 — 2015-06-06T02:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-166721>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=166720)
>
> that test must be repeated......its a likely lab error.

thanks - it was repeated three times and serum was also elevated.

My doctor uses the best lab for saliva testing - LABRIX. The only lab with the most up to date equipment.

### Bill1 — 2015-06-06T02:11:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-166723>

Journal of Neurotrauma

Acute Serum Hormone Levels: Characterization and Prognosis after Severe Traumatic Brain Injury

[http://online.liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/neu.2010.1586?journalCode=neu](http://online.liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/neu.2010.1586?journalCode=neu)&

### JanSz — 2015-06-06T02:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-166724>

> [Bill1 said:](/goto/post?id=166723)
>
> Journal of Neurotrauma
>
> Acute Serum Hormone Levels: Characterization and Prognosis after Severe Traumatic Brain Injury
>
>
> [http://online.liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/neu.2010.1586?journalCode=neu](http://online.liebertpub.com/doi/abs/10.1089/neu.2010.1586?journalCode=neu)&

Note that the hormone discussed in above article were tested in serum,
saliva test may be good for cortisol only.

"Serum samples (_n_\=536) were collected for 7 days post-TBI for cortisol, progesterone, testosterone, estradiol, luteinizing hormone (LH), and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH)."

//
Also get interested in

GH
IGF-1
IGFBP3

Likely all other pituitary hormone.

..

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T05:46:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-172397>

The binding of progesterone to its receptor allows for a conformational change which promotes binding to chromatin, and leads to gene transcription and protein synthesis. The Ubi series shows you how incredibly important the interaction of light is on proteins. The maintenance of high levels of progesterone is also crucial for the maintenance of pregnancy primarily because pregnancy is very tied to excess protein synthesis and progesterone is critical in this process. When progesterone is low it tells me ubiquitination rates are unsustainable and the environment is toxic and the result is usually a severe dehydrated state. Dehydration and high BUN creat ratios usually are seen with low progesterone levels.

The progesterone receptor is key to chromatin physiology. The biological importance of the progesterone receptor is that it allows for gene transcription and protein synthesis. It undergoes a conformational change with the binding of progesterone in the nucleus, that allows for **_chromatin binding_** and subsequently gene transcription and protein synthesis.

**_Quote from Ubi 12, "excessive nitrogen increases ubiquitination" In humans NAD_**

DNA only codes for proteins. All proteins contain nitrogen. Nucleic acids and vitamin D3 are low in nitrogen for a key reason tied to LIGHT. This makes them very sensitive to light's action. No one seems to realize this. This means the low end of the electromagnetic spectrum is critical to these two classes of biologic molecules. When your environment is loaded with higher non native parts of the electromagnetic spectrum it has dramatic affects on DNA and vitamin D levels. This is why the current low Vitamin D pandemic is a huge signal to humans. Right now they do not understand how technology is destroying our biology. In the case or proteins, molecules owe their stability to the same intermolecular forces that bind crystals that are not living. Van der Waal forces are weak electromagnetic interactions that control this process. In biotic systems, very weak forces are capable of holding DNA together. This is true of all proteins and cell membranes. T_he interesting part of this relationship, is because these electromagnetic forces are weak by design_, it allows these structures to change conformation in order to adapt their functions based upon the environments they encounter. **_This is why Vitamin D and DNA have very little nitrogen_**. This is the essence of epigenetics built into DNA and RNA.

Let us look at this really carefully. I am very detailed oriented in the Ubi series but people reading this series do not yet know the details or know them well. The more you realize what light and nitrogen do the more you'll understand how steroids really work. From Ubi 12 again: The link between chromatin light nitrogen and progesterone all link together. Consider when most illness begin, they are associated with excessive amounts of superoxide levels, but this happens over a short timescale; There is a huge difference between acute and chronic pseudo-hypoxia. NAD+ is where eukaryotes handle their nitrogen coupling system at cytochrome 1. NAD+ and SIRT1 availability play key roles in both acute and chronic inflammation. You need both present to regulate inflammation in a cell. In aging and illness, NAD+ is missing in action. NAD+ is the key source of nitrogen for cytochrome 1.
In acute inflammation, **chromatin departs from**, and returns to, homeostasis in an orderly sequence (spatially and temporally). This sequence depends on shifts in NAD+ availability for SIRT1 activation and deacetylation of signaling proteins, which support orderly gene reprogramming (epigenetics) during acute inflammation by switching between **euchromatin and heterochromatin.** Remember **chromatin change** determine if genes are expressed or repressed. In contrast, in chronic inflammation and cancer, limited availability of NAD+ and reduced expression of SIRT1 may sustain **aberrant chromatin** structure and functions.
Chronic low O2 the levels (think OSA) decay into a lack of superoxide pulses from cytochromes in mitochondria and directly alter epigenetic expression via changes in the size and shape of chromatin. This happens when mitochondria become pseudohypoxic and make little O2 from electron chain transport. Here is where [David Sinclair’s paper](http://www.cell.com/abstract/S0092-8674\(13\)01521-3) in 2013 becomes extremely important. It links all chronic disease generation and aging to nitrogen uncoupling.

**Nitrogen atoms represent 10-15% of the dry mass of our muscles and four nitrogen atoms cradle each rust colored iron atom in hemoglobin. And people wonder why fibromyalgia is now exploding. It makes 100% quantum sense when you see how nitrogen works. Four nitrogen atoms from the chlorin surround and bind the magnesium atom in chlorophyll. Nitrogen is always close to the action that makes life happen.**

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T05:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-172398>

SIRT 1 allows for deacetylation (carbon removal) to occur to inactivate the ubiquitin marking process in proteins in cells undergoing “carbon stress” from pro inflammatory or pro growth signaling. Acetylation is a synonym for additions of carbon to a protein to stabilize it or to eventually replace it. Acetylation of proteins in our cells means ubiquitin marking will continue to occur, and as a result, will continue to cost the cell a lot of energy. _Acetylation is one of the more ancient pathways used to move carbon in living things_. SIRT 1 is capable of altering the flow of carbon replacement in humans. SIRT 1 gains this control by being directly coupled to NAD+ function in cytochrome 1 of your mitochondria. This links the protein synthetic machinery directly to the respiratory complexes. NAD+ function is highly dependent upon the sirtuin presence or absence.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T05:55:31+0300

### Jack Kruse — 2015-07-20T05:55:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/progesterone-in-men.15134/post-172400>

_Protein turnover is a synonym for elevated ubiquitin marking._ Recall, that eukaryotes spend 80% of their total energy budget on protein synthesis. High ubiquitin rates, lower the redox potential by lowering the net negative charge in a cell faster than any other process in biology.

# Thread: "A journey of a thousand miles begins with one step."
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 54
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Brave New World — 2015-06-07T06:29:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166909>

Where to begin? I've been perusing these forums for several weeks now and have thumbed through Epi Paleo RX from time to time and it is on this day, whilst struggling with suicidal thoughts that I finally have decided to admit that perhaps I am not up to the task of solving my health on my own. Were I cognitively sound, I'd be in a better place, but my head has been heavy with fog of late. So here I am.

First I'd like to thank everyone willing to read through what is about to become a huge wall of text and for any and all insight I may glean from this.

To start I have struggled with depression/anxiety/suicidal ideation since I was very young. Perhaps 7 or 8. This was of course hard enough, but now I am in a bit more complex of a situation, and one that I still fail to fully fathom even now after four years of it. Since about four years ago I've been dealing with a conga line of fluctuating and evolving symptoms beginning first with abdominal pain and inflammation compounded with fairly severe joint pain. (Though now I suspect the terrible acne I developed before this was likely the first indicator of something awry besides the depression and other things) This was misdiagnosed as crown's, which while not necessarily an important fact seeing as it was a gross misdiagnosis, I mention it because the inflammation I had in the ileum was fairly significant and I began to search for good health based on that diagnosis for some time. It wasn't until I awoke one day to a very unfamiliar kind of fatigue that I really became worried. It was incredibly pervasive and unlike anything I had ever experienced. I struggled to go up and down stairs or to even stand and spent at least a month or two living on the couch. Unfortunately for my health I ended up watching television during the night and sleeping during the day for those two months until finally getting back on a daylight schedule and attempting to address things dietarily. I tried the SCD diet with some success, but was never incredibly compliant for long often eating things that while within the confines of the protocol were not necessarily ideal. A lot of my symptoms of pain both abdominal and joint related remitted to a great degree but the brain fog (I'm incredibly intelligent and even functioning at low capacity, people can see that, but it's almost like I have Alzheimers at times making all the IQ in the world of little worth) and fatigue remained fairly heavily. I've also experienced various shooting pains and other things indicative of issues with nerves. My walking and movement in general is no where near as fluid or coordinated as they have been in the past.

Jumping ahead after fluctuations of symptoms and limited success with SCD/GAPS type diets, I began to read more about health from various biohacking blogs: Dave Asprey's stuff, Joe Cohen, Ben Greenfield, and of course Jack Kruse's. I have decided based upon my reading of these to implement the following:

\-The Terry Wahl's Protocol for diet (the most strict ketogenic version which is pretty damn close to exactly what Jack seems to advocate for, only lacking perhaps with the emphasis on seafood although there is seaweed and fish)
\-Ideally a strict and consistent sleep/wake schedule with adequate signaling from the sun in the morning and limited exposure to blue lighting... this has been struggle to say the least
\-Limiting usage of electronics which has been easier than I had thought, my phone being the last major bastion of heavy usage. I hardly am on my computer and only watch television for maybe 2-3 hours a week at most. (Game of thrones is primarily to blame) I prefer books but since having developed brain fog my usual 1 book a week that I used to do is now more like 2-3 books a year
\-Some supplementation which I will list in detail later.
\-The Wim Hof Method (guy is a total badass and I'd recommend getting the online video guide he has)
\-Heart Math's hear rate variability training
\-Meditation as soon as I find a good resource on how to do it and find which makes the most sense
\-Spending time outside as often as possible especially when sunny
\-Grounding at least some time during the day
\-Avoiding alcohol (easy for me), Nicotine (struggled with gum for a bit but over that now), and caffeine (a work in progress! It's hard as I am a barista currently with a passion for tea and coffee)
\-Brain training of various sorts such as mnemonics and Dual N-Back to help my brain practice functioning as I try to improve the physical health of it
\-Reading books again despite it being a struggle
\-Drinking Spring water (Usually Poland Spring... I tried many and found that my body seemed to like his one most which isn't scientific but some made me feel ill) and Mineral Water only (hopefully San Pellegrino is okay?)
\-Turning off wifi and breaker to my room which is likely of limited help as I am in an apartment on the second floor but it's better than nothing. Looking forward to more insight on the EMF thing. Not sure how nuts I can afford to go with my current situation but I'm willing to do what I can. My goal now is to find some way to mitigate the effects via shielding for at least while I sleep.
\-I plan to take daily measures such as HRV and pulse/oxygenation, but have yet to organize that (or much of any of this really)
\-Limiting exercise! I get to feeling a bit better and increase my activity tremendously only to come crashing down
\-And lastly, this being the hardest for me now because of how depressing my situation is as well as how difficult it can be at times to do anything: trying to get out and live my life, spend time with friends, and maybe pursue a love interest although that to me is unwise given my current situation, which is unfortunate because I think I am in love

Here are the supplements I am taking: EXOS Multivitamin Elite, DHEA 10 mg a day (levels were less than 1/3 the optimal amount for my age), Advanced Research membrane complex, quercertin 500mg once daily, L-Theanine 200 mg as needed for anxiety/focus/sleep, Astragulus andrographis and olive leaf, vitamin D3 on days I don't get sun, Jarrow's Selenium Synergy once daily, Fish oil with high levels of DHA (may switch to krill or something else.... need to research more), Organic broken cell wall chlorella, 1000mg 5-HTP to help sleep on occasion, Black Cumin Seed oil, magnesium calm, and various probiotics. I will likely add in pregnenolone as soon as I test for it. It seems to help the few times I have messed with low doses.

Some oddities and insights that I have gained so far:

I think at this point "adrenal fatigue" fits the most out of everything I have seen and is likely the result of circadian mismatch and leaky gut from tons of antibiotics. Seems to explain my digestive issues and food sensitivities as well as the odd times I am wired and odd times I am fatigued and foggy. I can pretty much go down the line on websites that detail the symptoms and with few exceptions it seems exactly like me whereas other potential theories only fit partly with glaring holes. One thing that doesn't fit as far as I can tell is that I am lean and carry quite a bit of muscle mass. My resilience to cold is also pretty good but can be variable, and I have very poor resistance to heat. I've done a lot to attempt to boost mitochondrial function but have seen very limited improvement from any of it leading me to believe either I am missing a huge factor or that my core issue is more along the lines of signaling of hormones in my body. I didn't even notice much from 100mg PQQ with ubiquinol and 20mg sublingual NADH. I noticed nothing with power amounts of those over a month period. When I have days that I am function fairly well, or am hiked up on caffeine/sugar, I am incredibly strong and my brain simply flies in comparison to the norm. I would say I still have fogginess and fatigue but not nearly as bad though they quickly return and often with a vengeance.

Anyways to sum things up, I'm really hoping for some ideas on what else I can do, what I am doing incorrectly, and perhaps some novel ways to address EMF. (Shielding ideas? calcium blocking substances? I also question how much of a factor it is, though I am sure with my current state it is more of one than it would otherwise be were I generally healthy)

Lastly I want to say that I chose this community to first reach out to first because it seemed the friendliest and most willing to help with far less vitriol and pride than some the others out there. So thanks for keeping things friendly and approachable.

Here's to hoping I can start to kick life's ass in a big way and find some happiness in this journey.
Thanks and God bless,
Nicholas

P.S. I sincerely apologize for my tendency to be most verbose, and once again am thankful for anyone who tackles this with me.

### Brave New World — 2015-06-07T06:38:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166910>

One quick addition! Here are some blood markers from a panel I did last year about this time. I wish I had a recent one but this was comprehensive and I have yet to be able to do it again.

My HS CRP is nearly zero as are other inflammation indicators.
B12 was 778 last I checked (some time ago)
Folate was 6.4
Total Cholesterol was low at 143 (yikes!)
LDL 74
HDL 63
Trigyceride 54
Vitamin D 41
Cortisol at 9:30 that day was 15.7 but I'd like to do the more comprehensive spit test
T4 5.3
T3 1.1
Free T4 1.0
Free T3 2.8
TSH 0.7
Total bilirubin is always high. It was 2.8 on this panel I'm reading off of... I can dig for more of the same in my pile of tests
Sodium was marked as low on the sheet at 135
Bun was 17
Creatinine was 0.9

If it is absolutely necessary to get any more tests or to retest any of this, or if there is something else that may be of use please let me know. I'm reliant on help from my dad for paying for much of anything as I can only manage part time work at the moment and this is all very expensive so some things may not be possible.

### caroline — 2015-06-07T11:41:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166917>

Hi Nicholas and thanks for starting a journal .......

where do you live? your postcode matters huge!

Are you eating seafood? you need lots and lots of oysters etc. etc. etc. This is extremely important.

What is the health history of your family? siblings?
Please limit exercise for now.

Get outside as much as possible, with as little clothes as possible!

Avoid fake lite ..... do you have blue blocker glasses?

Upon rising - go outside for a while

Have you read the C/T protocol? there is an easy start guide.

Welcome Nicholas Looks like you have a good plan to re establish your health. Keep it up!

### PaulG — 2015-06-07T12:07:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166920>

G'day Nicholas and welcome from down under - you are doing some great things to improve your health = keep at it - try to record the things that make a difference and improve your mental and physical well-being and turn them into regular habits. Also notice what gets you down - keep a detailed diary and include activities, food, mood etc. Cut out any bad stuff as much as possible. Drop the Wahls protocol - go full in Epi-Paleo and use the healing frequencies of the sun. Happy to help if I can.

### fitness@home — 2015-06-07T14:39:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166926>

Just a thought... Severe joint pain and a history of antibiotics. Was Cipro (or any other fluoroquinolones) precribed in your past?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-07T16:46:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166928>

Nick with that story.........the one big miss in your plan is doing a Wahl's style diet without with a ton of seafood. Supplements can never equal seafood. That history tells me you have a pan body or pan tissue wide lack of DHA. The second question is I want to know a lot more about your mom, her life when she was pregnant with you and what her environment was like during that time. Third thing.........issues about your grandmother.........I have a sense you have mitonuclear dys synergy. If I am correct CT will have big benefits for you. I have a sense your altered course began with bad mitochondrial DNA.

> Thanks and God bless,[http://v6.player.abacast.net/2095](http://v6.player.abacast.net/2095)
> Nicholas
>
> P.S. I sincerely apologize for my tendency to be most verbose, and once again am thankful for anyone who tackles this with me.

Welcome. There is hope here, but more than hope the possibility for real change. My heartfelt suggestion: pick the top three things to work on (the 20% that is going to give the 80% result) and forget the rest of the list for now. Use that success to build momentum, rather than making more work for yourself in the beginning and getting stopped in your tracks by the enormity of it all.

### Brave New World — 2015-06-07T18:02:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-journey-of-a-thousand-miles-begins-with-one-step.15180/post-166937>

Cipro was prescribed once after I got sick and I took one pill then tried to throw it up after reading about it. Damn scary stuff. Didn't take the rest of course. The others were just a few different oral antibiotics for acne. There was one that was making me really sick at one point but I don't recall the name. Hopefully it wasn't another in the same class as cipro.

So is Wahls fine so in general so long as there is an emphasis on seaweed, fish bone broths/soups, and oysters (which i got poisoning from and it will be hard to start that journey again)?

Unfortunately my mother stopped breast feeding me fairly quickly and gave me formula which my body rejected so they then put me on soy formula which I'm sure was much worse. I'll have to ask about during the pregnancy. I know she ate spicy foods but I'm sure you're asking more about environmental aspects like EMF, location, sun exposure, toxin exposure, etc. Will update ASAP on that.

She has generalized fatigue as well as depression and has been on antidepressants. I don't know that she was on them during her pregnancy with me, but she certainly was during my three siblings pregnancy, all of them younger and none of them with as bad of health as me. Though they certainly aren't in the greatest health either. I too was on a cornucopia of antidepressants off and on for years and a few times stimulants (thanks again mom and dad!). I've been off them for probably 6 years. I don't think going off them was very close to the time this all got triggered but it seems relatively close. Kind of doubtful it's very much related but I'm not sure.

Grandmother on mothers side seems fine. She's healthy as far as I can tell. Probably has more general energy than myself a lot of days.

What would constitute a "mitonuclear dys synergy"? Is there much hope if the mitochondrial genetics suck? 23andMe a good idea with regard to this?

I will say cold thermogenesis seems to be one of the handful of things that has had a decent effect for mood and energy. I've mostly done it in the form of cold showers but I have to say my ability to really go for it varies some. Some days 10 minutes is a piece of cake and others just 1 minute seems difficult. I'd like to get better at using it regularly and doing more ice baths as well. Probably should get a cold vest too.

Another thing that seems to help at least with brain fog and mood is just fasting. Being in a ketogenic state is not quite as good and maybe it's because of caffeine intake, but it is far better than eating a lot of carbs/sugar. Carbs seem to really set me off mood wise and make me feel weak and as if the world is falling apart. I mean I feel good eating them initially but give it 20 minutes to an hour and I'm anxious and feel utter hopelessness.

It's hard to pick what to do exactly as I seem variable in my responses and thus am at a loss of what will give me the most bang for my buck.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-06-07T20:14:09+0300

So youre smart..............OK........here ya go.
The less one eats, the lower protein turnover is, because the body tries to recycle its usable proteins when substrates are unavailable. _Protein turnover is a synonym for elevated ubiquitin marking._ Recall, that eukaryotes spend 80% of their total energy budget on protein synthesis. High ubiquitin rates, lower the redox potential by lowering the net negative charge in a cell faster than any other process in biology. Consider the mitochondrial consequences of this action now. Free radical leak is designed to work with DHA in cell membranes to optimize respiration while simultaneously eliminating dysfunctional mitochondria. The mitochondrial that leak the **MOST** free radicals will leak the most light because of a lack of DHA in the surrounding membranes. The more a mitochondria leaks, the more copies it is capable of making. This is why I made the prediction in Ubiquitination 6 that Mr. Moore would likely have no superoxide pulse from his mitochondria. He is incapable of making new mitochondria and likely new respiratory proteins in his defective mitochondria.

When mitochondria leak MORE free radicals the signal is to CORRECT the respiratory deficit by increasing the capacity. Capacity is the shear number of mitochondria. Capacity can also relate to the amount of respiratory proteins capable of being made from our 13 mitochondrial genes. The more mitochondria a tissue has the better it tends to function bio-energenically. The more mitochondria a cell has, generally the CLOSER mitochondria come to approaching the nucleus distance wise to nucleus to coadapted better. This relationship was spoken about in Ubiquitin 5.

The initial coaptation of maternal mitochondrial DNA to nuclear DNA happens when an egg is fertilized by a sperm. At conception if there is a mismatch between maternal mitochondrial DNA and the nuclear genome infertile or spontaneous abortions. In humans, 40% of pregnancy's undergo early acute miscarriage. These pregnancies have no chromosomal abnormalities so it points the etiology to a bio-energenic one. This is where leptin enters the equation. Leptin sensitivity or resistance is a good measure of a properer coadaptation of nuclear and mitochondrial DNA. In a zygote, if there is a mismatch evolution makes the early choice. It wont allow an offspring to go onto completion if it cannot hope to survive. That is a wasted of resources. But what happens if this same situation develops when the embryo is a child, teenager or adult. _That is called leptin resistance._ Humans have a very high aerobic capacity. This is why Sinclair's paper on pseudohypoxia is incredibly important for you to understand on your road to optimal. For a human to survive this situation they would need _to retain the ability to alter how electrons flow_ in their mitochondria to optimize function to their current environment. **When they do a poor job of this, low NAD+ and SIRT 1 ratio's are the result.**
